boltevoir_2010:
How to Pilot — Boltevoir (2010)

Format: HGSS-on 2010. This is Michael Pramawat's 2010 World Championship finalist deck (2nd, Masters) — he lost a very close final to Komatsuda's LuxChomp.

One-line identity

A Psychic control/toolbox built on Gardevoir: lock the opponent out of Poké-Powers with Psychic Lock while a rotating cast of attackers (Gallade, Machamp, Dusknoir) handles every specific threat, and Gardevoir LV.X cleans up KOs by teleporting in to finish damaged Pokémon.

The meta it lived in

2010 was an SP/Power-heavy format (LuxChomp, DialgaChomp, Gyarados, Jumpluff all lean on Poké-Powers or setup Powers). A deck that shuts Powers off and has a silver-bullet attacker for each archetype was a strong, skill-rewarding answer — good enough to reach the Worlds final.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Gardevoir (7/132) ×3 — the core. Psychic Lock: 60 damage and the opponent can't use Poké-Powers next turn — chokes SP setup, Claydol/Uxie draw, Bright Look, everything. Telepass (Poké-Power) lets you use a Supporter from the opponent's discard, giving huge consistency.
  • Gardevoir LV.X (131/132) — Teleportation Poké-Power swaps your Active for free; Bring Down KOs the most-damaged Pokémon in play. Combo: soften a target, then teleport Gardevoir LV.X in and Bring Down for the KO. (Careful — Bring Down can hit your own most-damaged Pokémon.)
  • Gallade (6/132) — the 1HKO button. Psychic Cut flips prizes and does 60 +20 per prize flipped (up to ~180) — enough to one-shot almost anything, and you see your prizes. Sonic Blade drops a target to 50-from-KO and forces a switch, setting up Bring Down.
  • Machamp (20/100) [via Rare Candy, 1-0-1] — anti-SP / anti-Basic tech. Takes down Basic Pokémon (including SP) cheaply; Hurricane Punch off a DCE can hit big. The 3 Rare Candy exist largely to jump straight to Machamp (and Gardevoir/Gallade).
  • Dusknoir (1/100) [Duskull evolves same turn] — Dark Palm Poké-Power shuffles a benched opposing Pokémon back into the deck (disruption vs. wide benches); Hard Feelings for scaling damage.

Support / tech (why the 1-ofs)

  • Spiritomb (32/99) ×3 — early-game shield/setup: hide behind it while you build, and (Keeper of the deck era) slow the opponent's Trainers. Great vs. faster decks.
  • Claydol (15/106) — Cosmic Power draw engine (note: your own Claydol Power is off under an opponent's lock too, so sequence carefully).
  • Azelf LV.X / Azelf / Uxie — Psychic support: Azelf LV.X negates weakness (key in mirror and vs. CurseGar), pixies dig/draw.
  • Judge ×1 / Looker's Investigation ×2 / Lucian's Assignment — hand disruption to pair with Psychic Lock: strip resources while they can't use Powers to recover.
  • DCE ×4 + Call Energy ×3 — DCE powers Psychic Lock / Psychic Cut / Machamp's Hurricane Punch; Call Energy grabs two Basics early (turn-1 setup).
  • Expert Belt ×2 — +20/+20, usually on Gallade or Dusknoir to push a Bring Down setup, or to make Psychic Lock hit harder.
  • Moonlight Stadium ×2 + Warp Point — Psychic switching/recovery and gust effects; Moonlight helps retreat your Psychic types.

Note (collection accuracy): Pramawat's own Worlds report says he ran the Stormfront Machop (retreat 1); the packaged WC deck printed the wrong Machop (retreat 2).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: hide behind Spiritomb, or Call Energy to fetch two Basics (Ralts
  • support). Begin building Ralts → (Rare Candy) → Gardevoir/Gallade.
  • Early turns: get the first Psychic Lock online ASAP — every turn they can't use Powers is a turn they fall behind against your toolbox.

The core loop

Psychic Lock every turn for 60 + Power denial; when a specific threat needs removing, switch to the right tool — Gallade to 1HKO a big attacker, Machamp vs. Basics/SP, Dusknoir to shuffle away a key benched Pokémon — then use Sonic Blade + Teleport + Bring Down to convert damaged targets into clean KOs. Layer Judge/Looker so they can't rebuild while locked.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Establish the lock early, then keep it up — your disruption Supporters are far stronger while their Powers are off.
  • Watch your own most-damaged Pokémon before using Bring Down (it can KO yours).
  • Pick the right attacker for the matchup rather than forcing Gardevoir into everything — the toolbox is the point.
  • Manage bench size vs. their damage (e.g. vs. Jumpluff, a wide bench feeds their Mass Attack; Dusknoir can trim benches).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Speed — SP decks (LuxChomp) are faster and can go up on prizes; you win by missing their attack turns via lock + disruption, not by racing.
  • Cards that ignore the lock's value (fast Basic aggro that doesn't need Powers) can pressure you before the lock matters.
  • Your own reliance on Powers (Telepass/Claydol) means opposing Psychic Lock / Power denial hurts you too.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Psychic Lock: 60 damage + no opponent Poké-Powers next turn.
  • Psychic Cut (Gallade): 60 +20 per prize flipped, up to ~180 (1HKO range).
  • Sonic Blade: drop target to 50 HP from KO + force switch → Bring Down finish.
  • Setup: Stage 2 attackers via Rare Candy (Ralts base, skip Kirlia).

Sources: Bulbapedia (Boltevoir, Gardevoir/Gallade), SixPrizes 2010 Worlds Boltevoir analysis and "Purple Reign" Gardevoir guide. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
gyarados_2010:
How to Pilot — Gyarados (2010)

Format: HGSS-on 2010. A non-SP "rogue" that punched into the SP-dominated meta by attacking for zero Energy.

One-line identity

Discard your own Magikarp, then swing with Gyarados for free: Tail Revenge does 50 damage per Magikarp in the discard, so a full discard = a huge, energy-less hit on a Stage 1. Cheap, fast, and full of tech room because you're not paying for Energy.

The meta it lived in

The format leaned hard on low-HP Basics and Pokémon SP (LuxChomp everywhere). A deck that hits 110+ for zero Energy on turn 2, on a bulky Stage 1, was a genuine problem for that field — and Expert Belt pushed it to 150 HP / higher damage. Gyarados had already top-cut 2009 Worlds and stayed strong into 2010–2011.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Gyarados (19/100) — main attacker. Tail Revenge: 50 × number of Magikarp in your discard pile, for no Energy. Three Magikarp down = 150 (or the classic ~110 with Belt math per era discussion).
  • Magikarp (65/100) ×4 — you want these in the discard, not in play. They're ammo.
  • Sableye (48/100) ×4 — the setup enabler. Impersonate copies a Supporter from hand as your first-turn play — usually Pokémon Collector turn 1.
  • Felicity's Drawing (98/106) ×4 — draw that discards to draw more — the primary way to dump Magikarp into the discard on turn 2.
  • Broken Time-Space (104/127) ×4 — Stadium that lets you evolve the turn a Pokémon is played/comes down — Magikarp → Gyarados with no wait, enabling turn-2 (or faster) swings.
  • Combee (79/130) — bench-filler / setup (Honey Gather-style draw) that helps you churn to your pieces.

The tech suite (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Luxray GL + Luxray GL LV.X (1-1) — after a Gyarados trades, promote Luxray, use Bright Look to drag up a specific threat and KO it. Adds reach the pure Gyarados shell lacks.
  • Uxie / Azelf / Mesprit (LA pixies) — Uxie draw; Azelf digs a prized Magikarp back (critical — a prized Magikarp is lost damage); Mesprit for a turn of Power denial.
  • Crobat G ×2 + Poké Turn ×4 — free Flash Bite damage counters to reach KO numbers, recycled with Poké Turn.
  • Pokémon Rescue ×4 / Super Scoop Up ×4 — recover Gyarados/Magikarp; SSU also picks up a damaged Gyarados to reset it.
  • Cyclone Energy / Warp Energy — attached "switch" effects: Cyclone forces the opponent to switch (drag up a target), Warp lets you retreat free.
  • Expert Belt ×2 — +20 damage / +20 HP; turns Gyarados into a 150-HP threat.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: bench Magikarp + Sableye; Impersonate → Pokémon Collector to load Basics (grab Magikarps, a pixie, Combee).
  • Turn 2: Felicity's Drawing to discard 2 Magikarp (with Broken Time-Space out), evolve a Magikarp into Gyarados, and Tail Revenge for a big energy-free hit.

The core loop

Keep Magikarp count in the discard high (3 = 150 damage), attack for free every turn, and use Luxray GL LV.X / Cyclone Energy / Crobat G to make sure the damage lands where it wins prizes. Rescue/SSU keep the Gyarados coming when they trade.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Discard is your Energy. Protect the count — don't let all your Magikarp get stuck in hand or prizes (Azelf fixes prizes).
  • Free retreat + Cyclone/Warp = you dictate the Active matchup. Use it to dodge or to pull up a target.
  • Save Luxray Bright Look for the moment it steals a clean prize, not turn 2 filler.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Lightning weakness — Luxray GL LV.X (in opposing SP decks) hits Gyarados for weakness; be careful trading into their Luxray.
  • Prized / hand-clogged Magikarp — a slow discard start means weak Tail Revenge; mulligan-y hands hurt.
  • Judge / hand disruption at the wrong moment can stall the Felicity's engine.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Tail Revenge: 50 × Magikarp in discard, 0 Energy. 3 Magikarp = 150.
  • Expert Belt: +20 damage / +20 HP (Gyarados → 150 HP).
  • Turn-2 attacker is the plan (Broken Time-Space + Felicity's Drawing).
  • Energy is all utility (Cyclone/Warp) — no Energy needed to attack.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Gyarados/AMU era), Retro Library & PTCG Archive 2010 deck guides, SixPrizes format coverage. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
jumpluff_2010:
How to Pilot — Jumpluff (2010)

Format: HGSS-on 2010. Based on the Junior-division World Championship-winning list, Yuka Furusawa's "Power Cottonweed."

One-line identity

A fast Grass swarm: Jumpluff attacks for a single Grass Energy and scales its damage off your board, so you flood the bench, evolve quickly, and hit for big numbers as early as turn 1–2 — then just keep replacing Jumpluffs as they trade.

The meta it lived in

A field of low-HP Basics and Pokémon SP was perfect prey for a cheap, high-damage swarm. Jumpluff dominated the younger divisions (it won Juniors at 2010 Worlds) and stayed relevant up top thanks to its low attack cost leaving room for techs. It later fused with Luxray into "LuxPluff."

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Jumpluff (6/123) ×4 — the whole plan. Mass Attack: for one Grass Energy, does 10 damage per Basic + Evolution Pokémon in play (scaling up to ~120, more with help). Leaf Guard reduces damage taken by 30 next turn — when you can't KO, use this to make Jumpluff obnoxious to remove.
  • Hoppip / Skiploom — the evolution line; run heavy to keep swarming.
  • Broken Time-Space (104/127) ×3 — evolve the turn you play/bench, so Hoppip → Skiploom → Jumpluff can happen fast (turn-1 attacks are possible).
  • Rare Candy ×3 — skip Skiploom to hit Jumpluff a stage faster.
  • Claydol (15/106) ×3 — Cosmic Power: draw/refresh engine; critical for getting multiple Jumpluff out and recovering them after KOs.
  • Unown R (77/146) ×4 — extra "R" bodies count toward Mass Attack and add a little draw — a Power Cottonweed signature that boosts your damage ceiling.
  • Uxie (43/146) ×2 — Set Up draw to power the fast start.

Support / tech

  • Pokémon Collector ×4 / Pokémon Communication ×4 / Bebe's Search / Luxury Ball — huge search package to find bodies and evolutions consistently.
  • Judge ×2 — hand disruption; cut the opponent's resources once you're ahead so they can't stabilize.
  • PlusPower ×3 — nudge damage over the line for key KOs.
  • Warp Point ×2 — switch/gust effect to bring up a target or escape; also cleans up the last prizes (a 3rd copy was considered a real upgrade).
  • Expert Belt ×1 — +20/+20 on a key Jumpluff.
  • Night Maintenance ×2 — recycle KO'd Pokémon back into the deck to keep swarming.
  • Crobat G ×1 — free damage counters to reach numbers.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Collector for Hoppip(s) + support; bench wide. With Broken Time-Space and/or Rare Candy you can even evolve into a Jumpluff and start attacking turn 1.
  • Turn 2: Claydol online for draw, multiple Jumpluff set up, Mass Attack for a meaningful hit that scales with your (now full) bench.

The core loop

Keep the bench full (more bodies = more Mass Attack damage), attack every turn for one Grass, and use Leaf Guard on turns you can't KO to survive the return hit. When a Jumpluff trades, Claydol/Night Maintenance replace it immediately. Add reach with the Luxray GL LV.X line if your build runs it (Bright Look to pull + KO a benched target).

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Bench width = damage. Don't under-commit bodies; a fuller board is a bigger hit.
  • Leaf Guard when you can't KO — a −30 Jumpluff is very hard to remove profitably.
  • Use Judge when you're set up and they aren't — it's a tempo weapon, not a panic button.
  • Watch for whiffing the single Grass attachment — it stalls a whole turn of attacks.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Gardevoir / PLOX (Psychic Lock) — shuts off your Poké-Powers (Claydol/Uxie), choking your draw and your ability to keep swarming. A real problem matchup.
  • Fire / Rain of Fire spreaders — Grass is Fire-weak; spread damage can wipe Hoppip/Skiploom/Jumpluff lines.
  • Anything that answers a single Power (Mesprit/Power Spray) can slow your setup.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Mass Attack: 10 × (Basics + Evolutions in play) for 1 Grass Energy (~120 base, higher with Unown R / PlusPower / Belt).
  • Leaf Guard: −30 damage taken next turn.
  • Fastest attack: turn 1 possible via Broken Time-Space / Rare Candy.
  • Recovery: Claydol + Night Maintenance keep the swarm going.

Sources: Bulbapedia (LuxPluff, Power Cottonweed, Jumpluff card), Retro Library & PTCG Archive 2010 deck guides. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
luxchomp_2010:
How to Pilot — LuxChomp (2010)

Format: HGSS-on / DP-on 2010 (SP-dominated). This build is essentially Yuta Komatsuda's 2010 World Championship-winning list — the DialgaChomp variant, running Dialga G + Dragonite FB + Professor Oak's New Theory.

One-line identity

The defining deck of the format: a Pokémon SP toolbox that takes six prizes in six turns by using free switching (Bright Look) and free-target sniping (Dragon Rush) to pick off the opponent's weakest, most valuable targets — you don't out-damage, you out-manoeuvre.

The meta it lived in

SP decks had ruled for over a year; the HGSS reprint of Double Colorless Energy pushed Garchomp C LV.X over the top and made LuxChomp the deck to beat. It won more sanctioned events in 2009–2010 than anything else and took Masters + Seniors at 2010 Worlds. If you were building any deck that year, it needed a LuxChomp answer — it was the undisputed best deck in the format (BDIF).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Luxray GL LV.X (109/111) — Poké-Power Bright Look: drag any benched Pokémon into the Active spot (like a free Catcher). This is how you pull up a helpless support Basic and KO it. Flash Impact also makes it a real attacker in a pinch.
  • Garchomp C LV.X (145/147) — Dragon Rush: 80 to any Pokémon, bench or active, no weakness/resistance dodging you. Snipes their setup Pokémon (Uxie, Azelf) or finishes a target. Powered by DCE + Energy Gain.
  • Uxie (43/146) — Set Up: draw until you have 7. The deck burns its hand fast, so Uxie is your refill. Searchable off Pokémon Collector.
  • Cyrus's Conspiracy (105/127) ×4 — the glue: search a Supporter + a Team Galactic Invention + a basic Energy in one card. The most important card in the deck.
  • Dialga G / Dialga G LV.X — the "Dialga" in DialgaChomp: its LV.X Poké-Body (Deafen-style) shuts off opposing Trainers/Poké-Bodies, a tech vs. other SP and lock decks.
  • Dragonite FB (56/147) — tech vs. the SP mirror: Mach Blow does 80 to a Defending Pokémon SP with no chance to respond — enough to KO most SP attackers.

The Team Galactic's Invention (TGI) toolbox — the deck's "tricks"

  • G-105 Poké Turn ×4 — pick up your own SP Pokémon (+ everything on it) back to hand. Heals damage, re-uses Bright Look (Luxray only triggers Bright Look when it comes into play), and dodges KOs.
  • G-101 Energy Gain ×4 — SP Pokémon attacks cost 1 less. Turns DCE into a full Dragon Rush.
  • G-103 Power Spray ×3 — free counter: negate an opponent's Poké-Power as they use it. Save these for high-value Powers (opposing setup, Regigigas, etc.).
  • G-109 SP Radar ×3 — search any SP Pokémon.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: get a Basic + draw engine going. Pokémon Collector for Uxie(s) + your SP Basics; Uxie's Set Up to load the hand. Cyrus's Conspiracy to assemble the next play.
  • Going second, you can attack immediately: with Energy Gain down, Garchomp C can chip ~50 with DCE, and donks are possible (Garchomp C / Luxray GL / a DCE'd attacker can OHKO a lone Basic on the first turn).
  • "Set up" is achieved when you have: a Garchomp C LV.X with Energy Gain + DCE ready for Dragon Rush, a Luxray GL on the bench ready to level up, and Poké Turns in hand to recycle.

The core loop

Bright Look their weak/benched target into the Active spot → KO it (or Dragon Rush snipe it directly) → Poké Turn to reset Luxray / heal / dodge → repeat. Play reactively: answer whatever they just threatened, then snowball the prize lead.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Deal with the opponent's Garchomp C LV.X first in SP mirrors — it's the biggest reciprocal threat.
  • Don't drop high-retreat Pokémon without a Poké Turn available (you can get stranded Active / lured up).
  • Spend Power Spray only on Powers that matter; don't waste it on the first Uxie.
  • Prizes-in-6 mindset: every turn should take or set up a prize, not just trade.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Donphan (Prime/other) — Fighting resistance + damage-reduction Body + cheap big hits. Luxray is nearly useless into it; you grind with Crobat G poison + Dragon Rush.
  • Disruption/lock decks (Sablock, Vileplume variants) — Judge + resource denial can cut you off before you assemble attacks; lean on consistency and Dialga to break locks.
  • Anything that OHKOs your LV.X attackers before you can Poké Turn them.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Dragon Rush: 80 to any Pokémon (bench or active).
  • Bright Look: free switch of an opponent's Pokémon (once per entry into play → recycle with Poké Turn).
  • Win plan: 6 prizes ASAP, off weak targets, not a damage race.
  • Energy: 4 DCE do most of the lifting; Lightning/Metal support Luxray/Dialga.

Sources: Bulbapedia (LuxChomp, LuxChomp of the Spirit), Limitless (Komatsuda 2010 Worlds list), SixPrizes ("The Bible on LuxChomp"), Alex Fields' LuxChomp strategy article, Retro Library. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
cmt_2012:
How to Pilot — CMT (2012)

Format: HGSS-on 2012. "CMT" = Celebi Prime / Mewtwo-EX / Tornadus. A top-tier speed deck of the format (multiple Regional/Championship results).

One-line identity

Pure speed and consistency: Celebi Prime accelerates Grass Energy by hand, so you power a hard-hitting Mewtwo-EX (X-Ball) or a Tornadus in a single turn — often hitting for 60–100 as early as turn 1. No gimmicks, just fast, flexible beatdown with a deep tech pool.

The meta it lived in

An extremely fast Basic-EX format. CMT thrived because it could donk, win the all-important Mewtwo war, and tech for everything (Terrakion, Shaymin-EX, Regigigas). It repeatedly put multiple copies into Regional top cuts and won the European Challenge Cup; it was a defining "big three" deck alongside Darkrai and Eelektrik.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Celebi Prime (92/102) ×3 — the engine. Forest Breath: attach a Grass Energy from hand to one of your Pokémon each turn (on top of your normal attachment). With Skyarrow Bridge giving it free retreat, you can Forest Breath multiple times a turn and pour Energy onto Mewtwo.
  • Mewtwo-EX (54/99) ×3 — X-Ball scales with total Energy on both Mewtwos; attacks for Colorless, so Grass fuels it with no drawback. The deck's main attacker and the reason it wins the Mewtwo mirror (more Energy = you 1HKO first).
  • Tornadus-EX (90/108) ×1 — Power Blast: 100 (discards an Energy); a heavy hitter that, unlike Mewtwo, is hard to 1HKO and near-impossible to Catcher-lock (retreat 1 under Skyarrow). Fighting-resistant.
  • Smeargle (8/90) ×2 — starter; Portrait borrows the opponent's Supporter, free-retreat under Skyarrow.
  • Terrakion (73/101) ×2 — Fighting tech / revenge attacker; can be loaded fast with Celebi + Energy Retrieval and swings the mirror.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Skyarrow Bridge (91/99) ×2 — Stadium that gives Basics with retreat 1 (Celebi, Tornadus, Smeargle) free retreat. Central to the whole engine — it's what lets you loop Forest Breath.
  • Switch ×3 — "frosting" on Skyarrow: play Switch and still retreat the same turn, letting you Forest Breath then swap back into your attacker (huge in the Tornadus war).
  • Dual Ball ×4 / Random Receiver ×3 / PONT ×4 / Juniper ×3 / N ×1 — the speed and consistency package; you want to open Celebi or Tornadus almost every game.
  • Junk Arm ×4 / Pokémon Catcher ×3 / PlusPower ×2 / Eviolite ×2 — reach and tanking; PlusPower pushes X-Ball over KO lines, Eviolite makes your attackers survive a hit.
  • Energy Retrieval / Energy Search / Revive ×1 each — utility: recur Energy for Terrakion, fix Energy, get back a prized/KO'd Mewtwo.
  • 6 Grass / 3 Fighting / 4 DCE — Grass for Forest Breath + X-Ball fuel, Fighting for Terrakion, DCE for fast Mewtwo/Tornadus.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: open Celebi (best) or Tornadus. Forest Breath a Grass onto Mewtwo or Tornadus, use PONT/Juniper to dig for DCE + Skyarrow Bridge, and if it comes together, hit for 60–100 immediately (Hurricane / early X-Ball).
  • Setup achieved = Celebi(s) online with Skyarrow down, giving you two Energy attachments a turn to load a Mewtwo.

The core loop

Every turn: Forest Breath (+ manual attach, + Switch tricks) to keep Energy flowing onto the active attacker, then X-Ball / Power Blast for a KO. In the Mewtwo war, get more Energy than they do so you win the exchange; in the Tornadus war, use Eviolite + Switch to trade favorably. Bring up Celebi/Smeargle after a KO (free retreat) so you never waste a turn.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Win the Mewtwo war — it's the deck's whole reason to exist; don't bench a Mewtwo you aren't ready to attack with (it's Catcher/Mewtwo bait).
  • After a KO, promote Celebi or Smeargle if Skyarrow is down — even if you don't plan to use them, it keeps your attackers safe.
  • Watch Celebi's 60 HP — a lone Celebi start can be donked; don't over-expose it.
  • Use PlusPower to force whiffs — making the opponent extend for an extra Energy is a real edge.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Six Corners / big Dragon decks (Outrage) — Tornadus is useless into them and Mewtwo gets answered by their own Mewtwo + DCE; a genuinely hard matchup.
  • Lightning decks — Tornadus is Lightning-Weak (Zekrom/Eelektrik punish it).
  • Item lock / early disruption (Vileplume, Zebstrika Disconnect) can cut off the Item-heavy engine.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Forest Breath: +1 Grass attachment per turn (stacks with your normal attach).
  • X-Ball: ×20 per Energy on both Mewtwos combined.
  • Power Blast (Tornadus-EX): 100, discard 1 Energy.
  • Turn-1 damage of 60–100 is realistic and often the plan.

Sources: SixPrizes ("Complete Guide to CMT", States/Regionals reports, "From 9-0 to Shucks"), PokéBeach Celebi Variants, The Deck Out. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
darkrai_mewtwo_terrakion_2012:
How to Pilot — Darkrai/Mewtwo/Terrakion (2012)

Format: HGSS-on 2012 (BLW–DRX, "Big Basics" era). This build closely mirrors Igor Costa's 2012 World Championship-winning list.

One-line identity

The premier "Big Basics" toolbox: a wall of powerful Basic EXs where Darkrai-EX gives the whole team free retreat and Night Spear snipes the bench, while Mewtwo-EX and Terrakion are matchup-specific hammers. You pick the right attacker for what's across the table and grind out prizes with superior mobility.

The meta it lived in

2012 was defined by fast, hard-hitting Basics — Mewtwo-EX was in nearly every deck, and Darkrai-EX became just as ubiquitous after Dark Explorers. Because Terrakion was being teched everywhere to punish Darkrai, this deck answered its own weakness by running Mewtwo and Terrakion itself. It won Worlds and multiple National Championships; it was the deck to know.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Darkrai-EX (63/108) ×3 — the core. Ability Dark Cloak: every Pokémon with a Darkness Energy has no retreat cost, making the whole clunky team mobile. Night Spear: 90 to the Active + 30 to a benched Pokémon — the snipe wins prize races by softening two targets at once.
  • Mewtwo-EX (54/99) ×3 — X-Ball scales with total Energy on both Mewtwos; the format's best attacker and your answer to opposing Mewtwo and to Fighting decks.
  • Terrakion (73/101) ×2 — Fighting tech: Retaliate hits big the turn after a KO, and it 1HKOs opposing Darkrai (Fighting Weakness). The anti-meta card.
  • Dark Patch (93/108) ×4 — accelerator: attach a Darkness from the discard to a benched Dark Pokémon. With Shaymin, you can fully load a Darkrai in one turn.
  • Shaymin (8/95) ×2 — Celebratory Wind-style energy movement / support to concentrate Energy where you need it fast (helps the one-turn setups).
  • Smeargle (8/90) ×2 — starter: Portrait uses a Supporter from the opponent's hand; free-retreat under Dark Cloak so it's not a liability.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Junk Arm ×4 — reuse a spent Item (Catcher, Dark Patch, PlusPower) from the discard by pitching two cards; huge consistency + late-game reach.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×3 — drag up a target to Night Spear or to remove a support Basic. Often the prize-winning play.
  • Eviolite ×3 — −20 from attacks on the holder; makes Darkrai a 180-HP wall and forces opponents to find PlusPower to hit KO numbers (esp. vs. Terrakion).
  • Random Receiver ×2 / PONT ×4 / Juniper ×3 / N ×2 — the draw/consistency spine. N doubles as disruption when you're ahead.
  • Super Scoop Up ×2 — reuse Smeargle's Portrait, or pick up a damaged Darkrai to reset it (with Shaymin, replay it fresh).
  • Prism ×2 / DCE ×3 / 7 Darkness / 2 Fighting — Prism enables Terrakion's colored costs; DCE fuels Mewtwo; Darkness feeds Dark Patch and Night Spear.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: ideally start Smeargle (free retreat under Darkrai), Portrait for a Supporter, and set up a Darkrai on the bench. Discard Darkness (Juniper/Ultra Ball) so Dark Patch is live.
  • Turn 2: Dark Patch + attachment (+ Shaymin) to fully load a Darkrai, then Night Spear — 90 to the Active and 30 onto their most important benched Basic.

The core loop

Night Spear every turn, placing the 30 snipe to set up future KOs; use free retreat (Dark Cloak) to rotate the right attacker in — Mewtwo into the Mewtwo war, Terrakion into Darkrai/Landorus, Darkrai into everything else. Catcher + snipe to pick off support Basics. Junk Arm keeps your Catchers/Patches/PlusPowers flowing.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Know your prized Darkrai count on your first search — prizing multiple is scary; three copies is the number that survives it.
  • Match the attacker to the matchup rather than defaulting to Darkrai.
  • Eviolite on the wall early forces the opponent to over-invest to KO you.
  • Place the Night Spear snipe deliberately — it's how you take a 6th prize a turn early.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Pure Fighting decks (Landorus/Terrakion, Fighting boxes) — Darkrai is Fighting-Weak; even Mewtwo doesn't fully cover it.
  • Blastoise/Keldeo-EX — Keldeo 1HKOs Darkrai while Darkrai can't 1HKO Keldeo, and Squirtle BCR blunts the Night Spear snipe, losing you the prize race.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Dark Cloak (your free retreat) and Dark Patch; Tool Scrapper helps but this list is light on it.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Night Spear: 90 active + 30 bench snipe (no Weakness on the bench damage).
  • Dark Cloak: free retreat for all your Darkness-carrying Pokémon.
  • X-Ball: scales with total Energy on both Mewtwos (×20 per Energy).
  • Setup: turn-2 Night Spear is the goal, via Dark Patch + Shaymin.

Sources: Limitless (Igor Costa, 1st 2012 Worlds), SixPrizes (Darkrai analyses, Nationals reports), The Deck Out, PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
blastoise_2013:
How to Pilot — Blastoise (2013)

Format: NXD-on / BW-era (2013 Worlds format). This exact 60 is essentially James Good's 3rd-place 2013 World Championship list.

One-line identity

A Stage-2 "toolbox" deck: get Blastoise out fast, use its Deluge Ability to dump a pile of Water Energy onto an attacker in a single turn, and take knockouts with Keldeo-EX (scaling damage) or Black Kyurem-EX (flat 200).

The cards that matter (your engine)

  • Blastoise (31/149) — Ability Deluge: attach as many Water Energy as you like from your hand each turn. This is the whole deck. It's a Stage 2, so you run 4 and lean on Rare Candy to skip Wartortle.
  • Squirtle (29/149) — the base you want on the bench turn 1.
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) — Secret Sword scales with Energy attached; ~7 Energy 1HKOs most Basic EXs. Rush In (Ability) lets it swap into the Active spot for free — your pivot/retreat button.
  • Black Kyurem-EX (95/135) — Black Ballista: 200 damage for WWLC, discarding 3 Energy on use. 1HKOs virtually every EX in the format, but is totally reliant on Blastoise to re-fuel.
  • Water Energy ×10 / Lightning Energy ×2 — Lightning exists purely to pay the L in Black Ballista's cost.

Support you'll lean on

  • Rare Candy ×4 + Ultra Ball ×4 — the Squirtle → Blastoise shortcut.
  • Tropical Beach ×4 — Stadium; on a turn you can't do much, draw up to 7. This is the consistency backbone for setup turns.
  • Skyla ×4 — search any Trainer; grab the Rare Candy, Ultra Ball, or Catcher you need to lock in a turn-2 Blastoise.
  • Superior Energy Retrieval ×4 — return 4 basic Energy from discard to hand; this is what keeps Black Ballista firing every turn.
  • N ×4 / Professor Juniper ×3 / Colress ×2 — draw. N doubles as disruption; the deck is on the slower side, so N against faster decks is strong.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×3 — drag up a weak/benched target for a knockout. Often the literal wins-the-game card.
  • Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 / Heavy Ball ×1 / Level Ball ×1 — extra search.

The game plan (turn by turn)

Turn 1 (ideal): - Bench a Squirtle (and ideally a second). Play Tropical Beach to draw 7 if you have nothing better. Aim to end the turn with the pieces for Blastoise next turn (Rare Candy + Blastoise, or an Ultra Ball / Skyla to find them).

Turn 2 (the key turn): - Rare Candy the Squirtle straight into Blastoise. Now Deluge is online. - Load your attacker: dump Water Energy from hand onto Keldeo-EX (for Secret Sword) or a Black Kyurem-EX (for Black Ballista). - Start attacking. Black Ballista only needs ~4 Energy to 1HKO an EX, which is why it's the faster kill; Keldeo wants ~7 for the same.

Turn 3 onward (the loop): - Attack → Superior Energy Retrieval to pull the discarded Energy back → Deluge it onto the next attacker → attack again. That's the rhythm. - Use Keldeo's Rush In to pivot a damaged/exhausted attacker to the bench and promote a fresh one without paying retreat. - Use Pokémon Catcher to pick off a benched EX (or a low-HP retreater) for a clean prize when you can't 1HKO the Active.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Two Blastoise on board is the goal, not one. If your only Blastoise gets KO'd, the energy engine dies — that's how you lose. Set up a backup.
  • Blastoise treats Energy as expendable. Seeing a Water in your opening hand with an Ultra Ball? Discard it — you'll get it back with SER. Setup > hoarding.
  • Black Kyurem for EXs, Keldeo for the rest (and for non-EX attacking, since Keldeo/Blastoise itself doesn't give up two prizes as easily as the EXs do).
  • Don't be shy with N when you're behind or when the opponent is comboing off.

Weaknesses to respect

  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) shuts off Abilities — including Deluge. If they land it with a Tool attached, your whole engine stops. (Historically the reason lists carried Tool Scrapper; this build doesn't, so play around it: race, or force them to remove the Tool.)
  • Slow starts / lone Blastoise. Prize a Blastoise or whiff the turn-2 evolution and you can fall badly behind.
  • Weakness: Black Kyurem-EX is Dragon-Weak; opposing Dragons can 1HKO it back.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Blastoise setup target: turn 2 (via Rare Candy).
  • Black Ballista: 200 dmg, cost WWLC, discard 3 Energy.
  • Secret Sword: ~7 Energy to 1HKO a Basic EX.
  • Prize math: you have EX attackers (worth 2 prizes to the opponent), so avoid handing over easy multi-prize KOs — pivot with Rush In.

Sources consulted: Bulbapedia (Blastoise/Keldeo TCG), Limitless (James Good, 3rd 2013 Worlds), SixPrizes deck analyses, Retro/Professor's Research format guides. Card counts verified against the collection workbook (60 cards).
darkrai_2013:
How to Pilot — Darkrai (2013)

Format: BW-on 2013 (Plasma Storm era). A "Speed Darkrai" / Dark Cloak build with the Hypnotoxic Laser + Virbank City Gym damage engine. This build mirrors Jason Klaczynski's 2013 World Championship-winning list — his record third Worlds title.

One-line identity

The consistency king of the format: Darkrai-EX with Dark Cloak free retreat and Night Spear snipe, supercharged by Hypnotoxic Laser + Virbank City Gym so your attacks hit KO numbers a full turn early. Fast, flexible, and hard to disrupt — built almost entirely from staples.

The meta it lived in

2013's format rewarded decks that could 1HKO EXs (RayEels, Black Kyurem/Blastoise) or grind efficiently (Darkrai). Darkrai's edge was LaserBank: a Night Spear + Dark Claw + Laser + Virbank does 140, enough to 1HKO a Blastoise; add the 30 snipe and it 1HKOs Keldeo-EX, Mewtwo-EX, or Tornadus-EX. It's the deck other decks had to beat — widely considered the best deck in the format (BDIF), and Jason Klaczynski piloted it to the 2013 World Championship (his third Worlds title, a record at the time).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Darkrai-EX (63/108) ×4 — the core. Dark Cloak: all your Darkness-carrying Pokémon retreat free. Night Spear: 90 Active + 30 bench snipe. You run four so a prized copy never stops the plan and you always have a backup vs. surprise Dragon Bursts.
  • Sableye (62/108) ×3 — the setup MVP. Junk Hunt: return two Item cards from discard to hand. Free-retreat under Dark Cloak, it recurs Lasers, Catchers, Energy Switch — a near-infinite Item engine that also gives donk potential (Confuse Ray + Dark Claw + Laser can KO a small Basic).
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) ×2 — Rush In free pivot to escape Status/Catcher; a Water attacker when needed. Note: it's the same card that answers opposing status locks.
  • Hypnotoxic Laser ×4 + Virbank City Gym ×2 — the damage engine: poison (20 between turns with Virbank) + a sleep flip. Pushes Night Spear to lethal numbers.
  • Dark Claw ×2 — +20 to Darkrai's attacks; part of the 140-damage LaserBank math.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Dark Patch ×4 — accelerate Darkness from the discard onto benched Darkrai; the reason you can 1HKO from a standing start.
  • Energy Switch ×3 — move Darkness around (e.g. borrow from a free-retreating Sableye) to hit the Turn-2 Night Spear.
  • Professor Juniper ×4 / N ×4 / Random Receiver ×3 / Bicycle ×2 — the draw spine; Juniper discards Darkness to feed Dark Patch, N disrupts.
  • Ultra Ball ×3 / Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 — search + more Darkness discard.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×4 — drag targets for Night Spear / to remove support Basics.
  • Enhanced Hammer ×2 — strip Special Energy (great vs. Plasma's Blend/Plasma/Prism and vs. DCE decks).
  • Mr. Mime (47/116) ×1 — Bench Barrier: blocks bench snipe damage, protecting your own board from mirror Night Spears.
  • Tool Scrapper ×1 — remove opposing Tools (or Garbotoxin's).
  • 10 Darkness — the fuel for Dark Patch + Night Spear.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: ideally start Sableye; Junk Hunt to bank Items and set the engine. Bench a Darkrai, discard Darkness (Juniper/Ultra Ball) to arm Dark Patch.
  • Turn 2: Dark Patch + attach (often via Energy Switch off Sableye) to reach a Night Spear, placing the 30 snipe where it sets up a future KO.

The core loop

End every turn with a Night Spear (90 + 30 snipe). Layer Laser + Virbank + Dark Claw to hit exact 1HKO numbers on the format's EXs. Use Dark Cloak free retreat to rotate attackers, Sableye Junk Hunt to recur Lasers/Catchers indefinitely, and Enhanced Hammer to strip the special Energy that fast decks depend on.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Keep two Darkrai ready so a surprise 1HKO (Dragon Burst, etc.) doesn't end your offense.
  • Sableye is your engine — protect it and abuse Junk Hunt; late game it retrieves the exact Item you need.
  • Do the LaserBank math before attacking: know whether Claw+Laser+Virbank gets you to the KO this turn or next.
  • Consider Switch for the mirror and Garbodor (retains Energy, clears Status).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting decks (Landorus-EX) — Darkrai is Fighting-Weak; Landorus pressures you from turn 1. Lasers help you race, but it's tough.
  • Blastoise/Keldeo-EX — Squirtle BCR blunts the snipe and Keldeo out-tanks; you need LaserBank to keep pace.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Dark Cloak (free retreat) and Dark Patch; lean on Tool Scrapper and Switch.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Night Spear: 90 active + 30 bench snipe.
  • LaserBank max: Night Spear + Dark Claw + Laser + Virbank = 140 (+30 snipe → 1HKOs Keldeo/Mewtwo/Tornadus).
  • Poison w/ Virbank: 20 between turns.
  • Setup: turn-2 Night Spear; Energy is 10 Darkness + Dark Patch.

Sources: SixPrizes ("The Different Types of Dark Cloak", 2013 Darkrai analyses), Retro Pokémon TCG BW decks, PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
gothitelle_2013:
How to Pilot — Gothitelle (2013)

Format: HGSS/BW-on 2013 (Boundaries Crossed–Plasma era). This build closely mirrors Edmund Kuras's US Nationals 2013-winning Accelgor/Gothitelle list.

One-line identity

A lock deck: Gothitelle stops the opponent from playing Item cards while Accelgor (copied endlessly by Mew-EX) paralyzes and poisons their Active every turn. Set it up and the opponent can't retreat, can't use Items, and can't attack — you win at your leisure while chipping them down.

The meta it lived in

2013 was full of Item-reliant setup decks (Blastoise, RayEels, Plasma, Darkrai), all of which fold if their Items get shut off early. Gothitelle exploited exactly that. It was a Nationals-winning, Worlds-relevant deck — feared enough that some players teched specifically against it. Its kryptonite was Keldeo-EX's Rush In (the one clean out to paralysis + item lock).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Gothitelle (47/98) ×3 (3-1-3 line w/ Gothita ×4, Gothorita ×1) — Ability Magic Room: while it's Active, neither player can play Item cards. This is the lock. It has to sit Active and take hits, so you protect it with Float Stone (free retreat) and keep spares.
  • Accelgor (11/108) ×2 (from Shelmet ×3) — Deck and Cover: 10 damage, paralyze
  • poison, then shuffle Accelgor (and itself) back into the deck. Paralysis every turn = they never attack.
  • Mew-EX (46/124) ×2 — Versatile: copies Accelgor's Deck and Cover, then Mew shuffles itself back in (better than shuffling Accelgor, since Mew is a Basic and Accelgor is an evolution you'd have to rebuild). This is the loop's heart.
  • Dusknoir (63/149) (1-0-1 via Rare Candy) — Sinister Hand: move damage counters. Pull damage off the opponent's Active so it can't die/leave (staying locked), and pile it onto benched Pokémon for KOs. Enables the "un-loseable" lock.
  • Musharna (59/99) (1-1) — Forewarn-style draw to keep the DCE + Mew loop fed.
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) — utility retreat / a Water attacker vs. mirror-ish spots.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Rare Candy ×4 — jump Gothita → Gothitelle (and Duskull → Dusknoir) a turn faster; hitting T2–3 Gothitelle is the whole game.
  • Skyla ×4 / Ultra Ball ×3 / Level Ball ×2 / Pokémon Communication ×2 — heavy search to assemble the multi-piece lock (2 Stage-2s + Stage-1s).
  • Float Stone ×3 — free retreat for Gothitelle so you can send Mew out to attack and pull Goth back Active each turn.
  • Tropical Beach ×2 — Stadium; draw 7 on setup turns you can't do much else.
  • N ×3 / Juniper ×4 / Colress ×2 — draw; N also resets the opponent to a small hand while they're locked (with Dusknoir keeping the loop, you can N them repeatedly).
  • Tool Scrapper ×1 — strip a Float Stone/Dark Claw (or shut off Garbodor's Tool); after a Goth is up they can't replay Tools anyway.
  • Super Rod / Town Map / Computer Search / Pokémon Catcher ×3 — recovery, prize fixing, and dragging the right target under the lock.
  • DCE ×4 — the only Energy; powers Deck and Cover via Mew.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: bench Gothita + Shelmet; ideally Tropical Beach, Juniper, drop your Basics. Use Mew or Shelmet so you have a way out of the Active spot.
  • Turn 2 (critical): Rare Candy → Gothitelle and get Magic Room online. Getting Goth up T2 shields your fragile benched evolutions from Catcher/Laser.

The core loop

With Goth + Float Stone Active, each turn: retreat Goth free, promote a Mew, Deck and Cover (paralyze/poison, shuffle Mew back), then Goth returns Active — so the opponent is Item-locked and paralyzed permanently. Use Dusknoir to move their Active's damage away (so it can't be KO'd out of the lock) and dump it on their bench. Add N to keep their hand empty. That's the "un-loseable" state: Goth + Float Stone, Dusknoir, Musharna, Accelgor, Mew/DCE, and all their N in the discard.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • T2–3 Gothitelle is the priority above all else — the lock protects the rest of your slow setup.
  • Shuffle Mew, not Accelgor, when you have the choice (keep Accelgor rebuilt on the bench; Mew re-loops as a Basic).
  • Don't waste Float Stone on the wrong Pokémon (e.g. a stuck Munna/Duskull start) — guard your Goth's free retreat.
  • Against Keldeo-EX (the main out), get Goth up fast and Catcher + Deck-and-Cover loop it to death before it can Rush In and reset.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Keldeo-EX / Rush In — the clean answer to paralysis + item lock; respect it.
  • Donks / bad starts — the deck is slow and setup-reliant; a Munna/Duskull opening or a prized key piece can cost the game before the lock lands.
  • Fast decks that race the setup (Plasma T2 Blizzard Burn, aggressive Blastoise) can KO your Duskull/Goth pieces before Magic Room is stable.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Magic Room (Gothitelle): no Item cards for either player while it's Active.
  • Deck and Cover (Accelgor, copied by Mew): 10 + paralyze + poison, shuffle back.
  • Lock target: T2–3 Gothitelle.
  • Energy: 4 DCE only.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Mew/Accelgor), Limitless (Kuras, 1st US Nationals 2013), SixPrizes ("Item Lock is Preeetty Good" Top-64 Nats report, "Gothitelle from the Ground Up"), The Deck Out. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
plasma_2013:
How to Pilot — Plasma (2013)

Format: BW-on 2013 (Plasma Freeze era). "TDK" = Thundurus-EX / Deoxys-EX / Kyurem PLF — regarded as the BDIF (best deck in format) of its time.

One-line identity

A fast, consistent Team Plasma toolbox: Thundurus-EX and Colress Machine accelerate Energy onto a benched Kyurem, whose Blizzard Burn does big damage as a single-prize attacker, all boosted by Deoxys-EX's Power Connect. You spread and snipe with cheap attacks, then finish with a non-EX hitter — winning the prize trade by giving up fewer prizes than you take.

The meta it lived in

After Plasma Freeze, TDK was the deck to beat: fast, consistent, powerful, and full of 1HKO potential. Its non-EX main attacker (Kyurem) meant it traded prizes favorably against the EX-heavy field, and Colress Machine let it attach multiple Energy a turn. It stayed dominant even as anti-Plasma tech (Silver Mirror) appeared.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Thundurus-EX (38/116) ×2 — the starter/accelerator. Raiden Knuckle: 30 damage and attach any Energy from your discard to a benched Team Plasma Pokémon — usually charging a Kyurem. Discard special Energy early (Ultra Ball) to fuel it.
  • Kyurem (31/116) ×4 — the main attacker, and crucially a non-EX (1 prize). Frost Spear: 30 + 30 bench snipe (great opener). Blizzard Burn: 120 (can't attack next turn) — reset it with a Switch/Float Stone to Burst again.
  • Deoxys-EX (53/116) ×4 — Power Connect: boosts your Team Plasma Pokémon's damage, stackably. Four benched Deoxys pushes Raiden Knuckle to 70 and Blizzard Burn to 160 (before Laser/Bangle). Best sitting on the bench.
  • Colress Machine ×3 — accelerator: attach a Plasma Energy from the deck to a Plasma Pokémon. It's an Item, so multiples per turn — this is what makes the deck truly fast (T1 Frost Spear, T2 Blizzard Burn).
  • Virbank City Gym ×2 + Hypnotoxic Laser ×4 — the damage engine: poison (20 with Virbank) + sleep, pushing Kyurem to 1HKO numbers (e.g. Landorus-EX).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Team Plasma Ball ×2 / Ultra Ball ×1 / Energy Search ×1 — search; Ultra Ball also discards Energy to fuel Raiden Knuckle.
  • Switch ×3 / Float Stone ×2 — reset Kyurem after Blizzard Burn so it can Burst again next turn (key to streaming damage).
  • Skyla ×3 / Juniper ×4 / N ×4 / Colress ×2 / Bianca ×1 — the draw/consistency spine; Skyla fetches the exact Item (Colress Machine, Laser) you need.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×4 — drag up EXs / support to snipe or KO.
  • Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 — grab any card at the critical moment.
  • Energy: 4 Plasma / 4 Prism / 2 Lightning / 3 Water — Plasma pairs with Colress Machine and powers Deoxys; Prism/Blend cover Kyurem (Water) and Thundurus (Lightning). Keeping some basic Water helps against Enhanced Hammer.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: start Thundurus-EX, discard special Energy (Ultra Ball), and Raiden Knuckle — 30 damage + an Energy onto a benched Kyurem. Or open Kyurem + Colress Machine for an immediate Frost Spear.
  • Turn 2: with Colress Machine(s) + Raiden Knuckle, a Kyurem is charged → Blizzard Burn for a big early hit. Get Deoxys on the bench ASAP to boost everything.

The core loop

Charge a benched Kyurem via Raiden Knuckle + Colress Machine, promote it, Blizzard Burn, then Switch/Float-Stone it out and charge the next Kyurem so you Burst again. Stack Deoxys on the bench to raise damage, add Laser + Virbank to hit exact 1HKO numbers, and Catcher to choose your targets. You give up single prizes off Kyurem while taking EXs — that prize math wins the game.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Fill the bench with Deoxys — more Deoxys = higher damage on every attack; 3–4 is the goal.
  • Kyurem is a single-prize attacker — lean on it so the opponent gets fewer prizes per KO than you do.
  • Reset Blizzard Burn with Switch/Float Stone so the "can't attack next turn" clause never stalls you.
  • Watch your special-Energy reliance — Enhanced Hammer and Aegislash-type effects punish it; keep some basic Water and don't over-commit Energy to a fragile 130-HP Kyurem.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Enhanced Hammer / special-Energy hate — the deck leans on Plasma/Prism/Blend; stripping it stalls your Bursts.
  • Fighting (Landorus-EX) — Thundurus is Fighting-Weak; you rely on Kyurem/Deoxys to win the trade.
  • Garbodor / Item-lock — Colress Machine and Laser are Items; shutting them off slows the engine badly (and Gothitelle's Magic Room is brutal here).
  • Fragile Kyurem — 130 HP with a big Energy investment can be punished before it Bursts.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Raiden Knuckle: 30 + attach any Energy from discard to a benched Plasma Pokémon.
  • Blizzard Burn: 120 (can't attack next turn; reset with Switch/Float Stone).
  • Power Connect (each Deoxys): stacks damage — 4 Deoxys → Blizzard Burn 160 base.
  • Frost Spear: 30 + 30 bench snipe; add Laser+Virbank (+ Deoxys) for 1HKO lines.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Plasma/TDK), SixPrizes ("Ultimate Guide to Playing Plasma", "New Cards, New Decks: Plasma Freeze Overview", "Deoxys-EX: Making the Connection"), PokéBeach TDK threads. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
rayeels_2013:
How to Pilot — RayEels (2013)

Format: BW-on 2013 (Plasma Storm/Freeze era). Rayquaza-EX + Eelektrik energy- acceleration deck.

One-line identity

A 1HKO machine: Eelektrik's Dynamotor moves Lightning from your discard onto the bench every turn, letting Rayquaza-EX fire Dragon Burst for 180 — enough to one-shot almost any EX. Build 3–4 Eels, then take six prizes in three big swings.

The meta it lived in

2013 was an OHKO arms race. RayEels sat right at the top of it: alongside Black Kyurem EX, it was one of the few decks that could reliably 1HKO EXs, and it only needed ~4 Energy to do it (versus Darkrai needing a whole LaserBank rig). It "just won't die" — strong from BLW-DRX all the way through Plasma, and it took a National Championship in this era. Tropical Beach + Keldeo/Float Stone made its shaky early game livable.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Rayquaza-EX (85/124) ×3 — the attacker. Dragon Burst: 180 damage, discard all Fire and Lightning attached. That discard is fine because Eelektrik just refuels it. You need 3 to keep streaming Bursts (a prized Ray really hurts).
  • Eelektrik (40/101) ×4 (from Tynamo ×4) — the engine. Dynamotor: once per Eel per turn, attach a Lightning from the discard to a benched Pokémon. Three Eels = three Energy a turn onto Rayquaza. The whole deck exists to get these online.
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) ×2 — the streaming trick: after a 180 Burst, Rayquaza has 1 Energy left. Rush In to Keldeo, Dynamotor all the Energy back onto Rayquaza, free-retreat with Float Stone → a fully reloaded Rayquaza every turn off a single copy.
  • Raikou-EX (38/108) ×1 — snipe tech: Volt Bolt hits 100 to the bench (KO Tynamos/support) and discards Lightning to feed Dynamotor. Great in the mirror.
  • Victini (14/101) ×1 — non-EX attacker (V-create, 100 with full bench) and a Fire body to help early Energy / anti-metal.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tropical Beach ×3 — Stadium; draw 7 on slow setup turns. The deck has a weak early game, so this is the consistency crutch that makes it tournament-viable.
  • Float Stone ×4 — free retreat for Keldeo/Rayquaza; core to the Rush-In reload loop.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Level Ball ×4 — get Tynamo/Eelektrik down fast (Level Ball, no cost) and discard Lightning (Ultra Ball) to feed Dynamotor. 4-4 Eel line is mandatory.
  • Juniper ×4 / N ×4 / Colress ×3 — draw; Juniper discards Energy (good here), N disrupts, Colress rewards a wide bench.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×3 — drag an EX up for a 180 KO, or pick off support.
  • Super Rod ×2 — recur Pokémon/Energy so you never run dry of Eels or Lightning.
  • Dowsing Machine (ACE SPEC) ×1 — reclaim a key Item late.
  • 8 Lightning / 4 Fire — Dragon Burst needs both types; Lightning also feeds Dynamotor.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: the priority is Tynamo down and Energy in the discard (Ultra Ball, Juniper). Get 3–4 Eelektrik built as fast as possible while surviving.
  • Turn 2+: once 2–3 Eels are up, Dynamotor + attachment fully charges a benched Rayquaza; promote it and Dragon Burst for 180.

The core loop

Dragon Burst for 180 → Rush In to Keldeo → Dynamotor all the Energy back onto a fresh Rayquaza → Float Stone free retreat → Burst again next turn. Three Bursts on three EXs = game. Use Catcher to choose the KO, Raikou to snipe the mirror, Super Rod to refill.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • 4-4 Eelektrik, 3 Rayquaza are non-negotiable counts — the deck lives or dies on streaming Bursts.
  • Protect Tynamo — opponents will snipe your 30–40 HP Tynamos to kill the engine; Mr. Mime (if teched) blocks that.
  • Get set up, then it snowballs — a good start wins, a bad start drowns; use Tropical Beach to dig out of slow hands.
  • Keldeo/Float Stone is your reload — don't forget you can Burst every turn off one Rayquaza with it.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Kyurem PLF — the nightmare: snipes your Tynamo/Eelektrik and pressures the engine. Mr. Mime is the answer.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Dynamotor; the engine dies.
  • Landorus-EX / fast Big Basics — early Catcher + snipe on Tynamo before you set up; a rough race.
  • Plasma (TDK) — heavy early pressure and Energy removal (Thundurus's Thunderous Noise, Crushing Hammer-style disruption) can starve your Bursts.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Dragon Burst: 180, discard all Fire + Lightning on Rayquaza.
  • Dynamotor: 1 Lightning from discard → bench, per Eelektrik, per turn.
  • Reload: 180 leaves 1 Energy → Rush In + Dynamotor refills to full.
  • Core counts: 4-4 Eels, 3 Rayquaza; Energy 8 Lightning / 4 Fire.

Sources: Bulbapedia (RayEels), SixPrizes ("RayEels – Navigating the Plasma Storm", "Handling the Freeze… With Eels!", UK Nationals winner report), PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
mega_kangaskhan_2014:
How to Pilot — Mega Kangaskhan (2014)

Format: NXD-on 2014 (XY "Big Basics + Muscle Band" era). This is the Fairy Box / "Crazy Punch" archetype — Michikazu Tsuda's build took Top-4 at 2014 Worlds.

One-line identity

A near-unkillable toolbox: M Kangaskhan-EX is a 230-HP wall that punches for big damage, while Aromatisse shuttles Fairy Energy around so you can strip a Pokémon bare, Max Potion it to full with zero downside, and then reload the Energy the same turn. You out-attrition everything and swap in the right attacker for each matchup.

The meta it lived in

2014 was a fast Basic-EX format where Muscle Band pushed everyone into high-damage, one-shot territory. Kangaskhan's answer was to be almost impossible to OHKO (230 HP) and to erase any chip damage with Aromatisse + Max Potion. It earned Top-4 at Worlds precisely because the format's OHKO math didn't work against it — though the arrival of Fighting attackers (Landorus/Lucario) that hit Kangaskhan's Weakness was its kryptonite.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • M Kangaskhan-EX (79/106) ×2 (from Kangaskhan-EX ×2) — the wall/attacker. 230 HP makes it a two-shot at best for most decks. Wham Bam Punch does big damage on double heads (Muscle Band lowers the flips needed to hit 180). It's a Mega, so it ends your turn to evolve — plan for that.
  • Aromatisse (93/146) ×2 (from Spritzee ×3) — the engine. Fairy Transfer: move Fairy Energy between your Pokémon freely. This does two jobs: load an attacker in one turn, and pull Energy off a Pokémon before Max Potion so you heal without discarding Energy.
  • Max Potion ×3 — with Aromatisse, this is a full heal for free: transfer the Energy away, Max Potion to full HP, transfer the Energy back. This is the whole "unkillable" plan.
  • Fairy Garden ×4 — Stadium giving free retreat to any Pokémon with Energy (trivial here). Lets you pivot the right attacker up without paying retreat.

The toolbox attackers (pick per matchup)

  • Mewtwo-EX (54/99) — X-Ball; answer to opposing Mewtwo and a splashable heavy hit (transfer all your Energy onto it for a huge swing).
  • Xerneas-EX (97/146) / Xerneas (96/146) — Geomancy (Xerneas) is easy Fairy acceleration to the bench; Xerneas-EX a Fairy attacker.
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) — Rush In pivot / status escape.
  • Suicune (20/101) — wall vs. EX attackers (takes reduced/again-immunity vs. EX in its era text); buys time.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Sycamore ×3 / N ×4 / Colress ×3 / Shauna ×1 / Bianca ×1 — the draw spine; N disrupts, Colress rewards a wide bench.
  • Ultra Ball ×2 / Level Ball ×2 / Heavy Ball ×1 — search the evolutions and Basics.
  • Muscle Band ×3 — the damage modifier that makes Wham Bam Punch's flips count.
  • Lysandre ×2 — drag up a support Basic or a target you can KO.
  • Tool Scrapper ×2 / Startling Megaphone ×1 — remove opposing Tools; crucial vs. Garbodor (which shuts off Fairy Transfer).
  • Super Rod ×1 / Dowsing Machine (ACE SPEC) ×1 — recovery / reclaim a key card.
  • 7 Fairy / 1 Water / 1 Rainbow / 2 DCE — Fairy fuels the engine; Rainbow/DCE enable the off-type attackers.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: bench Kangaskhan-EX + Spritzee; use draw Supporters (Sycamore) to build a hand. Start an Aromatisse and put Fairy Energy in play.
  • Turn 2: evolve into M Kangaskhan-EX (this ends your turn) once Aromatisse and a couple of Energy are set. From there, punch + Max Potion loop begins.

The core loop

Attack with M Kangaskhan → next turn, if damaged, Fairy Transfer the Energy off → Max Potion to full → Transfer back and punch again. Because you never actually lose the Energy, the wall just keeps swinging. Use Fairy Garden free retreat + Lysandre to line up the KO you want, and rotate Mewtwo/Xerneas/Keldeo in for specific matchups.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Play to the wall, not to speed — you win by being un-KO-able and healing, not by racing. Don't over-extend Energy you can't protect.
  • Sequence the Mega evolve — it ends your turn, so set up Aromatisse/Energy first.
  • Watch Wham Bam Punch's flips — Muscle Band lowers how many heads you need; know your out before committing.
  • Guard against Garbodor — Tool Scrapper/Megaphone reinstates Fairy Transfer, which is your entire engine.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Landorus-EX / Lucario-EX) — Kangaskhan is Fighting-Weak; these OHKO through your HP and heal plan. The deck's biggest problem after Furious Fists.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — turns off Fairy Transfer, breaking the Max Potion combo.
  • Enhanced Hammer / special-Energy hate — punishes Rainbow-reliant off-type attackers.
  • Pyroar (Intimidating Mane) is not a problem — M Kangaskhan isn't a Basic, so it KOs Pyroar cleanly.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • M Kangaskhan-EX: 230 HP (very hard to OHKO).
  • Max Potion combo: full heal, no Energy lost (via Fairy Transfer).
  • Fairy Garden: free retreat for Energy-bearing Pokémon.
  • Setup: T2 Mega evolve (ends turn), then the punch/heal loop.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Aromatisse/Techs, Crazy Punch — Tsuda Top-4 2014 Worlds), PokéBeach (Fairy/Kangaskhan threads). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
thundurus_deoxys_kyurem_tdk_2014:
How to Pilot — Thundurus/Deoxys/Kyurem (TDK) (2014)

Format: NXD-on 2014 (XY era). The same Team Plasma "TDK" core as 2013, updated with Muscle Band and XY trainers — still a top-tier deck into the new season.

One-line identity

A fast, consistent Team Plasma toolbox: Thundurus-EX + Colress Machine accelerate Energy onto a benched Kyurem, whose Blizzard Burn hits hard as a single-prize attacker, all boosted by Deoxys-EX's Power Connect. You spread, snipe, and 1HKO EXs while giving up fewer prizes than you take.

The meta it lived in

Carried over from its 2013 dominance, TDK stayed strong in the XY "Big Basics" format. Muscle Band gave every attack +20, sharpening Kyurem's KO math; Genesect-EX's Red Signal added a gust effect. Its single-prize main attacker kept the prize trade in its favor against an EX-saturated field.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Thundurus-EX (38/116) ×2 — starter/accelerator. Raiden Knuckle: 30 damage + attach any Energy from the discard to a benched Team Plasma Pokémon (usually charging Kyurem). Discard special Energy early (Ultra Ball) to fuel it.
  • Kyurem (31/116) ×3 — main attacker, and crucially a non-EX (1 prize). Frost Spear: 30 + 30 bench snipe. Blizzard Burn: 120 (can't attack next turn; reset with Switch). 1HKOs Landorus-EX with support.
  • Deoxys-EX (53/116) ×4 — Power Connect: stackably boosts your Team Plasma Pokémon's damage. Four on the bench pushes Raiden Knuckle to 70 and Blizzard Burn to 160 before modifiers. Best benched.
  • Genesect-EX (11/101) ×1 — Red Signal: when you attach a Plasma Energy to it, switch the opponent's Active with a benched Pokémon — a gust to drag up your target.
  • Colress Machine ×3 — accelerator: attach Plasma Energy from the deck to a Plasma Pokémon. It's an Item, so multiples per turn = the deck's speed.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Hypnotoxic Laser ×2 + Virbank City Gym ×1 — poison/sleep to push Kyurem to KO numbers.
  • Muscle Band ×4 — +20 per attack; central to Kyurem's 1HKO math in this format.
  • Switch ×3 / Scramble Switch (ACE SPEC) ×1 — reset Kyurem after Blizzard Burn to Burst again; Scramble Switch also moves Energy in a pinch.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×4 / Colress ×2 / Skyla ×3 — draw + fetch; Skyla grabs the exact Item (Colress Machine, Laser) you need.
  • Lysandre ×2 — gust EXs/support up for KOs (complements Red Signal).
  • Team Plasma Ball ×2 / Ultra Ball ×2 — search + Energy discard.
  • Startling Megaphone ×1 — clear opposing Tools (e.g. Garbodor's, or Silver Bangle/Muscle Band).
  • Max Potion ×1 / Tropical Beach ×1 — heal a Thundurus (it re-fuels itself); Beach digs on slow turns.
  • Energy: 4 Plasma / 4 Prism / 4 Rainbow / 2 Blend WLFM — heavy special-Energy base for Colress Machine + the multi-type attack costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: start Thundurus-EX, discard special Energy (Ultra Ball), Raiden Knuckle — 30 + an Energy onto a benched Kyurem. Or open Kyurem + Colress Machine for an immediate Frost Spear.
  • Turn 2: with Colress Machine(s) + Raiden Knuckle, a Kyurem is charged → Blizzard Burn. Get Deoxys down ASAP to boost everything.

The core loop

Charge a benched Kyurem (Raiden Knuckle + Colress Machine), promote, Blizzard Burn, then Switch it out and charge the next Kyurem to Burst again. Stack Deoxys for damage, add Laser + Virbank + Muscle Band to hit exact 1HKO numbers, and use Red Signal / Lysandre to pick targets. Single prizes off Kyurem while taking EXs wins the prize race.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Fill the bench with Deoxys — 3–4 for maximum damage.
  • Lean on Kyurem's single-prize attacks to keep the prize trade favorable.
  • Reset Blizzard Burn every turn with Switch so the "can't attack" clause never stalls you.
  • Manage special-Energy reliance — Enhanced Hammer / Aegislash-type effects punish it; don't over-commit Energy to a fragile 130-HP Kyurem.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Special-Energy hate (Enhanced Hammer, Aegislash-EX) — stalls the Plasma engine.
  • Fighting (Landorus-EX) — Thundurus is Fighting-Weak; rely on Kyurem/Deoxys to win the trade.
  • Garbodor / Item-lock — Colress Machine, Laser, Switch are all Items; shutting them off cripples the deck (Seismitoad's Quaking Punch is rough).

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Raiden Knuckle: 30 + attach any Energy from discard to a benched Plasma Pokémon.
  • Blizzard Burn: 120 (can't attack next turn; reset with Switch).
  • Power Connect (each Deoxys): stacks; 4 Deoxys → Blizzard Burn 160 base.
  • Muscle Band: +20; Frost Spear 30 + 30 snipe.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Plasma/TDK), SixPrizes (Plasma guides, Plasma Freeze overview), PokéBeach TDK threads. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
virizion_genesect_2014:
How to Pilot — Virizion/Genesect (2014)

Format: NXD-on 2014 (XY era). "VirGen." This build mirrors Andrew Estrada's 2014 World Championship-winning list (VirGen went 1st/2nd/3rd in Masters at Worlds).

One-line identity

A consistent acceleration deck: Virizion-EX's Emerald Slash loads Grass Energy onto a benched Genesect-EX, which then 1HKOs anything with the G Booster ACE SPEC and drags up whatever it wants with Red Signal. Immune to Special Conditions, hits huge, and plays the same reliable game every match.

The meta it lived in

2014's format rewarded consistency and the ability to punch through gimmicks (Pyroar, Suicune, high-HP walls). VirGen did exactly that — G Booster ignores protective effects and 1HKOs anything — and it was so reliable it swept the Masters podium at 2014 Worlds. Its kryptonite: Fire attackers (both key Pokémon are Fire-Weak) and the ever-present Garbodor.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Virizion-EX (9/101) ×4 — the accelerator and ideal starter. Emerald Slash: 60 damage and attach 2 Grass Energy from the deck to a benched Pokémon (usually Genesect). Verdant Wind (Ability): your Grass-Energy Pokémon are immune to Special Conditions — a huge shield vs. LaserBank. Run 4 so one's in play turn 1.
  • Genesect-EX (11/101) ×4 — the attacker. Megalo Cannon: 100 + 20 bench snipe. Red Signal (Ability): when you attach a Plasma Energy to it, switch the opponent's Active with a benched Pokémon — a free gust to line up KOs (saves Catcher slots).
  • G Booster (92/101, ACE SPEC) — the finisher. A Tool giving Genesect an attack that does 200 and ignores all effects on the Defending Pokémon (discard 2 Energy). This 1HKOs anything and beats Pyroar/Suicune/Safeguard. The single biggest reason the deck is strong.
  • Jirachi-EX (60/101) ×1 — Stellar Guidance: search any Supporter; consistency glue (and a fetch for the exact draw card you need).
  • Mr. Mime (47/116) ×1 — Bench Barrier: blocks bench damage (protects vs. Landorus/opposing Genesect snipe).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×4 / Skyla ×4 / Colress ×1 — draw + fetch; Skyla grabs Professor's Letter / G Booster / the right Item.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 — the only good Virizion search; also thins tech cards.
  • Energy Switch ×3 — move Grass off a spent Virizion onto Genesect; keeps G Booster streaming without wasting Emerald Slashes.
  • Skyarrow Bridge ×3 — free retreat for Virizion/Genesect (both retreat 1), so you pivot between accelerator and attacker for free.
  • Muscle Band ×3 — +20; nudges Megalo Cannon over KO lines.
  • Tool Scrapper ×2 — remove Garbodor's Tool to reinstate Verdant Wind / free retreat, and strip opposing Tools.
  • Enhanced Hammer ×2 — strip opposing special Energy (great vs. Plasma).
  • Shadow Triad ×2 / Professor's Letter ×1 / Colress Machine ×1 — recur Pokémon/Supporters, fetch Grass, extra acceleration; Super Rod ×1 / Town Map ×1 for recovery and prize fixing.
  • 9 Grass / 4 Plasma — Grass for Emerald Slash + attack costs (needs a lot, since Slash pulls it from deck); Plasma enables Red Signal.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: get Virizion-EX active with a Grass attached (or, if you can't, attach Grass to Genesect so you still threaten a turn-2 Megalo Cannon). Skyarrow down for free pivoting.
  • Turn 2: Emerald Slash — 60 damage and 2 Grass onto a benched Genesect. Now Genesect is charging toward Megalo Cannon / G Booster.

The core loop

Emerald Slash to load Genesect while chipping the Active → promote Genesect → Megalo Cannon (100 + snipe) or, when you need a clean kill, G Booster for 200 ignoring effects. Use Red Signal to drag the target you want up, Energy Switch to keep G Booster fueled, and Verdant Wind to shrug off Lasers the whole time.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Get Virizion active turn 1 — it's the whole engine; without the turn-2 Emerald Slash you fall behind.
  • Save G Booster for the target that needs it (a wall or a big EX); Megalo Cannon handles the rest.
  • Tool Scrapper vs. Garbodor — reinstating Verdant Wind + free retreat is often game-deciding.
  • Respect the late-game N — the deck can stall on a small hand; sequence draw so a topdeck N doesn't brick you.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire attackers (Charizard-EX, Flareon) — both key Pokémon are Fire-Weak and get 1HKO'd.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Verdant Wind and Red Signal; you need Scrapper.
  • Slow starts — no turn-1 attack means a whiffed Virizion setup can lose tempo you don't get back; the deck can be "cloggy."

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Emerald Slash: 60 + attach 2 Grass from deck to a benched Pokémon.
  • Megalo Cannon: 100 + 20 bench snipe.
  • G Booster: 200, ignores all effects on the Defending Pokémon (discard 2 Energy).
  • Verdant Wind: immunity to Special Conditions for Grass-Energy Pokémon.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Virizion-EX/Genesect-EX — Estrada 1st 2014 Worlds), Limitless (Estrada list), SixPrizes ("The Grass is Always Greener"), PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
yveltal_raichu_2014:
How to Pilot — Yveltal/Raichu (2014)

Format: NXD-on 2014 (XY era). A Dark "Big Basics" deck splashing a Raichu line as a Yveltal-mirror counter.

One-line identity

One of the two decks that defined 2014: Yveltal-EX hits for one of the format's biggest attacks (Evil Ball scales with Energy), Darkrai-EX gives the team free retreat and Dark Patch acceleration, and a Pikachu/Raichu line is teched in so the mirror doesn't come down to a coin-flip — Raichu's Circle Circuit 1HKOs opposing Yveltal-EX.

The meta it lived in

Yveltal-EX was arguably the most centralizing card of the year — so strong that people teched Raichu into everything just to beat it (some even inside their own Yveltal decks, exactly like this list). Muscle Band made every attack hit harder, and the Dark engine (Sableye, Dark Patch, Yveltal's Oblivion Wing) was the most consistent in the format. Yveltal-EX went on to headline the metagame for years.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Yveltal-EX (79/146) ×3 — the core. Evil Ball: 20 + 20 per Energy on both Active Pokémon (with Muscle Band + Laser it hits huge). Y-Cyclone moves an Energy to the bench as you attack — the preferred early attack so you don't overcommit.
  • Darkrai-EX (63/108) ×2 — Dark Cloak: free retreat for Darkness-carrying Pokémon (mobility for the whole team). A backup attacker and the reason Dark Patch works.
  • Pikachu (42/146) ×2 → Raichu (43/146) ×2 — the tech line. Circle Circuit: 10 × your benched Pokémon, for a single DCE. With a full bench it 1HKOs Yveltal-EX (and Sigilyph/other Lightning-Weak threats) — your mirror-breaker.
  • Sableye (62/108) ×1 — Junk Hunt: recur two Items (Laser, Energy Switch) from discard; free retreat under Dark Cloak.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Dark Patch ×4 — accelerate Darkness from the discard onto benched Dark Pokémon; the reason you can power a big Evil Ball fast.
  • Energy Switch ×2 — move Darkness around to hit exact Evil Ball numbers or fuel a quick attacker.
  • Hypnotoxic Laser ×4 + Virbank City Gym ×2 — poison/sleep + damage; pushes Evil Ball to KO numbers and disrupts.
  • Muscle Band ×3 — +20; central to the format's KO math.
  • Juniper ×4 / N ×4 / Colress ×2 / Random Receiver ×1 — the draw spine.
  • Ultra Ball ×3 / Level Ball ×1 / Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 / Professor's Letter ×1 — search + Energy discard for Dark Patch.
  • Lysandre ×1 / Pokémon Catcher ×1 — drag up targets for KOs.
  • Enhanced Hammer ×1 / Startling Megaphone ×1 / Switch ×1 / Super Rod ×1 — special- Energy removal, Tool removal, status escape, recovery.
  • 8 Darkness / 4 DCE — Darkness feeds Dark Patch + Evil Ball; DCE powers Raichu and a fast Yveltal.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (baby Yveltal-style): going second, use Oblivion Wing (baby Yveltal — note this list leans on Yveltal-EX/Y-Cyclone) or start chipping and banking Energy in the discard for Dark Patch. Bench a Darkrai for free retreat and a Pikachu if the mirror looms.
  • Early turns: Y-Cyclone to attack while stashing Energy on the bench, building toward a lethal Evil Ball without overexposing Energy to a return KO.

The core loop

Y-Cyclone early (attack + bank Energy safely) → build a big Evil Ball with Dark Patch + Energy Switch + Muscle Band + Laser for the KO → use Dark Cloak free retreat to rotate → drop the Raichu when you need to 1HKO an opposing Yveltal-EX. Sableye recurs your Lasers/Items to keep the pressure on.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Prefer Y-Cyclone until the late game — a huge early Evil Ball just feeds the opponent's own Evil Ball/Mewtwo/G-Booster return KO. Build the board first.
  • Bench management for Raichu — a fuller bench = bigger Circle Circuit; set it up before you need the mirror KO.
  • Bank Energy in the discard for Dark Patch; Juniper/Ultra Ball are your fuel.
  • Laser + Muscle Band are how a modest Evil Ball becomes lethal — track the numbers.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Landorus-EX/Lucario-EX) — Darkrai is Fighting-Weak; Yveltal's Fighting resistance helps, but Fighting decks still pressure the Dark side.
  • Opposing Raichu / Lightning — your own Yveltal-EX is Lightning-Weak; the mirror and Raichu decks punish it (which is why you run Raichu too).
  • Pyroar (Intimidating Mane) — blocks your Basic attackers; this straight-Dark build has trouble (Raichu is a non-answer since Pyroar KOs it).
  • Garbodor — shuts off Dark Cloak (free retreat) and Dark Patch.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Evil Ball: 20 + 20 per Energy on both Active Pokémon (huge with Muscle Band + Laser).
  • Circle Circuit (Raichu): 10 × your benched Pokémon for 1 DCE (1HKOs Yveltal-EX on a full bench).
  • Dark Cloak: free retreat for Darkness-carrying Pokémon.
  • Y-Cyclone: attack + move an Energy to your bench (the safe early attack).

Sources: Retro Library (2014 decks), Whimsicast (2014 NXD-XY meta), SixPrizes / PokéBeach Yveltal variant discussions, Pokemon.com 2014 Nationals decks. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
archie_s_blastoise_2015:
How to Pilot — Archie's Blastoise (2015)

Format: BCR-on 2015 (XY "Item-heavy combo" era). "ArchieStoise" / "HonorStoise" — this is Jacob Van Wagner's 2015 World Championship-winning list, essentially card-for- card.

One-line identity

A turn-1 combo deck: dump your hand with Item draw, discard a Blastoise with Battle Compressor / Ultra Ball, then Archie's Ace in the Hole puts it straight into play on turn 1. Now Deluge pours Water Energy onto Keldeo-EX for a giant Secret Sword — often attacking for 110+ on the first turn.

The meta it lived in

2015 was full of Item lock (Seismitoad) and disruption — yet ArchieStoise thrived because it did everything in one explosive turn before the lock mattered. Once Blastoise was down and Keldeo/Wailord were healing off Lasers with Rough Seas, Seismitoad's 30-damage Quaking Punch couldn't keep up. It won Worlds 2015 (Masters).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Archie's Ace in the Hole (124/160) ×2 — the combo enabler. If your hand is down to exactly this card (0 others after playing it), put a Blastoise from the discard onto your bench. Reuse it with VS Seeker.
  • Blastoise (31/149) ×2 — Deluge: attach any number of Water Energy from hand to your Pokémon each turn. It's the whole engine — and you want a 2nd line, since losing your only Blastoise kills the deck. You discard it, then Archie it into play.
  • Keldeo-EX (49/149) ×3 — the main attacker. Secret Sword scales with Water attached (Deluge dumps a pile in one turn → 110+ turn 1, or a 1HKO later). Rush In free pivot.
  • Jirachi-EX (60/101) ×2 — Stellar Guidance: search a Supporter — usually to find Archie's for the combo. A key consistency piece.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×2 — Set Up: draw until you have 6 — the engine that lets you burn your whole hand down to just Archie's.

The tech attackers (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Articuno (17/108) ×1 — steals an extra prize vs. single-prize decks (Night March) via its era text; a nifty prize-tempo tech.
  • Wailord-EX (38/160) ×1 — a 250-HP wall that heals with Rough Seas; miserable for Seismitoad to deal with.
  • Mewtwo-EX (54/113) ×1 — off-type attacker (X-Ball) that still benefits from Deluge; answers opposing Mewtwo/Garbodor.
  • Exeggcute (4/116) ×2 — Propagation: replay it from the discard as a bench body/ Ultra Ball fodder without losing a card — grease for the combo turn.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Battle Compressor ×4 — discard Blastoise + tech Pokémon + Supporters straight into the discard to set up Archie's and VS Seeker. The single most important consistency Item.
  • VS Seeker ×4 — replay Archie's/Juniper/Lysandre from the discard; the reason 2 Archie's is enough.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Acro Bike ×4 / Trainers' Mail ×4 — dig fast and discard freely to empty your hand to Archie's-only.
  • Superior Energy Retrieval ×4 — return 4 Water from discard so Deluge never runs dry.
  • Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 / Float Stone ×1 / Muscle Band ×1 — grab any card, free retreat, +20.
  • Rough Seas ×2 — heal 30 off Keldeo/Wailord between turns (huge vs. Laser/Toad).
  • Juniper ×2 / Lysandre ×1 / N ×1 — minimal Supporter line (you find them via Jirachi
  • VS Seeker); Lysandre drags up KO targets.
  • 11 Water — the fuel for Deluge + Secret Sword.

Ideal early game (the combo turn)

  • Turn 1: Shaymin Set Up to draw into the engine → Battle Compressor a Blastoise (and junk) into the discard → Ultra Ball / Acro Bike / Trainers' Mail to burn your hand down to exactly Archie's Ace in the Hole → play it, Blastoise hits the bench → Deluge a pile of Water onto Keldeo-EX → Secret Sword for 110+.
  • If the T1 combo isn't there, you can fall back on a slower Blastoise line, but the deck wants the explosive open.

The core loop

Once Blastoise is down: each turn Deluge Water onto Keldeo (refilled by Superior Energy Retrieval) → Secret Sword for a KO → Rough Seas heals your Active → Lysandre drags up the next target. Swap to Wailord/Articuno/Mewtwo when the matchup calls for it.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Hand management is everything — you must reduce to exactly Archie's (nothing else) to trigger it; sequence Compressor/Ultra Ball/Bike carefully.
  • Protect a 2nd Blastoise — one Blastoise KO'd without a backup ends your engine.
  • Jirachi finds Archie's — treat it as your combo tutor.
  • Watch for the whiff — like any combo deck, a Jirachi/Archie's prized or a bad Shaymin draw can flatline you; know your outs before committing.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Itself — it's a combo deck; bad opening hands or a prized Jirachi/Archie's cost games.
  • Leafeon-type "energy on board" punishers — decks that exploit your huge Energy count.
  • Heavy early disruption before the combo lands (a T1 Seismitoad lock the turn you needed Items) can stumble you — though once Blastoise is down, Toad is a good matchup.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Archie's: put a Blastoise into play if your hand is only Archie's after playing it.
  • Deluge: attach any number of Water from hand per turn.
  • Secret Sword: scales with Water on Keldeo (~7 = 1HKO a Basic EX; 110+ turn 1).
  • Combo target: turn-1 Blastoise → Secret Sword.

Sources: Bulbapedia (HonorStoise — Van Wagner 1st 2015 Worlds; Blastoise/Keldeo), Limitless (Van Wagner list), Retro Library (2015 decks), Pokemon.com Worlds 2015. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
lando_bats_2015:
How to Pilot — Lando Bats (2015)

Format: BCR-on 2015 (XY "Fighting aggro" era). "Landobats" — this build mirrors Rowan Stavenow's Junior World Champion list.

One-line identity

Fast Fighting aggression: cheap, hard-hitting attackers (Landorus-EX, Hawlucha, Lucario-EX) boosted by Strong Energy / Fighting Stadium / Muscle Band, plus a Zubat → Golbat → Crobat line that drops free damage counters every time it evolves. You spread and snipe, then clean up KOs a turn early.

The meta it lived in

2015 rewarded fast, cheap damage, and Landorus was the dominant Fighting attacker — preying on Darkrai/Yveltal Weakness and, with the Bats, applying pressure every single turn. It won the Junior World Championship and put multiple copies into Worlds top cut. The Bats' evolve-damage is what makes the deck "hit every turn without attacking."

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Landorus-EX (89/149) ×2 — the star. Hammerhead: 30 to the Active + 30 to a benched Pokémon for a single Energy — cheap, repeatable spread that sets up KOs. Land's Judgment (3 Energy) for a big finish. With Strong Energy + Fighting Stadium
  • Muscle Band it hits hard fast.
