  • Hawlucha (63/111) ×4 — non-EX attacker; for one Energy it does 80 to EXs (+ more with Strong Energy/Stadium) — 100 vs. an EX with support, giving up only 1 prize. Free retreat. Your efficient EX-killer.
  • Zubat (31/119) ×4 → Golbat (32/119) ×3 → Crobat (33/119) ×3 — the damage engine. Sneaky Bite (Golbat) puts 2 counters and Surprise Bite (Crobat) puts 3 counters on any opposing Pokémon when you evolve it — free damage each turn to soften targets. The whole line has no retreat cost.
  • Lucario-EX (54/111) ×1 — a second Fighting attacker with a different Weakness and three attacks; Corkscrew Smash damages + draws. Good vs. Seismitoad matchups.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×1 — Set Up: draw to 6; the consistency engine.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Super Scoop Up ×4 — pick up a Golbat/Crobat to re-use its Bite on replay (more free damage), or save a damaged attacker. Flip-based, hence 4.
  • Scoop Up Cyclone (95/101, ACE SPEC) ×1 — a guaranteed scoop (no flip) to re- trigger a Bat's damage or dodge a KO.
  • Muscle Band ×4 — +20; core to the KO math.
  • Fighting Stadium ×3 — +20 when your Fighting Pokémon attack an EX (big with Hawlucha/Landorus).
  • Strong Energy ×4 — +20 damage when attached to a Fighting Pokémon; 5 Fighting round out the Energy.
  • Silent Lab ×2 — Stadium that shuts off Abilities on Basic Pokémon (hurts Shaymin-EX/Jirachi-EX-reliant decks) — a counter-Stadium and disruption piece.
  • Korrina ×2 — search a Fighting Pokémon and a Fighting Item (Muscle Band, etc.) in one card — huge consistency.
  • Juniper ×4 / N ×2 / Colress ×2 / AZ ×1 — draw; AZ picks up a Bat to re-Bite or saves a Pokémon.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×2 / Lysandre ×2 — search, Supporter recursion, and gust targets for KOs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Shaymin Set Up, bench Zubat(s) and a Landorus/Hawlucha; use Korrina to grab an attacker + Muscle Band. Hammerhead to start chipping (30 active + 30 bench) and evolve a Zubat if you can for a free Bite.
  • Early turns: stack evolve-damage (Golbat/Crobat) + Hammerhead snipe so multiple opposing Pokémon are in KO range.

The core loop

Each turn: Hammerhead for spread, evolve/replay Bats (via Super Scoop Up / Scoop Up Cyclone / AZ) to keep dropping free counters, and use Hawlucha (+ Strong Energy / Fighting Stadium / Muscle Band) to 1HKO EXs cheaply. Lysandre drags the softened target up. You take prizes faster than the opponent can stabilize.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Spread first, then convert — Hammerhead + Bites set up multi-prize turns; don't rush a single KO when you can set up two.
  • Re-use the Bats — Super Scoop Up / Scoop Up Cyclone / AZ replaying a Crobat is extra free damage; that's the deck's engine.
  • Hawlucha kills EXs for 1 prize — lean on it so the prize trade favors you.
  • Korrina is your setup button — it finds attacker + Item together.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Seismitoad-EX (Item lock) — Quaking Punch shuts off Super Scoop Up and your Item engine; lean on Lucario-EX and Supporters. A real grind.
  • Grass attackers — Landorus-EX is Grass-Weak (Leafeon/other Grass punish it).
  • Bench-damage protection (Mr. Mime) — blunts Hammerhead's snipe and the Bites.
  • Silent Lab cuts both ways — it can shut off your Shaymin-EX too; sequence its use.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Hammerhead: 30 active + 30 bench snipe for 1 Energy.
  • Hawlucha: 80 to EXs base (+ Strong Energy / Fighting Stadium / Muscle Band → ~100+), single-prize.
  • Golbat/Crobat on evolve: 2 / 3 damage counters on any opposing Pokémon.
  • Boosters: Strong Energy +20, Fighting Stadium +20 vs. EX, Muscle Band +20.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Landorus/Crobat), Pokemon.com Worlds 2015 (Stavenow, Junior World Champion list; tournament analysis), PokéBeach Landobats threads. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
seismitoad_crobat_2015:
How to Pilot — Seismitoad/Crobat (2015)

Format: BCR-on 2015 (XY "control" era). "Seismibats." Seismitoad-EX went 2nd/3rd at 2015 Worlds (Masters); the Crobat build was its most popular form.

One-line identity

A lock-and-grind control deck: Seismitoad-EX's Quaking Punch does 30 and stops the opponent from playing Item cards every turn, while a Zubat → Golbat → Crobat line drops free damage counters on evolve and Hypnotoxic Laser + Virbank add poison. The opponent can't use Items, slowly drowns in chip damage, and you heal/reset with Super Scoop Up.

The meta it lived in

2015 leaned heavily on Items (Blastoise's combo, VS Seeker, ball search, Night March's Battle Compressor). Seismitoad punished all of it: Quaking Punch every turn is a soft Item lock that most decks fold to. It was a season-long menace, taking 2nd and 3rd at Worlds. Its own weaknesses were energy-acceleration decks and high-Supporter decks that don't need Items.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Seismitoad-EX (20/111) ×3 — the core. Quaking Punch: 30 damage + the opponent can't play Items from hand next turn, for a single DCE. The lock is the deck. High HP; run 3–4 so a prized/KO'd Toad doesn't stop you (and you can keep a "blank" backup).
  • Zubat (31/119) ×4 → Golbat (32/119) ×4 → Crobat (33/119) ×3 — the damage engine. Sneaky Bite (2 counters) / Surprise Bite (3 counters) on any opposing Pokémon when you evolve — this is what turns Quaking Punch's measly 30 into real KOs. No retreat cost.
  • Hypnotoxic Laser ×4 + Virbank City Gym ×3 — poison (20/turn with Virbank) + sleep; the other half of your damage output and a disruption flip.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×2 — Set Up: draw to 6; helps you hit the T1 DCE + Muscle Band + Laser + Virbank opener.
  • Mewtwo-EX (54/113) ×2 — off-type attacker (X-Ball) for when you need a bigger hit or to answer opposing Mewtwo.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Super Scoop Up ×4 — the utility MVP: pick up a damaged Seismitoad and instantly replace it (denying KOs), or re-use a Bat's Bite on replay. Being able to Scoop while the opponent can't (Item lock) is huge.
  • Muscle Band ×3 — +20; makes Quaking Punch a real 2HKO on EXs.
  • Xerosic ×2 — Supporter that discards an opposing Tool or Special Energy — great vs. Garbodor's Float Stone and vs. special-Energy decks.
  • Juniper ×4 / N ×3 / Colress ×1 — draw + disruption; N shrinks their hand while they're already Item-locked.
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Computer Search (ACE SPEC) ×1 — Supporter recursion and search (used before the lock, or on your own turn).
  • Lysandre ×1 / AZ ×1 — gust a target up; AZ picks up a Bat to re-Bite or saves a Toad (no flip, unlike Super Scoop Up).
  • 4 DCE / 3 Water — DCE powers Quaking Punch in one attachment; Water for the rare Grenade Hammer / off-cost.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (ideal): Shaymin Set Up into DCE + Muscle Band + Hypnotoxic Laser + Virbank City Gym on a Seismitoad → Quaking Punch. Now they're Item-locked from turn 1 and taking poison.
  • Early turns: bench Zubats and evolve for free Bite counters; keep the lock up every turn.

The core loop

Quaking Punch every turn (announce it "for the thirtieth time") to keep Items locked, stack Laser + Virbank poison and Bat evolve-counters to build KOs, and Super Scoop Up to reset a damaged Toad (or re-Bite). N keeps their hand thin while they're locked. You grind six prizes through chip damage while they can't execute their Item-dependent plan.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Never stop Quaking Punching — if you're not announcing it, you're probably losing. The lock is worth more than the 30 damage.
  • Keep a backup Toad — ideally one with Muscle Band up; Super Scoop Up momentum depends on having another Toad ready.
  • Bank Bite damage — evolve Bats onto the targets you'll KO; Super Scoop Up + AZ let you Bite the same board twice.
  • Xerosic vs. Garbodor — strip the Float Stone/Tool so their Garbotoxin can't turn off your (few) Abilities, and to keep pressure on special-Energy decks.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Energy-acceleration decks — anything that floods Energy (Bronzong, Primal Kyogre, fast Fighting) can overpower a 30-damage attacker before the lock wins.
  • Heavy-Supporter / low-Item decks — they don't care about the Item lock.
  • Rough Seas / healing (Manectric-EX decks) — heals off your chip damage; you lose the Stadium war.
  • Primal Groudon-EX (Ω Barrier) — shuts off your Lasers/disruption Items and out- muscles you; a very hard matchup.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Quaking Punch: 30 + opponent can't play Items next turn, for 1 DCE.
  • Golbat/Crobat on evolve: 2 / 3 damage counters on any opposing Pokémon.
  • Poison w/ Virbank: 20 between turns; Muscle Band +20.
  • Setup: T1 Quaking Punch off Shaymin + DCE.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Seismitoad-EX), Pokemon.com Worlds 2015 (seismitoad-crobat; tournament analysis), SixPrizes ("Talking Toad", States reports), PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
darkrai_giratina_garbodor_2016:
How to Pilot — Darkrai Giratina Garbodor (2016)

Format: XY Standard 2016. "Black Dragon" — this build mirrors Shunto Sadahiro's 2016 Junior World Championship-winning list.

One-line identity

A fast, controlling toolbox: Max Elixir + Double Dragon Energy power a turn-2 Darkrai-EX whose Dark Pulse scales with Darkness Energy in play (up to 180+), while Giratina-EX's Chaos Wheel locks the opponent out of Tools, Stadiums, and Special Energy — and Garbodor's Garbotoxin shuts off their Abilities. Hit hard early, then strangle the game.

The meta it lived in

2016 Standard was full of decks reliant on Abilities (Greninja, Vespiquen, Volcanion's setup) and Special Energy (Night March's DCE, Mega Rayquaza, other Dragons). Darkrai/ Giratina/Garbodor attacked all of that at once: Chaos Wheel + Garbotoxin is a double lock, while Dark Pulse provided the raw damage. With Startling Megaphone/Xerosic rotated, opponents had almost no way to remove Garbodor's Tool. It won Juniors Worlds and put up strong Masters results.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Darkrai-EX (74/122) ×2 — the main attacker. Dark Pulse: 20 + 20 per Darkness Energy on all your Pokémon. With Max Elixir + Double Dragon (counts as Darkness) + Reverse Valley, it quickly reaches 180 for a big EX KO. Note: this is the BKP Darkrai (attacker), not the old Dark Cloak one.
  • Giratina-EX (57/98) ×2 — the lock. Chaos Wheel: 90 damage and the opponent can't play Tools, Stadiums, or Special Energy next turn. Renegade Pulse (Ability): immune to damage/effects from Mega Evolutions. Mostly sits on the bench feeding Double Dragon to Darkrai until you want the lock.
  • Garbodor (57/122) ×2 (from Trubbish ×2) — Garbotoxin: while it has a Tool attached, all Abilities (both players') are shut off. Turns off Greninja's Shuriken, Vespiquen/Yanmega engines, Shaymin draw, etc. Attach Float Stone so it can retreat.
  • Yveltal (65/114) ×2 — Oblivion Wing: 30 + attach a Darkness from the discard to a Pokémon; a non-EX attacker and extra acceleration toward big Dark Pulses.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Max Elixir ×4 — look at the top 6 cards and attach a basic Darkness to a Basic Pokémon; the deck's turbo acceleration (this is why it wants a high Darkness count).
  • Double Dragon Energy ×3 — counts as 2 of every type: fuels Giratina's Chaos Wheel and boosts Darkrai's Dark Pulse (as Darkness) — the glue between the two attackers.
  • Fighting Fury Belt ×3 — +40 HP and +10 damage; makes Darkrai/Giratina bulkier and nudges Chaos Wheel/Dark Pulse over KO lines.
  • Float Stone ×3 — free retreat, especially to keep Garbodor mobile (and to pivot attackers).
  • Reverse Valley ×1 / Parallel City ×1 — Reverse Valley boosts your Dark damage (+10) and bumps opposing Stadiums; Parallel City shrinks benches / cuts Water damage.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×2 / Lysandre ×2 / Xerosic ×1 / Pokémon Ranger ×1 / AZ ×1 — draw + control. Lysandre drags up Shaymin-EX/support for KOs; Xerosic strips a Tool/ Special Energy; Pokémon Ranger frees you from an opposing Chaos Wheel/lock; AZ heals/resets.
  • Hoopa-EX (36/98) ×1 / Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×2 — Hoopa pulls the EX trio (Darkrai/ Giratina/Shaymin) for setup; Shaymin burst draw.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×4 / Trainers' Mail ×2 / Super Rod ×1 — search, Supporter recursion, dig, recovery.
  • 10 Darkness + 3 Double Dragon — Darkness is the whole point (Max Elixir + Dark Pulse scaling).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: Hoopa-EX for Darkrai/Giratina/Shaymin, hit Max Elixir(s) to bank Darkness on a benched Darkrai, and get a Giratina-EX down with Double Dragon. By turn 2 you can Dark Pulse for a meaningful hit; aim to establish Garbodor around turn 3.

The core loop

Accelerate Darkness (Max Elixir + Oblivion Wing) so Dark Pulse climbs toward 180 → attack while Garbodor shuts off their Abilities → drop Chaos Wheel when you want to lock Tools/Stadiums/Special Energy and stall them out. Lysandre picks off benched support; N keeps their hand thin under the lock. You control the game from a prize lead.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Bank Darkness aggressively — Dark Pulse and Max Elixir both want a high Darkness count; more Energy in play = bigger hits.
  • Establish Garbodor early vs. Ability decks (Greninja, Vespiquen, Volcanion) — Garbotoxin is often the whole matchup; protect the Tool.
  • Chaos Wheel to stall / vs. Special-Energy decks — locking DCE/Double Dragon cripples Night March and other Dragons.
  • Keep a Float Stone on Garbodor so it isn't stranded Active.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Yveltal/Garbodor — Yveltal BKT (Fright Night) turns off your Float Stone (and Abilities), and Pitch-Black Spear snipes your bench; a genuinely awful matchup.
  • Yveltal-EX — strong anti-Giratina card; 2HKOs Chaos Wheel Giratina cheaply, so you can't lean on the lock into it.
  • Volcanion-EX — fast Fire pressure (Darkrai has no Fighting Weakness, but Volcanion's bench damage + speed race you).
  • Pokémon Ranger / effect removal — opponents can shrug off your Chaos Wheel lock for a turn.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Dark Pulse: 20 + 20 per Darkness Energy on all your Pokémon (→ ~180 with setup; Double Dragon counts as Darkness).
  • Chaos Wheel: 90 + opponent can't play Tools / Stadiums / Special Energy next turn.
  • Garbotoxin: all Abilities off while Garbodor holds a Tool.
  • Max Elixir: attach a basic Darkness from the top 6 to a Basic Pokémon.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Black Dragon — Sadahiro 1st 2016 Juniors Worlds), Pokemon.com (2016 Juniors/Origins decks), SixPrizes ("Monster from the Black Lagoon"), PokéBeach ("A Formidable Friendship"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
greninja_2016:
How to Pilot — Greninja (2016)

Format: XY Standard 2016. "Ninja Blitz" — this build mirrors Cody Walinski's 2016 World Championship finalist Greninja BREAK deck.

One-line identity

A grindy spread-and-lock deck: Greninja BREAK's Giant Water Shuriken puts 6 damage counters on any Pokémon every turn (for a discarded Water) without attacking, while Greninja's Shadow Stitching can lock the opponent's Abilities. You chain multiple Shurikens per turn via free retreat, pick off benched Shaymin-EX, and win on damage the opponent can never quite answer.

The meta it lived in

2016 belonged to Night March, and Greninja was one of its best answers — the Shuriken snipes Joltik/Combee/Shaymin-EX off the bench, and 170-HP Greninja BREAK is far bulkier than any Night Marcher. The deck's fix for its historically slow setup was Talonflame, which lets a Stage-2 line come online fast. Its enduring claim to fame: reliably KO'ing opposing benched Shaymin-EX.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Greninja BREAK (41/122) ×3 — the engine. 170 HP; Giant Water Shuriken: once per turn as your Active, discard a Water to put 6 damage counters (60) on any opposing Pokémon — before your attack, and it doesn't cost your attack. Free retreat lets you rotate a fresh Greninja BREAK in to fire another Shuriken.
  • Greninja (40/122) ×3 + (41/146) ×1 — the Stage 2. Shadow Stitching: 40 and locks the opponent's Abilities next turn (shuts off Shaymin/Octillery/Trevenant). Moonlight Slash: up to 80. Free retreat — key to chaining Shurikens.
  • Frogadier (39/122) ×4 — the setup engine. Water Duplicates: search up to 3 Frogadier straight to the bench, skipping the Basic step — this is how a Stage-2 deck keeps pace.
  • Talonflame (96/114) ×4 — Gale Wings: starts as your Active on turn 1 even though it's a Stage 2; Aero Blitz does 40 and draws 2. Free retreat. The card that makes the deck consistent enough to compete.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Bursting Balloon ×4 — Tool that damages the attacker when they hit the holder; crucially, it lets you 2-shot Mega Evolutions and chip toward Shuriken KOs even under Ability lock (Garbodor).
  • Rough Seas ×3 — Stadium; heal 30 off your Active/benched Water Pokémon between turns. Combined with free retreat, you cycle damaged Greninja out and heal them — making 2HKOs very hard for the opponent.
  • Dive Ball ×4 / Level Ball ×2 — search your Water Pokémon (Froakie → Greninja BREAK, Octillery if run); the deck's main search.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×4 / Ace Trainer ×1 / Fisherman ×1 / Pokémon Ranger ×1 — draw + utility. Ace Trainer disrupts (good after a Frogadier is KO'd, since you're behind on prizes); Fisherman returns 4 Water to hand (a big Shuriken refuel); Pokémon Ranger removes effects (frees you from Chaos Wheel / status).
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Super Rod ×2 / Battle Compressor ×1 / Startling Megaphone ×1 — Supporter recursion, recover Pokémon/Energy, dig, strip Tools.
  • 8 Water + 2 Splash Energy — Water fuels Shuriken/attacks; Splash Energy returns a Greninja to hand when KO'd (recur your attackers).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: open Talonflame (Gale Wings puts it Active) — Aero Blitz for 40 + draw 2, or just set up. Bench a Froakie, and use Frogadier's Water Duplicates to flood the bench with Frogadier. By turn 2–3 you have multiple Greninja → Greninja BREAK.

The core loop

Each turn: Giant Water Shuriken (60 counters) on a target before attacking, then free-retreat to a second Greninja BREAK and Shuriken again — two or three Shurikens a turn adds up fast. Attack with Shadow Stitching to lock Abilities when it matters, or Moonlight Slash for damage. Rough Seas heals your board; Fisherman refuels Water; Splash Energy brings KO'd Greninja back. Snipe Shaymin-EX off the bench for cheap prizes.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Chain Shurikens — free retreat means multiple 60-counter drops per turn; this is the deck's real damage output, not the attacks.
  • Snipe benched Shaymin-EX / support — the Shuriken hits anything; picking off draw engines wins games.
  • Rough Seas + free retreat = near-un-2HKO-able — cycle damaged Greninja and heal 30 each turn.
  • Shadow Stitching at the right moment locks the opponent's whole engine — time it.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — turns off Giant Water Shuriken (your entire engine); with Megaphone/Xerosic rotated, your only out is Lysandre the Trubbish before it evolves, or 2-shotting with Bursting Balloon. The deck's biggest fear.
  • Mega Audino — high HP + Magical Symphony + Pokémon Center Lady + Parallel City is a brutal matchup (it beat Greninja in the 2016 Worlds final).
  • Hex Maniac — a one-turn Ability lock stops your Shuriken that turn.
  • Slow starts — it's effectively a Stage-3 deck; a bad Talonflame-less open falls behind fast.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Giant Water Shuriken: 6 counters (60) on any Pokémon, once per turn as Active, for a discarded Water — chainable via free retreat.
  • Shadow Stitching: 40 + Ability lock next turn.
  • Rough Seas: heal 30 off your Water Pokémon each turn.
  • Setup: Talonflame T1, Frogadier's Water Duplicates to flood the bench.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Greninja BREAK), Limitless / official Worlds deck ("Ninja Blitz" — Walinski, 2016 Worlds finalist), Pokemon.com (Giant Water Shuriken strategy), SixPrizes ("Greninja the Frog Here"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
mega_audino_2016:
How to Pilot — Mega Audino (2016)

Format: XY Standard 2016. "Magical Symphony" — Shintaro Ito's 2016 World Championship- winning (Masters) rogue deck, essentially card-for-card.

One-line identity

A patient control-beatdown: M Audino-EX is a 220-HP wall whose Magical Symphony does 110 to the Active and 50 to the bench whenever you play a Supporter — so your whole 37-card Trainer engine doubles as attack fuel. You out-tank the format while sniping fragile benched Pokémon (Shaymin-EX, Joltik) for easy multi-prize turns.

The meta it lived in

Everyone came to 2016 Worlds prepared for Night March and its counters. Nobody was ready for Mega Audino — a Japanese rogue whose 110+50 spread was perfectly tuned: 110 OHKOs Shaymin-EX and Vespiquen, and the 50 bench snipe simultaneously picks off Joltik/Combee/Eevee. Its 220 HP + healing made it miserable to KO. It "came out of left field" and won the Masters Division; ironically, Night March rotated right after, so its best matchup vanished.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • M Audino-EX (85/124) ×3 (from Audino-EX ×4) — the wall/attacker. Magical Symphony: 110 to the Active + 50 to a benched Pokémon if you played a Supporter this turn. 220 HP makes it a 3-shot for much of the format. It's a Mega (evolving ends your turn) — but Audino Spirit Link fixes that.
  • Audino Spirit Link ×4 — Tool that lets you Mega-evolve Audino without ending your turn, so you can evolve and attack the same turn. Essential.
  • Metal Energy ×6 + DCE ×4 — Metal fuels Magical Symphony's cost and enables Magearna-EX / Cobalion; DCE is flexible.
  • Absol (40/108) ×1 — Cursed Eyes: move 3 damage counters on the opponent's side. Combined with the 50 bench snipe, it lets you finish off exactly the Pokémon you want (Frogadier, Pumpkaboo).

The toolbox techs (why the 1-ofs)

  • Magearna-EX (75/114) ×1 — Mystic Heart: your Metal Pokémon take no effects of attacks (other than damage). Shuts off Trevenant BREAK's Silent Fear, Froakie's Bubble, and other counter-placement/effect attacks.
  • Cobalion (74/114) ×1 — a Metal attacker; answers Glaceon-EX / Regice (which block Mega damage) since they're Metal-Weak, and closes games.
  • Hoopa-EX (36/98) ×1 — Scoundrel Ring: pull 3 EXs from the deck (Audino-EX + Shaymin-EX) for an explosive setup.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×2 — Set Up burst draw.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×2 / AZ ×2 / Trainers' Mail ×4 — the draw spine. Because every Supporter triggers Magical Symphony, you want a high Supporter count; AZ doubles as healing (pick up a damaged Audino) and triggers the attack.
  • Pokémon Center Lady ×1 — heal 60 and remove Special Conditions — the key that forces opponents to attack Audino a third time, cementing the wall.
  • Parallel City ×2 — versatile Stadium: shrink your own bench to dodge snipes, or reduce the opponent's bench / their Water damage (great vs. Greninja).
  • Lysandre ×2 — drag up a benched target to KO (or combine with the 50 snipe for a double-prize turn).
  • Hex Maniac ×1 / Xerosic ×1 / Startling Megaphone ×1 — Ability lock, Tool/Energy removal, Tool removal (answers Garbodor, etc.).
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Mega Turbo ×1 / Float Stone ×2 / Escape Rope ×1 / Super Rod ×1 — Supporter recursion, search, fast Energy onto a Mega, free retreat, switching, recovery.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: get an Audino-EX down (Hoopa-EX can fetch it), attach Audino Spirit Link, and Mega-evolve without ending your turn. Use Mega Turbo / DCE to power up fast. Then play a Supporter every turn so Magical Symphony is always online.

The core loop

Attack with Magical Symphony every turn (always play a Supporter first!) — 110 to the Active, 50 to a chosen benched Pokémon. Use Absol to shift damage so the 50 finishes a specific target; Lysandre to pull a Shaymin-EX up for the double KO; Pokémon Center Lady / AZ / Parallel City to keep Audino un-KO-able. Bring in Cobalion / Magearna for the walls that stop Megas.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Always play a Supporter before attacking — no Supporter = no Magical Symphony bonus. Sequence your turn around it.
  • Aim the 50 snipe deliberately — it turns single-prize turns into double-prize turns (Shaymin-EX on the bench is the dream target).
  • Use Parallel City both ways — protect your own bench from snipes, or choke the opponent's Water damage/bench.
  • Cobalion/Magearna are your answers to Glaceon/Regice/effect-immunity walls — don't forget them.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting attackers — Audino is Colorless (some Fighting hits it), and dedicated Fighting/Fury decks pressure it.
  • Fast decks that ignore the wall — a deck that races prizes faster than you can heal (though Night March, its main "fast" foe, actually feeds Audino via Shaymin snipes).
  • Ability/effect walls without your techs prized — if Cobalion or Magearna is prized, Glaceon/Regice/Trevenant can be very hard.
  • Garbodor — turns off Magearna/your Abilities; lean on Hex Maniac/Xerosic.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Magical Symphony: 110 Active + 50 bench, only if you played a Supporter that turn.
  • M Audino-EX: 220 HP (often a 3-shot).
  • Audino Spirit Link: Mega-evolve without ending your turn.
  • Pokémon Center Lady: heal 60 + remove Special Conditions.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Mega Audino EX; Magical Symphony — Ito 1st 2016 Worlds), Limitless (Ito list), Pokemon.com (2016 Worlds Masters analysis), PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
night_march_2016:
How to Pilot — Night March (2016)

Format: XY Standard 2016 (Primal Clash–Fates Collide, "BREAKpoint" era). The Tier-0 deck of the format — won nearly half of Masters State Championships.

One-line identity

The fastest, most consistent aggro deck in the game: Battle Compressor dumps Joltik / Pumpkaboo / Lampent into the discard, and each one's Night March attack does 20 × the number of Night Marchers in the discard — up to 200+ for a single DCE, often on the very first attack. Cheap, single-prize attackers that 1HKO EXs.

The meta it lived in

Night March was the deck to beat in 2016 — speed, power, and consistency all at once. Two BREAKpoint cards pushed it over the top: Puzzle of Time (retrieve 2 DCE from the discard, breaking the old "only 4 DCE" ceiling) and Fighting Fury Belt (Joltik to 70 HP, dodging Yveltal's Oblivion Wing). It was so dominant that entire decks (Greninja, Mega Audino, Trevenant) existed mainly to counter it — Night March was the consensus best deck in the format (BDIF) for much of the year.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Joltik (26/119) ×4 — Lightning-type Night Marcher; the primary attacker. 1HKOs Yveltal-EX (Lightning Weakness) and hits huge with a full discard.
  • Pumpkaboo (44/119) ×4 — Psychic-type Night Marcher; different typing covers matchups Joltik can't, and it survives some attacks Joltik dies to. With Dimension Valley, its attack costs less Energy.
  • Lampent (42/119) ×4 — the third Night Marcher; you want all 12 (4 of each) in the discard for maximum damage. Not usually an attacker — it's fuel.
  • Mew (29/124) ×2 — Memories of Dawn: copies Night March while a real Night Marcher stays safe on the bench. Free retreat, single prize, expendable — the safe way to attack.
  • Dimension Valley ×3 — Stadium reducing Psychic attack costs by a Colorless; makes Pumpkaboo/Mew attack for a single DCE (or even free in some lines).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Battle Compressor ×4 — the engine: discard up to 3 Night Marchers (or Supporters for VS Seeker) instantly. This is what makes turn-1 lethal possible.
  • Puzzle of Time ×4 — play 2 together to retrieve any 2 cards — usually DCE, so your 4-DCE limit effectively becomes 8. The card that made Night March nearly disruption-proof.
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Trainers' Mail ×3 / Ultra Ball ×4 — dig and set up the discard; Ultra Ball also pitches Night Marchers.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×3 — Set Up: draw to 6; the burst-draw that assembles a first-turn kill.
  • Sycamore ×3 / N ×1 / Hex Maniac ×2 / Lysandre ×2 / Teammates ×1 / AZ ×1 / Pokémon Ranger ×1 — draw + disruption + utility. Hex Maniac shuts off Abilities (huge vs. Greninja/Trevenant); Pokémon Ranger removes effects (frees you from Giratina's Chaos Wheel / Regice); Lysandre drags up Shaymin-EX for easy prizes.
  • Fighting Fury Belt ×2 — +40 HP (Joltik→70, Pumpkaboo→100) so chip damage (Bats, Oblivion Wing) can't trade with you.
  • Special Charge ×1 / Startling Megaphone ×1 / Escape Rope ×1 / Target Whistle ×1 — recur DCE, strip Tools, switch tricks, and pull a KO'd Pokémon back onto their bench as a Lysandre target.
  • 4 DCE — the only Energy (doubled effectively by Puzzle of Time).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (ideal): Shaymin Set Up → Battle Compressor the Night Marchers into the discard → Ultra Ball/Trainers' Mail to dig → attach DCE → Night March (or Mew's Memories of Dawn) for a huge, often lethal hit. A first-turn KO on an EX is routine.

The core loop

Keep 10–12 Night Marchers in the discard so every swing is 200+. Attack with Mew to keep a real Night Marcher safe on the bench; when Mew dies, promote a fresh Joltik/ Pumpkaboo. Puzzle of Time back your DCE so you never run dry, Lysandre picks off Shaymin-EX for cheap prizes, and Hex Maniac turns off the Abilities of your counters when needed. You out-race everything.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Load the discard fully — damage scales with Night Marchers in the discard; get all 12 down fast.
  • Attack with Mew when you can — it protects your actual attackers and gives up only 1 prize.
  • Belt your attackers vs. chip decks — 70-HP Joltik dodges Oblivion Wing/Bat damage.
  • Hex Maniac is your out to Ability-lock counters (Greninja's Shadow Stitching, Trevenant, Vileplume); Pokémon Ranger answers Chaos Wheel/Regice effects.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Pyroar (Intimidating Mane) — blocks damage from Basics; Joltik/Pumpkaboo can't touch it without Hex Maniac (Mew is Basic too). A hard wall.
  • Item lock (Seismitoad-EX, Trevenant, Vileplume) — shuts off Battle Compressor/ Puzzle/VS Seeker; you get one turn of setup before Toad locks you.
  • Mega Audino — 220 HP wall that feasts on your benched Shaymin-EX and low-HP Basics; a bad matchup that beat Night March at 2016 Worlds.
  • Karen / Bunnelby mill / Yveltal BKT (Fright Night) — resource denial and turning off your Fighting Fury Belt.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Night March: 20 × Night Marchers in the discard (up to ~200, +20 with a Belt on a Marcher isn't damage — Belt adds HP, not damage).
  • Puzzle of Time (×2): retrieve any 2 cards (usually DCE → effectively 8 DCE).
  • Fighting Fury Belt: +40 HP (Joltik 70, Pumpkaboo 100).
  • Setup: turn-1 lethal is realistic off Shaymin + Battle Compressor.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Night March), Limitless (Brad Curcio list), Pokemon.com (States/Worlds analysis), PokéBeach ("Night March in Standard", "Marchbusters"), Retro Library (2016). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
vespiquen_yanmega_2016:
How to Pilot — Vespiquen/Yanmega (2016)

Format: XY Standard 2016. A Grass non-EX toolbox pairing Vespiquen's discard- scaling attack with the energy-free Yanmega BREAK — the archetype that took the 2016 Senior World Championship (Jesper Eriksen).

One-line identity

Two complementary non-EX attackers: Yanmega BREAK hits hard for zero Energy (when your hand has exactly 4 cards) as an early-game rush, and as it trades it feeds the discard for Vespiquen, whose Bee Revenge scales with any Pokémon in the discard for a powerful late game. Cheap, resilient, and hard to disrupt.

The meta it lived in

2016 was Night March's format, and the best answers to Night March (Yveltal, Seismitoad, Greninja, Trevenant) were exactly what Vespiquen was good against — because Bee Revenge scales off any Pokémon (not just Night Marchers) and Vespiquen/ Yanmega have more HP than Joltik/Pumpkaboo, they shrugged off the chip damage that answered Night March. It won Seniors Worlds and put up strong Masters results.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Vespiquen (10/98) ×4 (from Combee ×4) — the late-game closer. Bee Revenge: 20 + 10 × the number of Pokémon in your discard. Any Pokémon counts, so it can exceed Night March's ceiling with a full discard, and it's a Grass type (hits Seismitoad for Weakness). Free retreat.
  • Yanmega (7/114) ×4 (from Yanma ×4) — the early rush. Sonic Vision (Ability): if you have exactly 4 cards in hand, Yanmega (and its BREAK) attack for no Energy. Assault Boom does 50, or 120 if the target has a Tool attached.
  • Yanmega BREAK (8/114) ×3 — 140 HP, keeps Sonic Vision, and adds Barrier Break: 100 damage that ignores all effects on the Defending Pokémon — for zero Energy. Your main early attacker; when it's KO'd, 3 Pokémon hit the discard to pump Bee Revenge.
  • Forest of Giant Plants ×4 — Stadium letting you evolve the turn a Pokémon is played. This is what makes turn-1 Yanmega/Vespiquen possible (skip the wait on Stage 1 → Stage 2).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Bursting Balloon ×4 — a Tool that KOs the attacker when they hit the holder; free damage/deterrent, and it makes opponents hesitate. Reusable pressure.
  • Judge ×2 / N ×2 — draw and hand-size control: Judge sets both hands to 4, which is exactly the count Sonic Vision wants for a free Yanmega attack (while disrupting the opponent).
  • Sycamore ×4 / Lysandre ×1 / AZ ×1 — draw; Lysandre drags up Shaymin-EX/support for a KO; AZ resets a damaged attacker.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Level Ball ×2 / VS Seeker ×4 / Battle Compressor ×2 — search and discard-loading (Ultra Ball/Compressor put Pokémon in the discard to grow Bee Revenge).
  • Revitalizer ×1 / Special Charge ×1 / Super Rod (implied recovery) — recur Grass Pokémon / DCE so you don't run out of attackers or Energy.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×3 — Set Up burst draw (note: it's an EX liability vs. Lysandre, but the draw is worth it).
  • Spinarak/Ariados (1-1) & Klefki (if run) — minor tech; 4 DCE is the only Energy (Yanmega needs none; Vespiquen uses DCE).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: with Forest of Giant Plants, evolve Yanma → Yanmega (→ BREAK). Manage your hand to exactly 4 cards (Judge helps) so Sonic Vision lets Yanmega BREAK Barrier Break for 100, free. Meanwhile discard Pokémon (Ultra Ball) to seed Bee Revenge.

The core loop

Rush with Yanmega BREAK (free Barrier Break, ignores effects) to take early prizes; as your Yanmegas trade, they fill the discard. Transition to Vespiquen mid-to-late game, hitting 100–130+ Bee Revenge to 1HKO EXs. Use Judge to keep your hand at 4 for free attacks (and disrupt), Bursting Balloon for extra damage/deterrent, and Lysandre to snipe Shaymin-EX.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Hand size 4 = free Yanmega attacks — track your hand count; Judge is your lever.
  • Save Vespiquen for the late game — it's your closer once the discard is stacked; don't force it early.
  • Barrier Break ignores effects — it's your out to Glaceon/Regice/effect walls (though Pokémon Ranger backs that up if run).
  • Discard Pokémon actively — Bee Revenge only gets bigger; Ultra Ball/Compressor fuel it.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Ability lock (Garbodor Garbotoxin, Hex Maniac) — turns off Sonic Vision, so Yanmega suddenly needs Energy it doesn't run. A real problem (Lysandre the Trubbish before it evolves).
  • Fire attackers — both key lines are Grass (Fire-Weak); a Volcanion/Fire deck hits hard.
  • Mega Audino — high HP + Magical Symphony snipes your Combee/Shaymin and out-trades Vespiquen.
  • Parallel City / Delinquent — bench-shrinking and Stadium removal hurt your Grass Stadium reliance and setup.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Bee Revenge: 20 + 10 × Pokémon in your discard.
  • Barrier Break (Yanmega BREAK): 100, ignores effects, free with Sonic Vision.
  • Sonic Vision: attack for 0 Energy when your hand is exactly 4 cards.
  • Setup: turn-1 evolve via Forest of Giant Plants.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Vespiquen Box), Pokemon.com (Yanmega BREAK strategy; 2016 Seniors Worlds — Eriksen), PokéBeach (Yanmega/Vespiquen threads), Retro Library (2016). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
alolan_ninetales_2017:
How to Pilot — Alolan Ninetales (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (through Burning Shadows — note the Guzma). A Water toolbox built on Aqua Patch acceleration and Alolan Ninetales-GX.

One-line identity

A flexible Water toolbox: Aqua Patch pours Water Energy from the discard onto your bench, letting Alolan Ninetales-GX fire Ice Blade (a 2-Energy bench snipe) or its bulky attacks while Blizzard Edge and the Ice Path-GX attack lock the game up. Tapu Lele-GX finds any Supporter, and a small tech package covers the field.

The meta it lived in

This is the Burning Shadows-on Standard that led into 2017 Worlds — a slower, Supporter-driven format (VS Seeker still legal but the item-turbo of prior years gone) defined by Gardevoir-GX, Golisopod/Garbodor, Ho-Oh/Salazzle, Metagross-GX, and Zoroark. Alolan Ninetales-GX was a consistent, evasive Water deck that punished the field with snipe damage and free-retreat mobility, with Rough Seas healing to grind out the mirror and Fire matchups.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Alolan Vulpix (21/145) ×4 → Alolan Ninetales-GX (22/145) ×3 — the core. Ice Blade snipes the bench cheaply; Blizzard Edge hits hard for a Water + DCE; the Ice Path-GX attack can shut down damage from your opponent's Pokémon-GX/EX for a turn — a game-swinging wall. Alolan Vulpix's Beacon (search 2 Pokémon) sets up turn 1.
  • Aqua Patch ×4 — the accelerator: attach a basic Water from the discard to a benched Water Pokémon. This is what lets Ninetales attack a turn early and stream.
  • Remoraid (31/162) ×2 → Octillery (33/162) ×2 — Abyssal Hand: draw until you have 5 — the Ability draw engine that keeps the deck flowing without burning Supporters.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag: search any Supporter on the turn it's played (Brigette turn 1, Guzma for the KO, N to disrupt). The consistency glue of the whole format.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tapu Koko (SM30) ×1 — Flying Flip snipes 20 to a benched Pokémon and his Electropace-GX/attack spreads; free retreat pivot that also softens targets for Ice Blade.
  • Sudowoodo (66/145) ×1 — Roadblock: opponents can't play Stage-2 evolutions from hand as easily (a tech vs. Metagross/Decidueye); also a bench-snipe answer.
  • Giratina (XY184) ×1 — its Ability blocks effects of your opponent's Pokémon-GX/EX attacks in some lines (anti-lock tech / disruptive body).
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×3 / Guzma ×2 / Pokémon Center Lady ×1 / Karen ×1 / Brigette ×1 / Professor Kukui ×1 — the Supporter suite. Guzma drags up a target and switches (the format's premier gust); Karen shuffles an opponent's discarded Pokémon back (anti-Vespiquen/Night March); Pokémon Center Lady heals 60 + clears status; Brigette sets up your evolutions turn 1.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×3 / Field Blower ×1 — search, Supporter recursion, Tool/ Stadium removal (answers Garbodor/Choice Band).
  • Choice Band ×3 / Float Stone ×2 / Rough Seas ×3 — +30 vs. GX/EX to hit KO numbers, free retreat, and heal 30 off your Water Pokémon each turn.
  • 7 Water + 4 DCE — Water feeds Aqua Patch/attacks; DCE powers Blizzard Edge and off-costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Alolan Vulpix Beacon (or Brigette via Tapu Lele) to grab your line, bench Remoraid, drop Water so Aqua Patch is live.
  • Turn 2: evolve to Alolan Ninetales-GX, Aqua Patch onto the bench, and start Ice Blade sniping or set up a Blizzard Edge.

The core loop

Accelerate with Aqua Patch, snipe with Ice Blade / hit hard with Blizzard Edge, and use Guzma to pull up the Pokémon you want to KO. Drop Ice Path-GX at the key moment to blank a turn of GX/EX damage. Rough Seas + free retreat keeps your board healthy; Octillery keeps you drawing; Choice Band pushes attacks over GX KO lines.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Turn-2 Aqua Patch is the tempo goal — it's what makes Ninetales attack early and stream.
  • Save Ice Path-GX for a turn that blanks a big attacker (Gardevoir, Ho-Oh) — timing it well can win the game outright.
  • Guzma is your reach — the snipe + gust combination sets up KOs the opponent can't protect against.
  • Field Blower the Garbodor / Choice Band when it matters; don't let Garbotoxin sit.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Lightning attackers — Alolan Ninetales-GX is Lightning-Weak (Tapu Koko-GX/Vikavolt punish it).
  • Gardevoir-GX — out-tanks and out-scales you late (Max Potion + huge Infinite Force); a grind you can lose.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Octillery/Tapu Lele draw; lean on Field Blower and Supporters.
  • Decidueye-GX — Feather Arrow chip + Grass pressure can outpace the snipe game.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Aqua Patch: basic Water from discard → benched Water Pokémon (Item, multiple/turn).
  • Ice Blade: cheap bench snipe; Blizzard Edge: heavy hit for Water + DCE.
  • Ice Path-GX: prevents damage from opponent's GX/EX for a turn (once-per-game GX).
  • Choice Band: +30 vs. GX/EX; Rough Seas: heal 30 off Water Pokémon.

Sources: PokéBeach & SixPrizes 2017 Standard analyses, Pokemon.com (Alolan Ninetales-GX strategy), Bulbapedia (Alolan Ninetales-GX). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
decidueye_golisopod_2017:
How to Pilot — Decidueye/Golisopod (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (Burning Shadows — note Guzma/Acerola). A Grass toolbox pairing Decidueye-GX's snipe with Golisopod-GX's efficient First Impression.

One-line identity

Two Grass attackers that cover each other: Decidueye-GX's Feather Arrow puts free damage on any Pokémon every turn (an Ability, not an attack), softening the board, while Golisopod-GX swings for a huge First Impression (120 for one Energy) each time it comes into the Active spot. Snipe with the owl, hit hard with the bug, and recycle with Acerola.

The meta it lived in

Late-2017 Standard was defined by Gardevoir-GX, Golisopod/Garbodor, Zoroark, Metagross, and Ho-Oh. Decidueye/Golisopod thrived because Feather Arrow ignores the format's high HP — it stacks damage the opponent can't heal off fast enough — and Golisopod's First Impression is the most Energy-efficient big hit in the game. Forest of Giant Plants made the multi-Stage-2 setup fast enough to compete.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Rowlet (9/149) ×3 → Dartrix (10/149) ×3 → Decidueye-GX (12/149) ×3 — the snipe engine. Feather Arrow (Ability): once per turn, put 2 damage counters on any opposing Pokémon — no Energy, no attack needed. Multiple Decidueye = multiple arrows per turn. Feather Arrow + Guzma/First Impression sets up KOs.
  • Wimpod (16/147) ×3 → Golisopod-GX (17/147) ×3 — the hitter. First Impression: 120 for a single Grass Energy, but only the turn Golisopod becomes Active. So you must move it into the Active spot each turn (Guzma, Acerola, Float Stone) to re-trigger it.
  • Forest of Giant Plants ×4 — Stadium: evolve the turn a Pokémon is played. Essential to get two Stage-2 lines (Decidueye) online quickly.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag: search any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, Acerola) the turn it's played.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tapu Koko (SM30) ×2 — Flying Flip snipe + a free-retreat body to promote so Golisopod can be Guzma'd/Acerola'd back for another First Impression; also softens targets alongside Feather Arrow.
  • Espeon-EX (52/122) ×1 — Miraculous Shine: devolve the opponent's Pokémon (wreck a Metagross/Gardevoir setup) — a powerful disruption tech.
  • Sudowoodo (66/145) ×1 — anti-Stage-2 / bench tech.
  • Guzma ×2 / Acerola ×2 — the two ways to reset First Impression: Guzma pulls Golisopod Active (and gusts their target), Acerola picks Golisopod up to hand (healing it and re-arming First Impression next drop). Acerola also denies prizes.
  • Sycamore ×3 / N ×3 / Brigette ×1 — draw + turn-1 setup.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×3 / Revitalizer ×2 / Rescue Stretcher (via Revitalizer role) — search, Supporter recursion, and Revitalizer to get Grass Stage-1/2s back from the discard.
  • Field Blower ×2 / Choice Band ×3 / Float Stone ×2 — remove Garbodor's Tool/Parallel City, push First Impression to 150 (KOs 150-HP GX), and free-retreat to reset Golisopod.
  • 5 Grass + 3 DCE — Grass powers both attackers; DCE for flexibility.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Rowlet(s), Wimpod, and a Tapu Koko; drop Forest of Giant Plants. Ideal: two Wimpod + a promotable body so turn 2 you can First Impression immediately.
  • Turn 2: evolve Decidueye online (Feather Arrow starts), and First Impression for 120 (150 with Choice Band).

The core loop

Every turn: use Feather Arrow from each Decidueye to place free damage, then move a loaded Golisopod-GX Active (Guzma/Acerola/Float Stone) for a First Impression. Arrows + First Impression combine to KO things a plain 120 couldn't. Acerola recycles a damaged Golisopod (heal + re-arm); Guzma picks the target. You grind the board down from two angles.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Move Golisopod Active every turn — First Impression only hits 120 the turn it arrives; a Golisopod that sat there does 30. Guzma/Acerola/Float Stone are non- negotiable enablers.
  • Aim Feather Arrows to set up KOs — 2 counters/turn/owl adds up; place them where First Impression or Tapu Koko finishes the job.
  • Acerola = heal + reload — its dual purpose (deny a prize, re-arm First Impression) is the deck's engine; don't waste it.
  • Field Blower vs. Garbodor/Parallel City — protect your Abilities and Stadium.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire (Ho-Oh/Salazzle, Volcanion) — both your attackers are Grass (Fire-Weak); a fast Fire deck races you.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Feather Arrow (half your damage) and Tapu Lele; lean on Field Blower.
  • Gardevoir-GX — out-heals/out-scales; you need arrows + Choice Band math to keep up.
  • Slow starts — a multi-Stage-2 deck; a Forest-less or Brigette-whiff open falls behind.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Feather Arrow: 2 counters on any Pokémon, per Decidueye, per turn (Ability).
  • First Impression: 120 for 1 Grass, only the turn Golisopod becomes Active (150 w/ Choice Band).
  • Setup: turn-1 Forest of Giant Plants + Brigette.
  • Reset tools: Guzma / Acerola / Float Stone to re-arm First Impression.

Sources: Pokemon.com (Decks to Watch, Worlds 2017), SixPrizes / PokéBeach Decidueye/Golisopod analyses, Bulbapedia. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
gardevoir_gx_2017:
How to Pilot — Gardevoir-GX (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (Burning Shadows). "Infinite Force" — this build mirrors Diego Cassiraga's 2017 World Championship-winning list.

One-line identity

The premier attrition deck: Gardevoir-GX's Infinite Force does 30 × the total Energy on both Active Pokémon, so it scales into a 1HKO of anything, while Secret Spring (Gardevoir-GX's Ability) accelerates Fairy Energy by hand every turn. Pair it with Gallade as a Fighting attacker and Max Potion to erase damage, and you out-grind the entire format.

The meta it lived in

Gardevoir-GX was the hyped BDIF going into 2017 Worlds and delivered — it won the whole event. In a Supporter-driven, slower format (VS Seeker still legal, but no item-turbo), its combination of Energy acceleration, scaling damage, healing, and Fairy typing (resisting the popular Dark decks) made it the deck to beat. Its main predators were the things that could 1HKO it or ignore its bulk: Fire (Ho-Oh/Salazzle) and Metal.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Ralts (91/147) ×4 → Kirlia (92/147) ×3 → Gardevoir-GX (93/147) ×3 — the core. Secret Spring (Ability): attach a Fairy Energy from hand to a Pokémon each turn (on top of your normal attachment) — free, stacking acceleration. Infinite Force: 30 × all Energy on both Active Pokémon — scales to 1HKO territory. Twilight-GX (the GX attack) shuffles 10 cards from your discard back into the deck (recur Energy/Pokémon, and answer Trashalanche).
  • Gallade (84/162) ×1 (evolves from Kirlia) — a Fighting attacker (Sensitive Blade hits harder as you take damage / Premonition to set up); covers matchups Gardevoir can't, and hits the Metal/Colorless-weak Pokémon that wall Gardevoir.
  • Rare Candy ×3 — jump Ralts → Gardevoir-GX a turn faster (skip Kirlia); speed is everything for the setup.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter on demand (Brigette, N, Guzma, Acerola). Also a body that benefits from Fairy acceleration.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Remoraid (31/162) ×2 → Octillery (33/162) ×1 — Abyssal Hand draw engine, so you aren't wholly Supporter-reliant.
  • Diancie (94/147) ×1 — Princess's Curtain-style boost: raises your Fairy Pokémon's damage (+20 to Fairy attackers), nudging Infinite Force over KO lines.
  • Alolan Vulpix (21/145) ×1 — Beacon search / a splashable body.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×3 / Guzma ×2 / Brigette ×1 / Hex Maniac ×1 / Acerola ×1 — the Supporter suite. Guzma gusts + switches; Hex Maniac shuts off the opponent's Abilities for a turn (huge vs. Golisopod's setup, Metagross, etc.); Acerola picks up a damaged Gardevoir to reset it (heal + replay).
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Field Blower ×2 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 / Super Rod ×1 — Supporter recursion, search, Tool/Stadium removal, recovery.
  • Choice Band ×2 — +30 vs. GX/EX to hit exact KO numbers.
  • 7 Fairy + 4 DCE + 1 Wonder Energy — Fairy feeds Secret Spring/Infinite Force; DCE bulks the count fast; Wonder Energy protects the holder from GX/EX attack effects.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Ralts ×2–3; drop a Fairy. Bench Remoraid.
  • Turn 2: Rare Candy → Gardevoir-GX, use Secret Spring + your attachment to bank Energy, and start attacking / building toward a big Infinite Force.

The core loop

Every turn: Secret Spring an extra Fairy (stacking Energy on Gardevoir), attack with Infinite Force (which grows as your Energy grows and as they attach), and Max Potion-style reset via Acerola / heal to undo their progress. Use Guzma to pull up the KO you want; Twilight-GX to recycle Energy in the late game so you never run dry. You win the attrition war — they can't KO you fast enough while your damage only climbs.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Turn-2 Gardevoir (Rare Candy) is the goal — Secret Spring needs to start ASAP.
  • Bank Energy across turns — Infinite Force scales with total Energy; a fully-loaded Gardevoir 1HKOs anything. But don't over-load one target into an Espeon-GX Psychic.
  • Acerola to reset damaged Gardevoirs (heal + re-load) — it's your Max-Potion-style attrition.
  • Hex Maniac / Guzma are your tempo tools — deny their setup or pull the exact KO.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire (Ho-Oh/Salazzle, Turtonator) — Gardevoir is Metal-Weak and Fire hits hard/ fast; Ho-Oh can 1HKO before you stabilize. A real problem.
  • Espeon-GX (Psychic) — punishes over-loaded Gardevoirs by KO'ing them off their own Energy; spread your attachments.
  • Metal / Cobalion — Metal attackers exploit the Weakness.
  • Fast 1HKO decks — anything that can consistently one-shot a Gardevoir before Secret Spring snowballs.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Infinite Force: 30 × total Energy on both Active Pokémon.
  • Secret Spring: +1 Fairy attachment per turn (stacks with normal attach).
  • Twilight-GX: shuffle 10 cards from discard into the deck (once-per-game GX).
  • Setup: turn-2 Gardevoir via Rare Candy; Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Infinite Force — Cassiraga 1st 2017 Worlds), Pokemon.com & Pokémon Center (Cassiraga list), SixPrizes ("Twilight Infinity"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
golisopod_garbodor_2017:
How to Pilot — Golisopod/Garbodor (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (Burning Shadows). "Golisodor" — this build mirrors Naoto Suzuki's 2017 World Championship finalist list.

One-line identity

An efficient two-pronged deck: Golisopod-GX swings for First Impression (120 for one Grass) every time it re-enters the Active spot, while Garbodor provides both a punishing attacker (Trashalanche scales with the opponent's Items in the discard) and a disruptive Ability (Garbotoxin shuts off all Abilities). Cheap, consistent, and hard to play around.

The meta it lived in

Late-2017 Standard rewarded efficiency and disruption. Golisodor delivered both: First Impression is the most Energy-efficient big hit in the game, and Garbodor punishes the Item-reliant decks while Garbotoxin cripples Ability-based ones (Gardevoir's Secret Spring, Metagross, Zoroark's Trade). It reached the Masters final at Worlds. Its edge over pure Golisopod was the split Garbodor line — two different Garbodors for two jobs.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Wimpod (16/147) ×4 → Golisopod-GX (17/147) ×3 — the hitter. First Impression: 120 for a single Grass, only the turn Golisopod becomes Active. You must move it in each turn (Guzma, Acerola, Float Stone) to re-trigger it. Armor Press for a smaller, defensive hit when you can't reset.
  • Trubbish (56/122) ×4 → Garbodor — the split line:
  • Garbodor (57/122, "Trashalanche") ×2 — Trashalanche: 20 × the number of Item cards in the opponent's discard. In an Item-lighter format it's situational, but against Item-heavy decks it 1HKOs.
  • Garbodor (51/145, "Garbotoxin") ×2 — Garbotoxin (with a Tool attached): all Abilities off, both players. The disruption that beats Gardevoir/Metagross/Zoroark.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag for any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, Acerola).
  • Float Stone ×4 — free retreat: reset Golisopod's First Impression and give Garbotoxin Garbodor its required Tool + mobility.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tapu Koko (SM30) ×1 — Flying Flip snipe + a free-retreat promote body so Golisopod can be reset each turn.
  • Guzma ×2 / Acerola ×2 — the reset engine: Guzma pulls Golisopod Active and gusts the target; Acerola picks Golisopod up (heal + re-arm First Impression) and denies a prize.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×3 / Brigette ×1 / Teammates ×1 / Hex Maniac ×1 — draw + setup. Teammates (after a KO) fetches two cards; Hex Maniac is a second Ability-lock angle when Garbotoxin isn't set.
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Rescue Stretcher ×2 / Heavy Ball ×1 / Field Blower ×1 — Supporter recursion, search, recover Golisopod/Garbodor, and remove opposing Tools/ your own (to toggle Garbotoxin off when you need an Ability).
  • Choice Band ×2 — First Impression to 150 (KOs 150-HP GX) / Trashalanche math.
  • 4 Rainbow + 3 Grass + 3 DCE — Rainbow enables Trashalanche's Psychic cost and off- types; Grass powers First Impression; DCE flexibility.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette for Wimpod ×2 + Tapu Koko (ideal), or bench Trubbish. Get a promotable body so turn 2 you First Impression immediately.
  • Turn 2: First Impression for 120 (150 with Choice Band); start shaping whether you want Trashalanche or Garbotoxin online based on the matchup.

The core loop

Move a loaded Golisopod-GX Active each turn (Guzma/Acerola/Float Stone) for First Impression, using Acerola to heal + re-arm and Guzma to pick the KO. Decide per matchup: set up Garbotoxin (vs. Ability decks) or hold Trashalanche as a finisher (vs. Item decks). You take efficient 2HKOs and trade favorably with Acerola resets.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Reset Golisopod every turn — First Impression is 120 only on entry; never let it sit and do 30.
  • Pick your Garbodor — Garbotoxin vs. Gardevoir/Metagross/Zoroark; Trashalanche vs. Item-heavy decks. The split gives you the choice — use the right one.
  • Field Blower toggles your own Garbotoxin off when you need Abilities (rare, but matters).
  • Acerola trades up — heal + re-arm + prize denial is the deck's grind engine.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire (Ho-Oh/Salazzle) — Golisopod is Grass/Fire-Weak; fast Fire races you.
  • Gardevoir-GX — can out-scale even through chip; you lean on Garbotoxin + Choice Band math and hope to trade with Acerola.
  • Big Basic 1HKO decks — anything that consistently one-shots Golisopod before you reset.
  • Field Blower decks — opponents removing your Garbotoxin Tool blunts the disruption.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • First Impression: 120 for 1 Grass, only on entering Active (150 w/ Choice Band).
  • Trashalanche: 20 × opponent's Items in the discard.
  • Garbotoxin: all Abilities off while Garbodor holds a Tool.
  • Reset tools: Guzma / Acerola / Float Stone.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Golisodor — Suzuki 2nd 2017 Worlds), Pokémon Center (Suzuki list), PokéBeach ("Go Go Golisopod"), SixPrizes ("Bug Catching"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
ho_oh_salazzle_2017:
How to Pilot — Ho-Oh Salazzle (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (Burning Shadows). A fast Fire deck; first played at 2017 Worlds by Takuya Yoneda (started 6-0, finished 9th).

One-line identity

Explosive Fire aggression: Kiawe attaches 4 Fire Energy in one Supporter (your turn ends), instantly powering Ho-Oh-GX's Phoenix Burn for 180 — a turn-1/2 haymaker. Take early prizes with Ho-Oh, then close with Salazzle-GX's Diabolical Claws, which scales with the prizes you've already taken. Simple, fast, and punishing.

The meta it lived in

Post-rotation Standard slowed down — which made a fast, aggressive Fire deck valuable. Ho-Oh/Salazzle exploited the metagame's high-HP GX Pokémon (many Fire-Weak) with big, early Phoenix Burns, and it was especially strong into Metagross-GX and Zoroark while beating many rogues. It's meta-dependent (Energy-acceleration decks and Gardevoir can outpace it), but in the right field it races anything.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Ho-Oh-GX (21/147) ×3 — the main attacker. Phoenix Burn: 180 damage (discard all Energy on it) — but Kiawe reloads it. Sacred Fire: attach Fire from the discard, usable to follow up. Rebirth-GX heals/recovers. Note: Phoenix Burn can't be used the turn you play Kiawe on the same Ho-Oh unless set up right — plan attachments.
  • Kiawe ×3 — the engine: attach 4 basic Fire from the deck to 1 Pokémon, then your turn ends. Perfect for Phoenix Burn's cost; ideally played turn 1 (you can't attack first turn anyway).
  • Salandit (15/145) ×2 → Salazzle-GX (25/147) ×2 — the closer. Diabolical Claws: damage scales with the number of prizes you've taken (huge after Ho-Oh takes 3–4). Cheap 2-Energy cost. Callous Comet-GX for a big finish.
  • Volcanion-EX (26/114) ×2 + Volcanion (25/114) ×1 — Steam Up (Volcanion-EX Ability): discard a Fire to give a Fire attacker +30 this turn (stackable) — pushes Ho-Oh to 1HKO numbers. Baby Volcanion (Power Heater) accelerates Fire to the bench.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Turtonator-GX (18/145) ×1 — Nitro Tank-GX: refuel your attackers from the discard mid-game (answers Phoenix Burn's self-discard); a secondary attacker.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: grab Kiawe turn 1 (the whole plan); also N/ Guzma.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×1 — Set Up burst draw to find the Ho-Oh + Kiawe opener.
  • Sudowoodo (66/145) ×1 — tech vs. Mega Rayquaza / bench-snipe.
  • Sycamore ×4 / Guzma ×3 / N ×2 — draw + gust. Guzma pulls up a benched target for a Phoenix Burn KO (and switches your own to reset). Note the higher Guzma / lower N: this is a fast deck that doesn't want to N itself off a lead.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×3 / Switch ×2 / Max Elixir ×2 / Super Rod ×2 / Float Stone ×1 — search, recursion, reset attackers, extra Fire acceleration, and recover Energy/ Pokémon (crucial with all the discarding).
  • Choice Band ×4 — +30 vs. GX/EX; Phoenix Burn 180 + Band = 210 (1HKOs Gardevoir-GX).
  • 14 Fire — the whole deck runs on basic Fire (Kiawe, Sacred Fire, Steam Up, Max Elixir all want a lot).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Ho-Oh-GX active, Wonder Tag (Tapu Lele) → Kiawe to dump 4 Fire onto Ho-Oh (turn ends). Bench a Salandit with an Energy so Salazzle is a threat later.
  • Turn 2: Phoenix Burn for 180 (210 with Choice Band / Steam Up) — an early 2-prize KO.

The core loop

Kiawe → Phoenix Burn for 180 → next turn, Guzma the target and either Sacred Fire- reload another Ho-Oh or fire the next Phoenix Burn; use Steam Up to hit exact 1HKO numbers. Once you're up ~3–4 prizes, swing with Salazzle-GX's Diabolical Claws for a massive, cheap finish. Turtonator/Super Rod refuel so the self-discard never stalls you.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Turn-1 Kiawe is the dream — map your attachments so Phoenix Burn comes online turn 2; you often plan several turns ahead.
  • Don't wait to arm Salandit — put an Energy on it early so Salazzle is ready to close; whiffing the Energy hands the opponent a turn.
  • Steam Up to exact KO numbers — 180 → 210 lets Ho-Oh 1HKO Gardevoir/big GX.
  • Manage Fire in the discard — Turtonator/Super Rod recycle; you can run out if careless.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Water attackers — Ho-Oh/Salazzle/Volcanion are Fire (Water-Weak); Alolan Ninetales/ Water decks punish you.
  • Energy-acceleration / attrition decks (Gardevoir-GX) — can out-ramp and out-heal your burst; a documented rough matchup.
  • Getting N'd off a lead — as a fast deck, a well-timed N to a small hand with no Kiawe/ Ho-Oh can strand you.
  • Parallel City / damage reduction — cuts your Fire damage below KO lines.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Phoenix Burn: 180 (discard all Energy on Ho-Oh) → 210 with Choice Band.
  • Kiawe: attach 4 basic Fire from the deck to 1 Pokémon; your turn ends.
  • Diabolical Claws (Salazzle-GX): scales with prizes you've taken (2 Fire cost).
  • Steam Up: +30 to a Fire attacker per use (discard a Fire), stackable.

Sources: PokéBeach ("Fire in the Sky"), Parallel TCG ("The Phoenix and the Queen"), Pokemon.com 2017 Worlds coverage (Yoneda). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
mega_rayquaza_2017:
How to Pilot — Mega Rayquaza (2017)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard, late 2017 / Worlds era (Burning Shadows — note Guzma/ Tapu Lele-GX). A last-hurrah build of the M Rayquaza-EX turbo deck before it rotated.

One-line identity

Fast, explosive tempo: Sky Field expands your bench to 8, and M Rayquaza-EX's Emerald Break does 30 × your benched Pokémon — up to 240 for a single DCE. Flood the bench with Shaymin-EX / Hoopa-EX draw engines, Mega-evolve for free with Rayquaza Spirit Link, and 1HKO the format from turn 2.

The meta it lived in

This is Mega Rayquaza's final Standard season (it rotated after 2017). It was still a raw-power tempo deck that punished slow starts, but the metagame had adapted: Parallel City (shrinks your bench to 3, gutting Emerald Break) and Garbodor Garbotoxin (shuts off Shaymin/Hoopa/Dragonite) were everywhere, which is why this list packs Field Blower and Hex Maniac to fight through the hate.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Rayquaza-EX (75/108) ×4 → M Rayquaza-EX (76/108) ×3 — the core. Emerald Break: 30 × your benched Pokémon (8 bench under Sky Field = 240) for a single DCE. It's a Mega (evolving normally ends your turn — Spirit Link fixes that).
  • Rayquaza Spirit Link ×2 — Mega-evolve without ending your turn — essential to evolve and attack the same turn.
  • Sky Field ×4 — Stadium: 8-Pokémon bench (vs. the normal 5). More bench = more Emerald Break damage and more room for draw engines. You want it down and to win the Stadium war (hence Field Blower).
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×4 — Set Up: draw to 6. Both a draw engine and a benched body that adds 30 to Emerald Break. The heart of the turbo plan.
  • Hoopa-EX (36/98) ×2 — Scoundrel Ring: pull 3 EXs (Rayquaza/Shaymin) from the deck — instant bench-flood + setup.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag for any Supporter (N, Guzma, Skyla); also a bench body.
  • Tapu Koko-GX (47/145) ×1 + Tapu Koko (SM30) ×1 — Lightning attackers/Flying Flip snipe; Tapu Koko-GX's Aerial Ace/GX gives a second angle and free-retreat body.
  • Sudowoodo (66/145) ×1 — bench-snipe / anti-Stage-2 tech.
  • Sycamore ×3 / Guzma ×2 / Hex Maniac ×2 / N ×1 / Skyla ×1 / Pokémon Fan Club ×1 — draw
  • disruption. Hex Maniac turns off the opponent's Abilities (protect your combo turn/ answer their engine); Guzma gusts the KO; Fan Club bench-floods Basics.
  • VS Seeker ×3 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Mega Turbo ×3 / Field Blower ×2 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 / Float Stone ×2 — recursion, search, Mega Turbo (accelerate Energy onto Rayquaza from the discard), remove Parallel City/Garbodor's Tool, recover, free retreat.
  • Choice Band ×2 — +30 vs. GX/EX to top off KO numbers.
  • 5 Lightning + 4 DCE — DCE powers Emerald Break in one attach; Lightning for Tapu Koko/off-costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Sky Field down, Shaymin Set Up and Hoopa Scoundrel Ring to flood the bench, get a Rayquaza-EX active.
  • Turn 2: Spirit Link → M Rayquaza-EX (turn doesn't end), attach DCE (or Mega Turbo), and Emerald Break for 180–240 — an immediate 1HKO.

The core loop

Keep the bench full (Shaymin/Hoopa/Tapu) under Sky Field, Mega-evolve free with Spirit Link, and Emerald Break for a 1HKO every turn. Mega Turbo re-arms a fresh Rayquaza after one is KO'd; Guzma picks the target; Hex Maniac / Field Blower clear the Ability-lock and Parallel City that try to shrink your damage. Race them before they set up.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Protect Sky Field — Field Blower/extra Sky Field to win the Stadium war; Parallel City down = your damage collapses.
  • Full bench = lethal — 6–8 benched Pokémon is the difference between 180 and 240; don't under-develop.
  • Hex Maniac through Garbotoxin — if Garbodor is up, Hex/Field Blower restores your Shaymin/Hoopa draw for the key turn.
  • Mind the Shaymin liability — a full bench of EXs means Guzma/Emerald-Break-back can punish you; sequence so you're not handing easy prizes.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Parallel City — the defining counter: bench to 3 = Emerald Break does ~90. You must answer it.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — turns off Shaymin/Hoopa/Dragonite; your setup engine dies without Hex/Blower.
  • Lightning / weakness hits — M Rayquaza is Lightning-Weak (Tapu Koko-GX, Vikavolt 1HKO it).
  • Fast disruption / N to small hands — a turbo deck that bricks if its draw engines are denied.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Emerald Break: 30 × your benched Pokémon (8 = 240) for 1 DCE.
  • Sky Field: 8-Pokémon bench.
  • Spirit Link: Mega-evolve without ending your turn.
  • Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX; Mega Turbo: Energy from discard → Rayquaza.

Sources: Pokemon.com (Oceania/Worlds "Decks to Watch" M Rayquaza notes), Retro/XY deck references, PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
decidueye_2017_naic:
How to Pilot — Decidueye (2017 NAIC)

Format: PRC–GRI Standard (June 2017, the North American International Championships meta — pre-Burning Shadows, so Lysandre not Guzma, no Golisopod/Gardevoir-GX). A straight Decidueye-GX build with an Alolan Ninetales-GX support splash.

One-line identity

A control-toolbox: Decidueye-GX's Feather Arrow puts 2 free damage counters on any Pokémon every turn (an Ability), so you grind the whole board down without attacking, while Alolan Ninetales-GX and Tapu Koko add snipe and reach. Lysandre drags up the target you want to finish. Death by a thousand arrows.

The meta it lived in

NAIC 2017 was the PRC-GRI format, right after the Guardians Rising release. The field was Vespiquen, Metagross-GX, Garbodor variants (Espeon/Garb, Drampa/Garb), Greninja BREAK, Volcanion, Zoroark/Drampa, and Decidueye builds. Decidueye was a proven contender (Top-tier results at Internationals from John Kettler and Igor Costa). Crucially, this is the pre-Guzma format — your only gust is Lysandre, so setting up KOs is slower and more deliberate than the later Golisopod-era decks.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Rowlet (9/149) ×4 → Dartrix (10/149) ×4 → Decidueye-GX (12/149) ×4 — the core. Feather Arrow (Ability): once per turn per Decidueye, 2 counters on any opposing Pokémon — free board control. Hollow Hunt-GX recovers cards from the discard. You run the full 4-4-4 because arrows-per-turn is the deck.
  • Forest of Giant Plants ×4 — Stadium: evolve the turn a Pokémon is played — essential to get multiple Decidueye-GX (a Stage 2) online fast.
  • Alolan Vulpix (21/145) ×2 → Alolan Ninetales-GX (22/145) ×1 — support attacker: Beacon searches Pokémon, and Ninetales-GX's snipe/Ice Path-GX adds reach and a defensive GX wall.
  • Tapu Koko (SM30) ×2 — Flying Flip snipe + free-retreat pivot; combines with Feather Arrow to reach KO numbers.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Espeon-EX (52/122) ×1 — Miraculous Shine: devolve the opponent's Pokémon — wrecks a Metagross/evolving setup and combos with arrows for KOs.
  • Shaymin-EX (77/108) ×1 — Set Up burst draw (still legal this format).
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, N, Lysandre).
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×2 / Lysandre ×1 / Skyla ×1 / Delinquent ×1 — draw + control. Lysandre is your only gust (pre-Guzma) — save it for the KO that matters. Delinquent discards from the opponent's hand + removes their Stadium (disruption, and wins the Stadium war for your Forest).
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×4 / Level Ball ×2 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 / Revitalizer ×1 — search, Supporter recursion (VS Seeker still legal — get Lysandre back!), and recover Grass evolutions.
  • Field Blower ×2 / Choice Band ×2 / Float Stone ×2 — remove Garbodor's Tool/Parallel City, push snipes over KO lines, free retreat.
  • 4 Grass + 4 DCE — Grass for attack costs; DCE for flexible/Colorless attacks.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Rowlets; drop Forest of Giant Plants.
  • Turn 2: evolve one or two Decidueye-GX — the moment they're up, Feather Arrow starts stacking free damage every turn.

The core loop

Every turn: fire Feather Arrow from each Decidueye (2 counters each), add Tapu Koko snipes, and let the damage pile up across the opponent's board. Use Lysandre to pull a softened Pokémon Active and finish it; Espeon-EX to devolve a threat; Ninetales-GX for a bigger swing or Ice Path-GX wall. You control the pace and take prizes off arrows the opponent can't heal fast enough.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Get multiple Decidueye online — more owls = more arrows/turn = faster board control. Forest + Brigette is the priority.
  • Lysandre is precious (pre-Guzma) — you have one reliable gust; VS Seeker it back and time it for the game-deciding KO.
  • Aim arrows to set up KOs — pre-place counters so a Koko snipe or Lysandre'd attacker dies without a big attack.
  • Field Blower the Garbodor — Garbotoxin turns off Feather Arrow (your whole engine).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire (Volcanion) — Grass attackers are Fire-Weak; Volcanion's Power Heater + Steam Up pressures you fast.
  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) — shuts off Feather Arrow; without it you have very little damage. Field Blower is mandatory.
  • Fast, aggressive decks — this is a slower, pre-Guzma control build; decks that race (Volcanion, Drampa/Zoroark) can outpace the arrows.
  • Vespiquen — non-EX attackers that trade prizes efficiently and don't mind arrows can be awkward.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Feather Arrow: 2 counters on any Pokémon, per Decidueye, per turn (Ability).
  • Setup: turn-1 Forest of Giant Plants + Brigette → turn-2 Decidueye-GX.
  • Gust: Lysandre only (this is the pre-Guzma format) — recur with VS Seeker.
  • Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX; Espeon-EX devolves a threat.

Sources: Pokemon.com (2017 NAIC decks/analysis), SixPrizes / PokéBeach Decidueye-GX articles, Bulbapedia. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
espeon_garbodor_2017_naic:
How to Pilot — Espeon Garbodor (2017 NAIC)

Format: PRC–GRI Standard (June 2017 NAIC meta — pre-Burning Shadows, Lysandre not Guzma). A Psychic disruption-toolbox that was a genuine NAIC-era deck — and Vig's favourite deck of this format.

One-line identity

A punishing control deck: Garbodor's Trashalanche does 20 × the opponent's Items in the discard (a 1HKO against Item-reliant decks), while Espeon-GX's Psybeam confuses and its Psychic attack scales with the Energy the opponent has attached — so the deck punishes both Items and Energy. Garbotoxin shuts off Abilities on the side. You make every one of the opponent's plays cost them.

The meta it lived in

NAIC 2017 (PRC-GRI) was full of Item-heavy and Ability-heavy decks — exactly what this deck preys on. Trashalanche punishes the Item turbo (Ultra Ball, VS Seeker, Float Stone), Psychic punishes Energy-loaders, and Garbotoxin cripples Ability engines. It put up strong results (a writer's own NAIC deck; Top-4 Mexico City Regionals). Its Achilles' heel: Gardevoir wasn't in this format yet, but Darkrai (Psychic-resistant) and Greninja were rough.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Eevee (63/98) ×4 → Espeon-GX (61/149) ×3 — the attacker. Energy Evolution (Eevee): when you attach a Psychic to Eevee, evolve it instantly into Espeon-GX — a free tempo jump. Psybeam: 20 + Confusion (buys turns, forces awkward retreats). Psychic: scales with the opponent's attached Energy — 1HKOs over-loaded Pokémon. Divide-GX spreads damage for multi-KO turns.
  • Trubbish (56/122) ×4 → Garbodor — the split line:
  • Garbodor (51/145, "Trashalanche") ×3 — Trashalanche: 20 × opponent's Items in the discard; the main non-EX attacker (120 HP, trades great).
  • Garbodor (57/122, "Garbotoxin") ×1 — Garbotoxin (with a Tool): all Abilities off.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Lysandre, N).
  • Flareon (13/98) ×1 (from Eevee) — a Grass/Fire tech attacker (Flare Blitz-style) that forces certain matchups (e.g. Vespiquen) to change plans; also punishes evolving decks.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×4 / Lysandre ×3 / Hex Maniac ×2 / Brigette ×1 — a heavy control Supporter suite. Lysandre (×3, your only gust pre-Guzma) drags up a target to Psychic/ Trashalanche; Hex Maniac shuts off Abilities for a turn; N disrupts.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×3 / Float Stone ×3 / Field Blower ×1 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 — search, recursion (VS Seeker → more Lysandre), Float Stone (free retreat and the Tool for Garbotoxin), Tool/Stadium removal, recovery.
  • Choice Band ×3 — +30 vs. GX/EX so Psybeam/Psychic and even Trashalanche hit exact KO numbers.
  • 8 Psychic + 4 DCE — a high Psychic count (8) is deliberate: it maximizes the turn-1 Psybeam and keeps Energy Evolution live; DCE powers Trashalanche/Psychic's Colorless.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Eevee + Trubbish; ideally a Psybeam off Energy Evolution to confuse and start chipping. Bench a Trubbish toward Garbodor.
  • Early turns: decide the plan — set up Garbotoxin vs. Ability decks, or hold Trashalanche as the finisher vs. Item decks, while Psybeam stalls.

The core loop

Chip and disrupt: Psybeam (Confusion) to slow the Active, Psychic to punish an over-loaded attacker, Trashalanche to blow up an Item-heavy opponent, and Garbotoxin to lock Abilities. Lysandre pulls the exact target; Divide-GX takes a multi-prize turn when the board is spread. Every Item/Energy the opponent commits feeds your damage.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Hold your Energy back — attach Psychic before DCE, and spread attachments so an opposing Espeon-GX's Psychic can't punish you; Psychic scales off their Energy, so bait them into over-committing.
  • Pick the right Garbodor — Garbotoxin vs. Ability decks, Trashalanche vs. Item decks.
  • Lysandre is your only gust — VS Seeker it back; time it for the KO that wins.
  • Field Blower / remove your own Float Stone to toggle Garbotoxin when you need it (or off when you need Abilities).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Darkrai — Dark Pokémon resist Psychic; a documented 40-60 (or worse) matchup.
  • Greninja BREAK — plays "solitaire," ignores your disruption, and out-grinds you; tied for the worst matchup.
  • Drampa/Zoroark — high HP + efficient attacks that don't over-commit Energy/Items give Espeon/Garb trouble.
  • Low-Item / low-Ability decks — blunt both your punishers at once.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Trashalanche: 20 × opponent's Items in the discard.
  • Psychic (Espeon-GX): scales with the opponent's attached Energy.
  • Psybeam: 20 + Confusion; Energy Evolution: attach Psychic to Eevee → instant Espeon-GX.
  • Gust: Lysandre only (pre-Guzma); Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: SixPrizes ("Psychic Revelations – Espeon/Garbodor from NAIC to Now", "¡Viva Garbodor!"), cutortap ("Revisiting 2017"), Pokemon.com 2017 NAIC. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
tapu_bulu_vikavolt_2017_naic:
How to Pilot — Tapu Bulu/Vikavolt (2017 NAIC)

Format: PRC–GRI Standard (June 2017 NAIC meta — pre-Burning Shadows, Lysandre not Guzma). "BuluVolt" — a Day-2 NAIC deck. A Grass/Lightning energy-acceleration toolbox.

One-line identity

Turbo one-shots: Vikavolt's Strong Charge attaches a Grass and a Lightning Energy from your deck every turn (an Ability), letting Tapu Bulu-GX fire Nature's Judgment for 180 (210 with Choice Band) from turn 2 — enough to 1HKO almost anything. Set up one Vikavolt and you stream giant hitters all game.

The meta it lived in

NAIC 2017 (PRC-GRI): Vespiquen, Metagross-GX, Garbodor variants, Greninja, Volcanion, Zoroark/Drampa. BuluVolt's appeal was raw, weakness-independent power — Tapu Bulu-GX has no Weakness, and Nature's Judgment 1HKOs the format's 180-HP GXs. It hit Day 2 at NAIC and put up multiple Regional results. The catch: it's a multi-Stage-2 setup deck whose engine (Strong Charge) folds to Ability lock, so consistency and playing around Garbodor are everything.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Grubbin (13/149) ×3 → Charjabug (51/149) ×1 → Vikavolt (52/149) ×3 — the engine. Strong Charge (Ability): once per turn per Vikavolt, attach 1 Grass + 1 Lightning from your deck to your Pokémon. Two Vikavolt = 4 Energy a turn. The whole deck exists to get Vikavolt online (usually via Rare Candy). Note: Strong Charge pulls from the deck, so discarded Energy is dead — hence Energy Recycler.
  • Tapu Bulu-GX (SM32) ×3 — the main attacker. Nature's Judgment: 180, discard all Energy on it (Strong Charge refuels next turn). Tapu Wilderness-GX: heal all damage + a KO in some lines. No Weakness = it trades up against everything.
  • Rare Candy ×3 — jump Grubbin → Vikavolt a turn faster; turn-2 Vikavolt is the goal.
  • Energy Recycler ×2 — shuffle 5 Energy from discard back into the deck — critical, since Nature's Judgment discards Energy and Strong Charge only grabs from the deck.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Tapu Koko (SM30) ×1 — Flying Flip snipe (soften Ralts/benched targets so Nature's Judgment KOs without discarding) + free-retreat pivot; also a Lightning body.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Skyla, Lysandre, N).
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×3 / Lysandre ×2 / Skyla ×1 / Professor Kukui ×1 / Brigette ×1 — draw
  • search + reach. Skyla guarantees the Rare Candy / Heavy Ball for turn-2 Vikavolt; Lysandre (your only gust pre-Guzma) drags the KO; Kukui adds +20 for exact numbers.
  • VS Seeker ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Heavy Ball ×1 / Float Stone ×2 / Field Blower ×2 — search, recursion, get Vikavolt pieces, free retreat, and Field Blower to clear Garbotoxin (which kills Strong Charge) and Parallel City.
  • Choice Band ×2 / Fighting Fury Belt ×2 — Band pushes Nature's Judgment to 210 (1HKOs most GX); Fighting Fury Belt adds HP so Tapu Bulu survives a hit and lets it KO without discarding Energy in some spots.
  • 7 Grass + 5 Lightning — both types are mandatory for Strong Charge; the higher Grass count feeds Nature's Judgment.

Ideal early game
