  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Grubbin(s) + Tapu Koko; attach an Energy, pass. A Tapu Koko Flying Flip here helps future KO math.
  • Turn 2 (the key turn): Skyla/draw → Rare Candy → Vikavolt, Strong Charge onto Tapu Bulu-GX, and Nature's Judgment for 180 (210 with Choice Band).

The core loop

Get a Vikavolt (ideally two) online → Strong Charge to load a Tapu Bulu-GX every turn → Nature's Judgment for 180–210 to 1HKO whatever's Active → Energy Recycler the discarded Energy back so you never run dry → Lysandre to pick the target. With two Vikavolt you can even attack while building the next Bulu. No Weakness means you out-trade the field.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Turn-2 Vikavolt is everything — Skyla + Rare Candy is the line; a whiff here loses tempo you can't recover. Set up two Vikavolt when possible so a Lysandre/KO on one doesn't stop you.
  • Energy Recycler before you run dry — Strong Charge only grabs from the deck; recycle discarded Energy proactively.
  • Field Blower vs. Garbodor — Garbotoxin shuts off Strong Charge (your engine); clearing it is often the game.
  • Soften with Tapu Koko / Kukui so Nature's Judgment can KO without discarding Energy when you're low.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Garbodor (Garbotoxin) / Hex Maniac — turns off Strong Charge; the engine dies. Field Blower and careful sequencing are mandatory (Tapu Bulu's Grass hits Garbodor... but Garbotoxin still stops your acceleration).
  • Greninja BREAK — Shadow Stitching locks your Abilities and they snipe your Grubbin/Vikavolt; a rough matchup.
  • Decidueye/Vileplume — Vileplume Item-lock stops Rare Candy; hard to set up Vikavolt.
  • Slow starts — a two-Stage-2 line (Vikavolt + evolving attackers) can brick; a prized Brigette/Rare Candy hurts.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Strong Charge: 1 Grass + 1 Lightning from deck → your Pokémon, per Vikavolt, per turn.
  • Nature's Judgment: 180 (discard all Energy on Bulu) → 210 with Choice Band.
  • Setup: turn-2 Vikavolt via Skyla + Rare Candy.
  • Tapu Bulu-GX: no Weakness; Gust: Lysandre only (pre-Guzma).

Sources: PokéBeach ("Judgment Returns"), Limitless ("Tapu Bulu Vikavolt Deck Review"), 60cards ("This is How We Bulu"), Pokemon.com 2017 NAIC. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
zoroark_drampa_2017_naic:
How to Pilot — Zoroark Drampa (2017 NAIC)

Format: PRC–GRI Standard (June 2017 NAIC meta — pre-Burning Shadows, Lysandre not Guzma). "The Elusive Fox" — a Zoroark BREAK / Drampa-GX build tuned for NAIC.

One-line identity

A consistent, flexible Dark deck: Zoroark BREAK's Mind Jack scales with the opponent's bench (and Foul Play copies their Active's attack), while Drampa-GX's Berserk hits a hard 180 — the format's benchmark KO number. Zoroark (BKT) draws you through the deck with Trade, and you punish whatever the opponent presents.

The meta it lived in

NAIC 2017 (PRC-GRI): Metagross-GX, Vespiquen, Garbodor variants (Espeon/Garb, Drampa/Garb), Greninja, Volcanion, Vikavolt/Tapu Bulu. Zoroark/Drampa was a hyped, versatile pick — its attacks feed off the opponent's own board and attacks, so it flexes into most matchups. It notably took Zoroark BREAK to Top 8 at NAIC (Andrew Mahone). It's a "your damage comes from their choices" deck, which makes it consistent but weak to opponents who deny it those angles.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Zorua (89/162) ×4 → Zoroark (91/162) ×4 → Zoroark BREAK (92/162) ×2 — the core. Zoroark BKT (91) has Stand In (free switch Ability) and Mind Jack (20 × opponent's benched Pokémon). Zoroark BREAK keeps that and adds Foul Play: copy an attack of the Defending Pokémon for a single DCE — a devastating mirror to their own best attacker. (Note: this is the BKT Zoroark line, not Zoroark-GX — different card, Trade not printed here; Stand In is the switch engine.)
  • Drampa-GX (115/145) ×2 — the muscle. Berserk: 180 if your opponent has damage/ played a Supporter (the KO benchmark); Righteous Edge chips 30 and knocks an Energy off their Active — great vs. Energy-reliant decks. Colorless, so DCE powers it.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Lysandre, N, Kukui).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Eevee (63/98) ×1 → Flareon (13/98) ×1 — a Grass/Fire tech attacker; a specific answer (e.g. Vespiquen / evolving decks) that Mind Jack alone can't handle.
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×3 / Lysandre ×2 / Hex Maniac ×1 / Teammates ×1 / Professor Kukui ×1 / Brigette ×1 — draw + reach. Lysandre (your only gust pre-Guzma) drags the KO; Kukui (+20) pushes Mind Jack/Foul Play/Berserk over exact lines (e.g. Foul Play + Choice Band + Kukui = 180 on a Volcanion-EX); Hex Maniac shuts off Abilities; Teammates rebuilds after a KO.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / VS Seeker ×4 / Rescue Stretcher ×2 / Field Blower ×1 / Special Charge ×1 — search, recursion (VS Seeker → more Lysandre), recover Zoroark/Drampa, Tool/Stadium removal, and Special Charge to get DCE back (you lean on it heavily).
  • Choice Band ×3 / Float Stone ×2 — +30 vs. GX/EX for exact KOs; free retreat (Stand In covers Zoroark, Float Stone covers Drampa).
  • 4 DCE + 4 Rainbow + 5 Darkness — DCE/Rainbow power Foul Play and Drampa cheaply; Darkness for Riotous/Mind Jack's Dark cost and Rainbow flexibility.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for a full Zorua squad + a Drampa-GX; attach a Darkness/DCE. A strong Zorua-heavy open is what powers the rest of the game.
  • Turn 2: evolve Zoroark(s), start Trade-style digging (Stand In for mobility), and either Mind Jack (if their bench is wide) or set up Berserk for 180.

The core loop

Draw and dig with your Zoroark engine, then pick the right attack: Mind Jack when their bench is wide (2HKO/KO), Foul Play to turn their own best attack against them, or Drampa Berserk for a clean 180. Righteous Edge strips Energy from Energy-reliant decks. Lysandre + Kukui + Choice Band line up exact KOs. Stand In keeps you mobile without spending your Supporter.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Set up multiple Zoroark — the deck's consistency and Mind Jack both want a wide board; a Brigette turn 1 is huge.
  • Match the attack to the board — Mind Jack (wide bench), Foul Play (big-attack Active), Berserk (clean 180). Reading which to use is the skill of the deck.
  • Lysandre is your only gust — VS Seeker it; time it (often with Foul Play/Berserk) for the KO that matters.
  • Manage DCE — Foul Play and Drampa lean on it; Special Charge/careful discarding keep you stocked.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting — Zoroark (Dark) is Fighting-Weak (though this format's Fighting is limited; post-rotation Buzzwole/Lycanroc make it worse).
  • Item/Ability-lock or decks that deny board width — if the opponent keeps a tiny bench, Mind Jack falls off, and low-Energy/low-Supporter decks blunt Berserk/Righteous Edge.
  • Metagross-GX / Greninja / big Stage-2 setups — Drampa/Zoroark "can't really do anything to handle its bad matchups"; you pray for a turn-2 Berserk and out-tempo.
  • Decidueye/Vileplume — Item lock + stall gives this deck (no lock of its own) trouble.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Mind Jack: 20 × opponent's benched Pokémon (Zoroark BKT/BREAK).
  • Foul Play: copy the Defending Pokémon's attack for a single DCE.
  • Berserk (Drampa-GX): 180 (when the condition is met).
  • Gust: Lysandre only (pre-Guzma); Kukui +20, Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: SixPrizes ("The Elusive Fox – NAIC Potential"), cutortap ("Mind Jack of All Trades"), Pokemon.com 2017 NAIC (Mahone Top 8), PokéBeach. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
banette_garbodor_2018:
How to Pilot — Banette Garbodor (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–CES). "Garbanette" — this build mirrors Magnus Pedersen's 2018 World Championship-winning (Senior Division) list.

One-line identity

A disruptive single-Energy toolbox: Banette-GX attacks for just one Psychic (Shadow Chant scales with Supporters in your discard) and its Shady Move Ability slides a damage counter anywhere — even onto the opponent — for surprise KOs. Back it with the classic Garbodor split (Garbotoxin lock / Trashalanche punish) and Wobbuffet for a second Ability-lock, and you strangle the opponent's engine.

The meta it lived in

Garbanette is the spiritual successor to Espeon-GX/Garbodor — but stronger, because Banette-GX only ever needs one Energy and doesn't depend on drawing DCE. In the Zoroark-GX era, its Fighting Resistance and Shady Move damage-shifting made it a strong answer to Buzzwole/Shrine (the deck that preys on Zoroark), while Garbotoxin locked the mirror and Malamar. Magnus Pedersen won the Senior Division World Championship with it.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Shuppet (63/168) ×2 → Banette-GX (66/168) ×2 — the main attacker. Shadow Chant: 30 + 10 per Supporter in your discard (up to +100) for a single Psychic — a cheap, scaling hit. Shady Move (Ability): once per turn, move a damage counter from one Pokémon to any other (including the opponent's) — sets up 1HKOs and finishes softened targets. Tomb Hunt-GX recovers 3 cards. Fighting-Resistant = great vs. Buzzwole.
  • Trubbish (56/122) ×4 → Garbodor — the split line:
  • Garbodor (57/122, "Garbotoxin") ×2 — with a Tool, all Abilities off.
  • Garbodor (51/145, "Trashalanche") ×2 — 20 × the opponent's Items in the discard.
  • Wobbuffet (36/119) ×1 — Bide Barricade: while it's Active, Basic Pokémon Abilities are shut off — a second, Tool-free Ability-lock angle (and a body that stalls).
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×2 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, N, Guzma).

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Drampa-GX (115/145) ×1 — Berserk (180) and Righteous Edge (chip + Energy removal); a Colorless heavy-hitter for the KOs Banette can't reach, and Righteous Edge strips Energy from Energy-reliant decks.
  • Buzzwole (77/131) ×1 — a Fighting single-prize attacker tech (mirror / specific matchups).
  • Jirachi (XY67) ×1 — Stellar Guidance: search a Supporter (extra consistency).
  • Sycamore ×4 / N ×4 / Guzma ×3 / Cynthia ×2 / Brigette ×1 — heavy control Supporter suite (and Shadow Chant wants Supporters in the discard, so playing them fuels your attack).
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Mysterious Treasure ×1 / Field Blower ×2 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 — search, Tool/Stadium removal (toggle your own Garbotoxin), recovery.
  • Float Stone ×4 / Choice Band ×3 / Parallel City ×2 — free retreat (and Garbotoxin's Tool), +30 vs. GX/EX (Banette + Choice Band + full discard = 170 on a Tapu Lele-GX), and bench/damage control.
  • 4 Psychic + 4 Rainbow + 3 DCE — Psychic powers Banette/Shadow Chant; Rainbow enables Garbodor + Drampa; DCE for Drampa/off-costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Shuppet + Trubbish; bench your pieces. Pass.
  • Turn 2: evolve Banette-GX, Shadow Chant for a cheap hit (already boosted if you've discarded Supporters), and start setting up Garbotoxin.

The core loop

Attack cheaply every turn with Shadow Chant (which grows as your Supporter discard grows — and you're playing Supporters anyway), use Shady Move to slide counters and snipe/ finish exact KOs, and lock the opponent's Abilities with Garbotoxin (or Wobbuffet). Bring in Drampa Berserk for a big KO, hold Trashalanche vs. Item decks, and grind the prize trade with Fighting-Resistant, single-Energy attackers.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Shady Move sets up 1HKOs — pre-place/relocate counters so Shadow Chant (or a bench snipe) finishes a Pokémon it otherwise couldn't.
  • Your Supporters fuel Shadow Chant — the Supporter-heavy build is a feature; a fat Supporter discard = a bigger attack.
  • Garbotoxin / Wobbuffet vs. Ability decks — pick your lock; Wobbuffet needs no Tool.
  • Don't over-bench GX — Banette/Garbodor decks want to keep the prize trade tight; Tapu Lele-GX and Drampa are liabilities if left exposed (and to opposing Shrine).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Dark (Darkrai, opposing Zoroark's Dark attacks) — Banette-GX is Dark-Weak (some lists add Weakness Policy for exactly this).
  • Field Blower decks — removing your Garbotoxin Tool blunts the lock.
  • Fast 1HKO decks — anything that races before Garbotoxin + Shady Move take over.
  • Item-light / Ability-light decks — blunt both Trashalanche and Garbotoxin at once.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Shadow Chant: 30 + 10 per Supporter in your discard (max +100) for 1 Psychic.
  • Shady Move: move 1 damage counter anywhere (Ability, once/turn).
  • Garbotoxin: all Abilities off with a Tool; Wobbuffet: Basic Abilities off while Active.
  • Banette + Choice Band + full discard = 170 (1HKOs Tapu Lele-GX).

Sources: Bulbapedia (Garbanette — Pedersen 1st 2018 Worlds Seniors), Amazon/official Worlds deck list, SixPrizes ("On the Spooky Side", "The Spooky Side of Standard"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
buzz_garb_shrine_2018:
How to Pilot — Buzz/Garb/Shrine (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018, post-rotation (SM-on, Celestial Storm era — Strong Energy/Max Elixir gone). "Buzzwole/Shrine" — a single-prize Fighting control deck that was a defining post-rotation archetype.

One-line identity

A single-prize grind deck: attack almost entirely with non-GX Buzzwole (Jet Punch spread + Sledgehammer 1HKOs), lock the opponent's Abilities with Garbodor's Garbotoxin, and let Shrine of Punishment quietly place damage on every GX/EX the opponent benches. You give up few prizes, punish their GX-heavy boards, and win the attrition war.

The meta it lived in

After the 2018 rotation (Strong Energy and Max Elixir gone), the format slowed down and Shrine of Punishment — which had already made noise on Day 1 of Worlds 2018 — became a menace. In a GX-flooded meta (Zoroark, Rayquaza, Malamar all lean on GX Pokémon), a deck that's all single-prize attackers + Garbotoxin + Shrine flips the prize math: you take 2-prize KOs while giving up 1, and Shrine chips their board for free. It was a consistent League/Regional performer.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Buzzwole (77/131) ×4 — the main attacker, a non-GX (1 prize). Jet Punch: 30 active + 30 bench snipe. Sledgehammer: 120 when the opponent has 4 prizes left — a single-prize 1HKO on most GXs. Being all-baby-Buzzwole means Shrine doesn't hurt you.
  • Trubbish (56/122) ×3 → Garbodor (51/145) ×2 — Garbotoxin (with a Tool): all Abilities off. Shuts off Zoroark's Trade, Malamar's Psychic Recharge, Octillery — the glue of the format's engines.
  • Shrine of Punishment (143/168) ×3 — Stadium: between turns, put damage on each GX/EX in play. Your board is (almost) all non-GX, so it's a one-sided clock against the field. Multiple copies win the Stadium war.
  • Diancie Prism Star (74/131) ×1 + Beast Energy Prism Star ×1 — Fighting damage boosts; the modifiers (with Choice Band/Strong) that push Jet Punch/Sledgehammer to exact KO numbers now that Strong Energy is scarcer.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Remoraid (32/162) ×2 → Octillery (33/162) ×2 — Abyssal Hand draw engine. (Note: an Ability — you toggle your own Garbotoxin with Field Blower when you need to draw.)
  • Sycamore ×4 / Cynthia ×3 / N ×3 / Guzma ×4 — a heavy Supporter suite for a Supporter- reliant control deck. 4 Guzma = you always drag up the target to snipe/KO; N disrupts their comeback.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Nest Ball ×3 / Field Blower ×3 / Rescue Stretcher ×2 / Special Charge ×1 — search, bench Basics, remove opposing Tools (and toggle your own Garbotoxin), recover.
  • Float Stone ×3 / Choice Band ×4 — free retreat (and Garbotoxin's Tool); 4 Choice Band is the primary damage modifier to hit KO numbers post-Strong-Energy.
  • 4 Strong Energy + 3 Rainbow + 1 Psychic — Strong (+20 to Fighting) is the key modifier; Rainbow enables Garbodor's cost and flexibility; Psychic for off-costs. (Note: Rainbow hurts you 10 — but with mostly non-GX attackers and Shrine on your side, the trade is fine.)

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: Jet Punch early (30 + 30 snipe), bench Trubbish + Remoraid, and get Shrine of Punishment down so their GX board starts taking chip damage.
  • Establish Garbotoxin around turn 2–3 vs. Ability decks (Zoroark/Malamar) — it's often the whole matchup.

The core loop

Spread with Jet Punch, lock with Garbotoxin, and let Shrine tick their GX board down between turns. Use Guzma to drag up a softened Pokémon and finish it (often with a single-prize Buzzwole, keeping the prize trade lopsided). When they hit 4 prizes, Sledgehammer 1HKOs. You trade 1-for-2 and grind them out.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Stay single-prize — the deck's whole edge is giving up 1 prize per KO while taking 2; don't lean on Buzzwole-GX-style 2-prize liabilities unless necessary.
  • Shrine is one-sided — keep it in play (multiple copies, win the Stadium war); it damages their GX board, not your non-GX one.
  • Garbotoxin vs. Ability decks; toggle it off (Field Blower your own Tool) when you need Octillery to draw.
  • Play the 4-prize window for Sledgehammer; sequence KOs so it lands.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Psychic attackers (Malamar/Necrozma, Mewtwo) — Buzzwole is Psychic-Weak; a Psychic deck that ignores Shrine (few GX) is rough.
  • Non-GX / low-GX decks — Shrine does nothing to them, and they don't fear your prize math as much.
  • Fast 1HKO decks — anything that races before Garbotoxin/Shrine take over.
  • Field Blower decks — opponents removing Shrine/your Garbotoxin Tool blunt both your engines.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Jet Punch: 30 active + 30 bench snipe; Sledgehammer: 120 at opponent's 4 prizes (single-prize attacker).
  • Garbotoxin: all Abilities off while Garbodor holds a Tool.
  • Shrine of Punishment: damage on each GX/EX in play between turns.
  • Strong Energy +20 to Fighting; Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: therainbowwing (2018 post-rotation meta snapshots — Buzzwole/Shrine), SixPrizes ("The Fighting Force"), Flipside (Philadelphia recap — Buzz/Shrine). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
buzzwole_lycanroc_2018:
How to Pilot — Buzzwole Lycanroc (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–FLI, "Buzzroc"). The premier Fighting deck of the year and the top counter to Zoroark-GX.

One-line identity

Fast, disruptive Fighting aggression: Buzzwole-GX's Jet Punch does 30 to the Active and 30 to a benched Pokémon for a single Energy, Lycanroc-GX's Bloodthirsty Eyes drags up whatever you want to KO (no Supporter needed), and Beast Ring + Sledgehammer give you explosive comeback turns. You dismantle the opponent's board and take prizes a turn early.

The meta it lived in

Buzzroc was Tier 1 all year and the deck that kept Zoroark-GX honest — Buzzwole hits Zoroark for Weakness and can 1HKO it with the right modifiers. It's a highly disruptive "take down whatever they set up" deck: between 4 Guzma and 2 Lycanroc-GX you have six ways to drag up a target. With Beast Ring, it also has some of the best comeback turns in the format once the opponent is at 4 prizes.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Buzzwole-GX (57/111) ×2 — the star. Jet Punch: 30 active + 30 bench snipe for one Fighting (Landorus-EX's old attack). Knuckle Impact: 160 (can't attack next turn — pivot out). Absorption-GX: 40 × your remaining prizes (a huge early nuke).
  • Buzzwole (77/131) ×3 — the non-GX "baby" Buzzwole. Sledgehammer: 120 if the opponent has 4 prizes left — a single-prize attacker that 1HKOs GXs at the right moment. Bulky and hard for Zoroark to profitably KO.
  • Rockruff (75/131) ×2 → Lycanroc-GX (74/145) ×2 — Bloodthirsty Eyes (Ability): when it evolves, switch one of the opponent's benched Pokémon to Active — a free gust to line up KOs. Dangerous Rogue-GX scales with their bench for a big finish; Claw Slash hits hard with modifiers.
  • Remoraid (32/162) ×2 → Octillery (33/162) ×2 — Abyssal Hand draw engine; the deck's consistency glue ("without Octillery this deck would be garbage").

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Diancie Prism Star (74/131) ×1 — boosts your Fighting attackers +20; the modifier that turns Jet Punch/Sledgehammer/Claw Slash into exact KO numbers on Zoroark/Lycanroc.
  • Regirock (80/168) ×1 — another Fighting damage boost / bulky body for exact math.
  • Max Elixir ×2 — accelerate a basic Fighting from the top 6 onto a Basic — speeds up Knuckle Impact / a second attacker.
  • Beast Ring ×2 — when the opponent has 4 or fewer prizes, attach 2 basic Fighting from the deck to your Ultra Beasts — an explosive refuel that powers a comeback KO (or two).
  • Sycamore ×4 / Cynthia ×3 / Guzma ×3 / N ×2 — draw + gust. High Guzma count = you always choose the target (stacks with Bloodthirsty Eyes).
  • Ultra Ball ×3 / Field Blower ×2 / Super Rod ×1 / Brooklet Hill ×3 — search, Tool/Stadium removal, recovery, and Brooklet Hill to fetch a Fighting/Water Basic to the bench each turn.
  • Fighting Fury Belt ×2 / Choice Band ×2 / Float Stone ×2 — +40 HP/+10, +30 vs. GX/EX, free retreat (Buzzwole's retreat is heavy).
  • 9 Fighting + 4 Strong Energy + 1 Beast Energy Prism — Strong Energy (+20 to Fighting) and Beast Energy are the damage modifiers that hit KO numbers; Beast Energy also gives extra when your Beast is Active.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (going 2nd is great): Jet Punch immediately — 30 active + 30 to a key benched Pokémon (a Rockruff, Remoraid, Zorua). Bench Rockruff and Remoraid.
  • Early turns: stack Jet Punch snipes + Bloodthirsty Eyes to line up KOs; keep the opponent from stabilizing.

The core loop

Jet Punch to spread (active + bench snipe) → Bloodthirsty Eyes / Guzma to drag up the softened target → KO it with Buzzwole-GX or a buffed baby Buzzwole. Once they hit 4 prizes, Sledgehammer 1HKOs and Beast Ring refuels a fresh Buzzwole for a huge swing. Use Absorption-GX early (it's biggest when you have the most prizes) for a nuke, and Dangerous Rogue-GX to close.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Snipe to set up multi-prize turns — Jet Punch's 30 bench + a KO = tempo the opponent can't match.
  • Play the prize count — Sledgehammer wants them at 4; Absorption-GX wants you at 6. Sequence KOs so your big attacks land at the right prize totals.
  • Six gusts (4 Guzma + 2 Lycanroc) — nothing on their board is safe; pull up the piece that breaks their plan.
  • Diancie/Strong/Choice Band math — know your exact numbers to 1HKO Zoroark/Lycanroc.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Psychic (Malamar/Necrozma, Mewtwo, Mew-EX) — Buzzwole-GX is Psychic-Weak; Dawn Wings Necrozma and Mewtwo punish it (lean on Lycanroc, which doesn't share the Weakness).
  • Decks that avoid the 4-prize window — a careful opponent taking double-KO turns denies your Sledgehammer/Beast Ring.
  • Mirror / other Fighting — comes down to snipe sequencing and modifier math.
  • High-HP walls without Psychic Weakness — can be awkward for baby Buzzwole to close.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Jet Punch: 30 active + 30 bench snipe for 1 Fighting.
  • Sledgehammer (baby Buzzwole): 120 when opponent has 4 prizes (single-prize attacker).
  • Beast Ring: attach 2 basic Fighting from deck to your Beasts when opponent ≤4 prizes.
  • Absorption-GX: 40 × your remaining prizes; Bloodthirsty Eyes: free gust on evolve.

Sources: Pokemon.com ("Wallop Your Foes with Buzzwole-GX"), SixPrizes ("The Rock of the Cycle", "The Fighting Force"), PokéBeach ("Still the King of Standard"), Flipside ("Buzzroc 2.0"). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
zoroark_garbodor_2018:
How to Pilot — Zoroark Garbodor (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–FLI, the Zoroark-GX era). "ZoroGarb" — this build mirrors Stephane Ivanoff's 2018 World Championship-winning list.

One-line identity

The consistency king of the format with a disruption package bolted on: Zoroark-GX's Trade draws you 2 cards a turn (an Ability), Riotous Beating hits 20 × your Pokémon, and a split Garbodor line either shuts off all Abilities (Garbotoxin) or punishes Item decks (Trashalanche). You out-draw everyone, then lock or grind them out.

The meta it lived in

2018 Standard was the Zoroark-GX year — it was the best card in the format, and five of the top decks were built on it. Stephane Ivanoff took ZoroGarb all the way to the 2018 World Championship (beating Tord Reklev in the final). Garbotoxin was the key edge: it turns off the mirror's Trade, Malamar's Psychic Recharge, and every other Ability engine, letting a fair-and-square Zoroark deck grind ahead.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Zorua (52/73) ×4 → Zoroark-GX (53/73) ×4 — the core. Trade (Ability): once per turn, discard a card to draw 2 — the best draw engine in the game and the reason you set up multiple. Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon in play (up to ~120+) for a DCE. Trickster-GX copies any attack in a pinch.
  • Trubbish (56/122) ×3 → Garbodor — the split line (the deck's disruptive teeth):
  • Garbodor (51/145, "Garbotoxin") ×2 — with a Tool attached, all Abilities off (both players). Shuts off the mirror's Trade and Malamar. The primary plan in most matchups.
  • Garbodor (57/122, "Trashalanche") ×1 — 20 × the opponent's Items in the discard; a finisher vs. Item-heavy decks.
  • Kartana-GX (70/111) ×1 — a Metal attacker; Blade-GX and its cheap attack give you a non-Dark angle and a body that dodges Fighting Weakness in spots.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: search any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, N).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Puzzle of Time ×4 — play 2 together to retrieve any 2 cards from the discard — the recursion engine that lets you loop DCE, Guzma, Field Blower, or a KO'd Zoroark endlessly.
  • Mysterious Treasure ×4 / Ultra Ball ×0 (Treasure fills the role) / Evosoda ×3 — search: Mysterious Treasure (discard a card → any Psychic/Dragon, grabs Zorua/Trubbish/ Tapu Lele), Evosoda evolves without a Supporter, keeping tempo.
  • N ×4 / Brigette ×3 / Guzma ×2 / Sycamore ×1 / Cynthia ×1 — draw + setup + disruption. Brigette (×3) is the turn-1 board-builder (get 3 Basics down); N disrupts and refuels; Guzma gusts the KO.
  • Field Blower ×3 / Enhanced Hammer ×1 / Rescue Stretcher ×1 / Town Map ×1 — remove Tools/Stadiums (and your own Garbotoxin Tool when you need Trade back), strip special Energy, recover, and prize-fix.
  • Float Stone ×3 / Choice Band ×2 / Parallel City ×2 — free retreat (and Garbotoxin's Tool), +30 vs. GX/EX, and a Stadium that shrinks benches / cuts damage.
  • 4 DCE + 3 Unit Energy (LPM) — DCE powers Riotous Beating; Unit Energy Lightning/Psychic/Metal enables Kartana and off-costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette (via Tapu Lele) for Zorua ×2–3 + Trubbish; ideally a Zorua-heavy board. Pass.
  • Turn 2: evolve into Zoroark-GX, start Trade (2 cards/turn), and either Riotous Beating for a KO or set up Garbotoxin depending on the matchup.

The core loop

Trade every turn to churn through the deck, take 2HKOs with Riotous Beating, and use Puzzle of Time to recur whatever you need (DCE, Guzma, a KO'd Zoroark). Set up Garbotoxin to lock the opponent's Abilities (crucial in the mirror and vs. Malamar), or hold Trashalanche vs. Item decks. Guzma + Choice Band line up the exact KO; N keeps them off-balance.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Brigette turn 1 is the whole setup — a wide Zorua board = big Riotous Beating + more Trade next turn.
  • Garbotoxin in the mirror — turning off the opponent's Trade while keeping yours (via Field Blower toggling your own Tool) usually wins the ZoroGarb mirror.
  • Manage your Item count vs. your own Trashalanche isn't a risk, but watch the opponent's Trashalanche against you — Puzzle/Mysterious Treasure pile up Items.
  • Puzzle of Time is your recursion — plan two-Puzzle turns to reclaim the exact pieces.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Buzzwole/Lycanroc, Buzz/Shrine) — Zoroark-GX is Fighting-Weak; Jet Punch + Sledgehammer + Beast Ring punishes it. The defining bad matchup.
  • Shrine of Punishment decks — passive damage on your GX-heavy board adds up.
  • Opposing Garbotoxin — turns off your Trade; you must Field Blower it or race.
  • Big 1HKO decks (Ultra Necrozma/Malamar) — can out-damage your 2HKO grind.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Trade: discard 1 → draw 2, once per turn per Zoroark (Ability).
  • Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon in play for a DCE.
  • Garbotoxin: all Abilities off while Garbodor holds a Tool.
  • Puzzle of Time (×2): retrieve any 2 cards; Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: Pokemon.com (Ivanoff, 1st 2018 Worlds Masters list), Bulbapedia (Zoroark-GX), therainbowwing / Flipside 2018 meta snapshots, SixPrizes. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
zoroark_gardevoir_2018:
How to Pilot — Zoroark Gardevoir (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–FLI). "ZoroGard" — a Tier-1 Zoroark-GX variant pairing the format's best draw engine with Gardevoir-GX's scaling Fairy attacker.

One-line identity

Consistency plus a heavy hitter: Zoroark-GX's Trade churns through the deck while Gardevoir-GX provides Infinite Force (30 × total Energy on both Active Pokémon) and Secret Spring Fairy acceleration. Gallade covers the Fighting matchups that wreck normal Zoroark decks, and Mew-EX answers Buzzwole — a flexible, powerful toolbox.

The meta it lived in

ZoroGard was a Tier-1 pairing that tried to solve Zoroark's biggest problem — its Fighting Weakness — by teching Gallade (a Fighting attacker that hits Buzzwole/Zoroark back) and Mew-EX. Gardevoir-GX's Fairy typing also resists the Dark attacks in the mirror. It plays the classic Zoroark game (Trade, Riotous Beating) with a bigger late-game ceiling than the other variants via Infinite Force.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Zorua (52/73) ×4 → Zoroark-GX (53/73) ×4 — the draw/attack core. Trade (discard 1 → draw 2), Riotous Beating (20 × your Pokémon), Trickster-GX.
  • Ralts (91/147) ×4 → Gardevoir-GX (93/147) ×2 — the hitter/accelerator. Secret Spring (Ability): attach a Fairy from hand each turn (on top of your attachment). Infinite Force: 30 × all Energy on both Active Pokémon — scales into 1HKO range. Twilight-GX recycles 10 cards. Fairy typing resists Dark (mirror).
  • Gallade (84/162) ×1 (from Kirlia) — a Fighting attacker; the tech that lets this Zoroark deck punch Buzzwole/Zoroark for Weakness (the matchups that sink other variants).
  • Rare Candy ×3 — jump Ralts → Gardevoir-GX a turn faster (skip Kirlia).
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, Mallow).

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Mew-EX (XY126) ×1 — copies a benched attacker (a Buzzwole counter: copy Riotous Beating / a Fighting move to 1HKO the Psychic-Weak Beasts).
  • Giratina (XY184) ×1 — its Ability blanks effects of some GX/EX attacks / an anti-lock disruptive body (tech vs. specific attackers).
  • Puzzle of Time ×4 — retrieve any 2 cards (DCE, Rare Candy, Guzma, a KO'd attacker) — the recursion engine.
  • Brigette ×3 / N ×3 / Sycamore ×2 / Mallow ×2 / Guzma ×2 — setup + draw + gust. Mallow (×2) finds the exact combo piece (Rare Candy + Gardevoir); Brigette builds the turn-1 board.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Evosoda ×2 / Field Blower ×2 / Enhanced Hammer ×1 — search, Supporter- free evolves, Tool/Stadium removal, special-Energy strip.
  • Max Potion ×1 / Choice Band ×1 / Float Stone ×1 / Parallel City ×1 — heal (great with a loaded Gardevoir — reset their progress), +30 vs. GX/EX, free retreat, bench control.
  • 4 DCE + 4 Fairy — DCE powers Riotous Beating/Gallade; Fairy feeds Secret Spring + Infinite Force.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette for Zorua ×2 + Ralts; wide board, pass.
  • Turn 2: evolve Zoroark-GX, Trade to dig for Rare Candy → Gardevoir-GX, start Secret Spring banking Fairy, and take a Riotous Beating KO while you build Infinite Force.

The core loop

Trade every turn for gas; take early KOs with Riotous Beating; transition to Gardevoir-GX — Secret Spring banks Energy so Infinite Force climbs into 1HKO territory, and Max Potion resets damage. Bring in Gallade vs. Fighting/Zoroark and Mew-EX vs. Buzzwole. Puzzle of Time recurs pieces; Guzma picks the KO. You out- consistency the field and out-scale it late.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Brigette turn 1, then dig for turn-2/3 Gardevoir (Rare Candy + Mallow) — Secret Spring wants to start early.
  • Gallade is your Fighting answer — it's why this variant survives the Buzzwole matchups that kill other Zoroark decks; set it up when Fighting looms.
  • Bank Energy for Infinite Force, but spread attachments so an Espeon-GX/Psychic can't punish an over-loaded Pokémon.
  • Mew-EX for Buzzwole; Max Potion to erase their damage on a loaded Gardevoir.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Buzzwole/Lycanroc) — still the pressure point, though Gallade/Mew-EX make it far more winnable than other Zoroark variants.
  • Fire / Metal (vs. Gardevoir) — Gardevoir-GX is Metal-Weak; Fire hits it hard.
  • Garbotoxin (opposing Garbodor) — turns off Trade and Secret Spring; Field Blower is key.
  • Shrine of Punishment — chip on your GX-heavy board.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Trade: discard 1 → draw 2; Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon.
  • Infinite Force: 30 × total Energy on both Active Pokémon; Secret Spring: +1 Fairy/turn.
  • Gallade: Fighting attacker (Buzzwole/Zoroark answer); Mew-EX copies a benched attacker.
  • Puzzle of Time (×2): retrieve any 2 cards; Choice Band +30 vs. GX/EX.

Sources: PokéBeach tier list (Gardevoir-GX/Zoroark-GX Tier 1), Bulbapedia (Zoroark-GX, Gardevoir-GX), Flipside/SixPrizes 2018 Zoroark analyses. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
zoroark_golisopod_2018:
How to Pilot — Zoroark Golisopod (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–FLI). "ZoroPod" — a Tier-1 Zoroark-GX variant (Masters Worlds Top-8 archetype).

One-line identity

Consistency meets efficiency: Zoroark-GX's Trade draws you through the deck every turn while Riotous Beating provides scaling damage, and Golisopod-GX adds a hyper- efficient First Impression (120 for one Grass) as a second, harder-hitting attacker. Zoroark's free-switch Stand In and Acerola reset Golisopod so First Impression fires every turn.

The meta it lived in

ZoroPod was one of the strongest Zoroark-GX pairings of 2018 (Masters Worlds Top-8). It kept Zoroark's best-in-format consistency but gained a bigger single hit (First Impression) and a Grass typing to trade into the field. Its natural predator was the same as every Zoroark deck's — Fighting (Buzzwole) — but its efficiency and Acerola resets let it grind most of the field.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Zorua (52/73) ×4 → Zoroark-GX (53/73) ×4 — the core. Trade (Ability): discard 1 → draw 2 each turn; Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon; Trickster-GX copies an attack. Also note the BKT-line Stand In isn't on Zoroark-GX — here mobility comes from Float Stone + Guzma + Acerola.
  • Wimpod (16/147) ×3 → Golisopod-GX (17/147) ×2 — the hitter. First Impression: 120 for one Grass, only the turn Golisopod becomes Active. You reset it into the Active spot each turn (Guzma, Acerola, Float Stone) to re-fire.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, Acerola, Cynthia).
  • Puzzle of Time ×4 — retrieve any 2 cards (DCE, Guzma, Acerola, a KO'd attacker) — the recursion that keeps First Impression and Riotous Beating streaming.

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Oranguru (113/149) ×1 — Resource Management/Instruct: recycle cards or draw; a benched engine that survives N and doesn't rely on Trade.
  • Mew-EX (XY126) ×1 — copies a benched attacker's move (a Buzzwole-GX counter — copy Riotous Beating/First Impression to 1HKO a Buzzwole that's Psychic-Weak).
  • Acerola ×1 / Max Potion ×1 — the reset/heal package: Acerola picks up a damaged Golisopod (heal + re-arm First Impression) and denies a prize; Max Potion full-heals.
  • Brigette ×4 / N ×3 / Guzma ×3 / Cynthia ×1 / Sycamore ×1 — setup + draw + gust. Brigette ×4 guarantees the turn-1 board; Guzma resets Golisopod and gusts targets.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Evosoda ×3 / Field Blower ×3 / Counter Catcher ×1 — search, Supporter- free evolves, Tool/Stadium removal, and Counter Catcher (free gust when you're behind on prizes).
  • Choice Band ×1 / Float Stone ×2 / Parallel City ×3 — +30 vs. GX/EX, free retreat (reset First Impression), and Parallel City (×3) to shrink benches / cut Fire & Water damage and win the Stadium war.
  • 4 DCE + 3 Grass — DCE powers Riotous Beating; Grass powers First Impression.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette for Zorua ×2–3 + Wimpod; pass with a wide board.
  • Turn 2: evolve Zoroark-GX, Trade to dig, and either Riotous Beating or move a Golisopod-GX Active for First Impression (120).

The core loop

Trade every turn for gas, take 2HKOs with Riotous Beating, and drop First Impression whenever you can reset Golisopod Active (Guzma/Acerola/Float Stone). Acerola heals + re-arms Golisopod and denies prizes; Puzzle of Time recurs your pieces; Guzma/Counter Catcher pick the KO. Mew-EX answers Buzzwole. You out-consistency and out-efficiency the field.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Brigette turn 1 — wide board = bigger Riotous Beating + more Trade.
  • Reset Golisopod every turn — First Impression is 120 only on entry; Acerola/Guzma/Float Stone are the enablers.
  • Acerola = heal + reload + prize denial — the deck's grind engine; don't waste it.
  • Mew-EX for Buzzwole — copy the right attack to 1HKO the Psychic-Weak baby Buzzwole/ Buzzwole-GX.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Buzzwole/Lycanroc, Buzz/Shrine) — Zoroark-GX is Fighting-Weak; the defining bad matchup (Mew-EX + First Impression help, but it's tough).
  • Garbotoxin (opposing Garbodor) — turns off your Trade; Field Blower it or race.
  • Shrine of Punishment — passive damage on your GX-heavy board.
  • Fire attackers — Golisopod is Fire-Weak (Parallel City helps).

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Trade: discard 1 → draw 2 (Ability, once/turn/Zoroark).
  • Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon for a DCE.
  • First Impression: 120 for 1 Grass, only on entering Active (150 w/ Choice Band).
  • Puzzle of Time (×2): retrieve any 2 cards; reset tools: Guzma/Acerola/Float Stone.

Sources: Pokemon.com (2018 Worlds Masters — Zoroark/Golisopod Top-8), Bulbapedia (Zoroark-GX, Golisopod-GX), Flipside/SixPrizes 2018 Zoroark analyses. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
zoroark_lycanroc_2018:
How to Pilot — Zoroark Lycanroc (2018)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2018 (BKT–FLI). "ZoroRoc" — a Tier-1 (arguably the best) Zoroark-GX variant of the year.

One-line identity

The most flexible Zoroark deck: Zoroark-GX's Trade draws you through the deck, Riotous Beating scales with your board, and Lycanroc-GX's Bloodthirsty Eyes drags up any benched Pokémon (no Supporter) so you KO exactly what you want. Add Mew-EX and a baby Buzzwole for coverage and you can take 6 prizes in ~3 attacks against most decks.

The meta it lived in

ZoroRoc was considered by many the center of the 2018 metagame — oppressive against other Zoroark variants and slow Stage-2 decks thanks to Bloodthirsty Eyes disruption. It pairs Zoroark's raw consistency with Lycanroc's gust-on-evolve and a strong closing GX attack. Its only shaky spots were dedicated OHKO decks (Rayquaza-GX/Vikavolt) and the Fighting-Weakness that all Zoroark decks share.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Zorua (52/73) ×4 → Zoroark-GX (53/73) ×4 — the core. Trade (discard 1 → draw 2), Riotous Beating (20 × your Pokémon), Trickster-GX (copy an attack). The best draw engine in the format.
  • Rockruff (75/131) ×3 → Lycanroc-GX (74/145) ×2 — Bloodthirsty Eyes (Ability): switch a benched opposing Pokémon Active on evolve — a free gust to line up KOs. Claw Slash (110, buffable to 180 with Choice Band/Kukui/Strong) and Dangerous Rogue-GX (scales with their bench) give real attacking power.
  • Tapu Lele-GX (60/145) ×3 — Wonder Tag: any Supporter (Brigette, Guzma, Cynthia, Mallow).
  • Puzzle of Time ×4 — retrieve any 2 cards (DCE, Guzma, a KO'd attacker) — the recursion engine.

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Oranguru (113/149) ×1 — benched draw/recycle engine that survives N (doesn't rely on Trade).
  • Mew-EX (XY126) ×1 — copies a benched attacker (a Buzzwole counter — copy Riotous Beating to 1HKO the Psychic-Weak Buzzwole).
  • Buzzwole (77/131) ×1 — a Fighting single-prize attacker tech: Jet Punch snipe / a body that trades well and helps the mirror.
  • Brigette ×4 / N ×3 / Cynthia ×2 / Guzma ×2 / Sycamore ×1 / Mallow ×1 / Kukui ×1 / Acerola ×1 — a rich toolbox Supporter suite (all tutorable via Tapu Lele). Mallow finds combo pieces; Kukui (+20) hits exact KO numbers; Acerola resets/denies prizes.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Evosoda ×2 / Field Blower ×2 / Enhanced Hammer ×1 / Multi Switch ×1 — search, Supporter-free evolves, Tool/Stadium removal, special-Energy strip, and a switch trick.
  • Choice Band ×1 / Float Stone ×2 / Parallel City ×1 — +30 vs. GX/EX, free retreat, bench/ damage control.
  • 4 DCE + 2 Strong Energy + 2 Fighting — DCE powers Riotous Beating; Strong (+20) and Fighting fuel Lycanroc's Claw Slash and the Buzzwole tech to exact numbers.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Brigette for Zorua ×2–3 + Rockruff; wide board, pass.
  • Turn 2: evolve Zoroark-GX, Trade to dig, and either Riotous Beating or evolve Lycanroc-GX (Bloodthirsty Eyes) to gust + set up a KO.

The core loop

Trade every turn for gas; use Bloodthirsty Eyes / Guzma to drag up the exact target; KO it with Riotous Beating, a buffed Claw Slash, or Dangerous Rogue-GX. Mew-EX answers Buzzwole; Puzzle of Time recurs your pieces; N disrupts. With Lycanroc's gust you dismantle the opponent's plan and race to 6 prizes in ~3 big turns.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Bloodthirsty Eyes wins games — evolve Lycanroc at the moment that drags up a KO the opponent can't afford to lose; it's a Supporter-free gust, so you can still N/Cynthia.
  • Brigette turn 1 — the setup that powers everything.
  • Know your Claw Slash math — Choice Band + Kukui + Strong turns 110 into a 1HKO on Zoroark/other GX.
  • Mew-EX vs. Buzzwole — copy the right attack to 1HKO the Psychic-Weak Beasts.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting (Buzzwole/Lycanroc, Buzz/Shrine) — Zoroark-GX is Fighting-Weak; the shared Zoroark problem (your own Buzzwole/Mew-EX techs help).
  • Dedicated OHKO decks (Rayquaza-GX/Vikavolt, Ultra Necrozma) — can out-damage your 2HKO grind; a shaky matchup.
  • Garbotoxin (opposing Garbodor) — turns off Trade; Field Blower or race.
  • Shrine of Punishment — chip damage on your GX-heavy board.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Trade: discard 1 → draw 2; Riotous Beating: 20 × your Pokémon.
  • Bloodthirsty Eyes: free gust on evolving Lycanroc-GX.
  • Claw Slash: 110 base → ~180 with Choice Band + Kukui + Strong; Dangerous Rogue-GX scales with their bench.
  • Puzzle of Time (×2): retrieve any 2 cards.

Sources: Flipside ("An Early Tier List" / ZoroRoc analyses), PokéBeach tier list, Bulbapedia (Zoroark-GX, Lycanroc-GX), Pokemon.com. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
blacephalon_2019:
How to Pilot — Blacephalon GX (2019)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2019 (TEU–UNM). Blacephalon-GX + Naganadel — the Fire "Mind Blown" deck that OHKOs anything by sending Fire Energy to the Lost Zone.

One-line identity

A Fire OHKO deck built on Blacephalon-GX's Mind Blown: send any number of your Fire Energy to the Lost Zone to deal 50 damage each, scaling to a one-hit KO on literally anything in the format. Naganadel's Charging Up streams Energy back onto the board so you can keep Mind Blowing, and Beast Ring refuels explosively once the opponent is on the back foot. Louis and I (Vig) played this exact 60 at the 2019 World Championships in Washington, DC — Louis piloted it to Day 2.

The meta it lived in

2019 Standard was the Tag Team GX / Welder era — Reshiram & Charizard-GX, Pikachu & Zekrom-GX, Malamar/Giratina, Mewtwo & Mew-GX, and Zoroark variants. Blacephalon-GX punched into all of it because Mind Blown's damage is effectively uncapped — it OHKOs a 270-HP Tag Team as easily as a 120-HP Basic, and Naganadel's Energy acceleration + Beast Ring meant it could reload fast. The catch that defines how you pilot it: Mind Blown's Energy goes to the Lost Zone, not the discard.

The Lost Zone rule (the single most important thing)

Mind Blown sends Fire Energy to the Lost Zone — a zone you can never recover from. This is completely different from a discard (which Fire Crystal, Energy Retrieval, etc. can recycle). Consequences for how you play:

  • The deck runs a very high basic Fire Energy count precisely because every Mind Blown permanently removes Energy from the game. You are burning a finite resource.
  • You must plan your whole game around having enough Fire to take all six prizes before you fizzle out. Count it: roughly how many Mind Blowns (and how big) will it take to win, and don't Lost-Zone more Energy than a KO actually requires. Over-committing Energy early is how this deck loses — it runs dry at 2 prizes and can't close.
  • Because you can't get Lost-Zoned Energy back, Naganadel's Charging Up (which moves Energy from the discard, not the Lost Zone) and your Energy-search Items are how you keep Energy flowing onto the board, but they can't undo the Lost Zone — they only help you deploy what you still have.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Blacephalon-GX (52/214) ×4 — the attacker. Mind Blown: send any number of your Fire Energy to the Lost Zone → 50 damage each (OHKOs anything with enough Energy). Blazer is a cheap early attack, and Burst-GX (the GX attack) can take a quick prize / close a game. 180 HP.
  • Poipole (107/214) ×3 → Naganadel (108/214) ×3 — the Energy engine. Charging Up (Naganadel's Ability): once per turn, attach a Fire Energy from your discard to a benched Pokémon — this is how you keep putting Energy onto Blacephalon-GX to fuel the next Mind Blown without spending your manual attachment. The Poipole/Naganadel line is the deck's backbone.
  • Welder ×4 — attach 2 Fire from hand + draw 3 — the burst that lets you load Blacephalon-GX and Mind Blown the same turn, plus your main draw.
  • Beast Ring ×4 — when the opponent has 4 or fewer prizes, attach 2 Fire from your deck to your Ultra Beasts — an explosive refuel that reloads Blacephalon-GX for a huge Mind Blown right when you're closing (helps you hit the "take the last prizes" plan without running dry).

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Dedenne-GX (57/214) ×1 — Dedechange: discard hand → draw 6; the consistency draw (a small prize liability you accept).
  • Ditto Prism Star (154/214) ×1 — flexibly completes a Naganadel line when a piece is prized/missing.
  • Meowth (125/181) ×1 → Persian-GX (149/214) ×1 — a Colorless attacker option; Persian-GX's Cat Crash / Catwalk-GX utility and a body that doesn't cost Fire.
  • Pokémon Communication ×4 / Mysterious Treasure ×4 — search Pokémon (Blacephalon-GX, the Naganadel line, techs); Treasure also digs.
  • Custom Catcher ×4 — gust (two at once) — pull up the exact Pokémon you want to Mind Blown for a KO, or snipe a support Pokémon.
  • Cynthia ×4 / Lillie ×2 — draw. Lillie front-loads early hands; Cynthia refuels mid-game.
  • Ultra Space ×1 — search an Ultra Beast (Blacephalon-GX/Naganadel) — speeds setup.
  • Giant Hearth ×2 / Heat Factory Prism Star ×1 — Fire search Stadiums — get basic Fire into hand/onto the board to feed Mind Blown.
  • 15 Fire + 1 Beast Energy Prism Star — the high Fire count is mandatory given the Lost Zone drain; Beast Energy boosts Ultra Beasts (+30) and gives extra when Active. (Note: Beast Energy is Special — it can't be moved by Charging Up like a basic Fire can.)

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: set up Poipole → Naganadel and get Fire flowing. A strong turn: attach Fire + Welder (2 more Fire, draw 3), and either develop or take an early Mind Blown if the KO is there. Keep Blacephalon-GX safe on the bench until you can make the Energy count.
  • Don't over-commit Fire early — remember every Mind Blown Energy is gone forever; take the KO you need, not the biggest one you can.

The core loop

Stream Energy with Naganadel's Charging Up + Welder, then Mind Blown for exactly enough to KO the target (send only as much Fire to the Lost Zone as the KO requires). Custom Catcher picks off the Pokémon you want; Beast Ring reloads once they're at 4 prizes. Throughout, track your remaining Fire against the prizes you still need — the game plan is to have enough Energy left to take all six prizes, not to throw the biggest possible hit early.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Count your Fire to six prizes — the defining discipline. Know how many more KOs you need and whether you have the Energy (in play + deck + hand, not Lost Zone) to get there. Don't fizzle.
  • Mind Blown only as big as the KO needs — every extra Energy Lost-Zoned is one you can't use later.
  • Naganadel keeps Energy on the board — use Charging Up every turn to redeploy discard Fire; it's your renewable supply line (the Lost Zone is not).
  • Beast Ring is your closer — sequence prizes so it's live (opponent ≤4) for the reload that finishes the game.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Water attackers — Blacephalon-GX is Water-Weak (a Water deck 1HKOs it easily).
  • Running out of Fire — the deck's built-in clock; over-committing Energy to the Lost Zone early loses games you were winning. Resource discipline is everything.
  • Sleep / disruption (Darkrai's Abyssal Sleep) — a sleep-lock or switch-denial stalls your Mind Blown cadence; run/hold Switch.
  • Malamar/Giratina spread (Distortion Door + Spell Tag) — chip that pressures your Blacephalon-GX and complicates the race.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Mind Blown: 50 × Fire Energy sent to the Lost Zone (uncapped — OHKOs anything).
  • Blacephalon-GX: 180 HP; Burst-GX for a quick prize.
  • Welder: 2 Fire from hand + draw 3; Beast Ring: 2 Fire from deck at opponent ≤4 prizes.
  • Naganadel Charging Up: 1 Fire from discard → benched Pokémon per turn.
  • Lost Zone ≠ discard — Mind Blown Energy is gone for good; plan your Fire to cover all 6 prizes.

Sources: Bulbapedia (Blacephalon-GX / Mind Blown), Pokemon.com & PokéBeach ("Blacephalon-GX for Worlds", 2019 Worlds coverage), Flipside (Worlds 2019 breakdown). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards). Provenance: played by Vig & Louis at the 2019 World Championships, Washington, DC (Louis made Day 2).
green_s_reshiram_charizard_2019:
How to Pilot — Green's ReshiZard (2019)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2019 (TEU–UNM). The Green's Exploration control/tank variant of Reshiram & Charizard-GX — a slower, grindier take on the format's best Fire deck.

One-line identity

A tanky, Trainer-driven ReshiZard: Green's Exploration searches your deck for 2 Trainer cards — playable only while you have no Pokémon with Abilities in play — and because not a single Pokémon in this list has an Ability, Green's is always live. That turns the deck into a Trainer-toolbox engine (healing, gust, disruption) that makes a 270-HP Tag Team nearly impossible to KO. Welder still powers Flare Strike; the plan is to out-grind, not out-race.

The meta it lived in

2019 had two ReshiZard schools: the aggressive Firebox (Jirachi/Dedenne) and this one — the Green's Exploration control build. Instead of an Ability draw engine, it chains Green's Exploration + Pokégear to assemble Trainers, leaning on Mixed Herbs / Great Potion healing to make the 270-HP TAG TEAM nearly impossible to KO. It trades speed for resilience and a grindy, disruptive game.

Why Green's Exploration is always on (the deck's whole trick)

Green's Exploration reads: "You can play this card only if you have no Pokémon with Abilities in play. Search your deck for up to 2 Trainer cards, reveal them, and put them into your hand. Then, shuffle your deck." The build is designed so that clause is never a problem:

  • No Pokémon in the list has an Ability. Reshiram & Charizard-GX has none, and Volcanion's Flare Starter is an attack, not an Ability — so a benched Volcanion does not turn Green's off. There's simply nothing on your board that can ever disable Green's.
  • That means every turn you can reliably play Green's Exploration to grab exactly the two Trainers the matchup calls for. Consistency is the deck's identity — you tutor your way to the perfect answer each turn.
  • It also means you avoid the Firebox draw engines (Jirachi/Dedenne-GX) on purpose: adding an Ability Pokémon would break Green's. Your draw/consistency comes from Green's + Pokégear + Welder instead.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Reshiram & Charizard-GX (20/214) ×3 — the core/wall. Flare Strike (230), Outrage, Double Blaze-GX. 270 HP is the whole point — with healing, opponents struggle to ever KO it. No Ability (never blocks Green's).
  • Volcanion (25/214) ×4 — Flare Starter (an attack): does damage and accelerates Fire — a Fire attacker/accelerator that doesn't touch your Ability count, so it plays freely alongside Green's. (No Ability = no conflict with Green's Exploration.)
  • Green's Exploration ×4 — the engine: search 2 Trainer cards (always live here). With the deck's huge Trainer count it assembles exactly the piece you need (heal, gust, disrupt, Stadium) — and it grabs any Trainers (Items and Supporters, e.g. fetching Welder), not just Items.
  • Welder ×4 — attach 2 Fire + draw 3; still your acceleration/draw. (You often want Green's or Welder depending on the turn — Pokégear finds either.)

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Pokégear 3.0 ×4 — dig for Green's Exploration or Welder — the Supporter you need each turn.
  • Custom Catcher ×4 — the gust (two at once), fetchable in a pair with a single Green's — reach the KO target.
  • Great Potion ×4 / Mixed Herbs ×4 — the healing package: Great Potion heals 50 off a Pokémon with 3 Energy (turns 2HKOs into 3HKOs); Mixed Herbs heals cheaply. This is how the wall never dies — all Trainers, all searchable with Green's.
  • Fire Crystal ×3 / Energy Spinner ×1 / Fiery Flint ×1 — Energy recovery and fixing so you never run dry (all Trainers → Green's-able).
  • Acro Bike ×4 — dig/discard toward the empty-ish hands and setups you want.
  • Cherish Ball ×2 — search Pokémon-GX (a Trainer, so Green's can even find it).
  • Lusamine ×1 — recover 2 Supporters (Green's/Welder) from the discard for the long game.
  • Reset Stamp ×1 — hand disruption when ahead/behind.
  • Giant Hearth ×1 / Heat Factory Prism Star ×1 / Power Plant ×1 / Shrine of Punishment ×1 / Lysandre Labs ×1 — a flexible Stadium/Tool suite (all Trainers, all searchable): Power Plant shuts off opposing Abilities, Shrine chips GX boards, Lysandre Labs turns off opposing Tools. (Note: your own Power Plant is fine — you have no Abilities to lose.)
  • 11 Fire — enough for Welder + attacks (Green's finds the Energy-recovery Trainers so you can run a leaner Energy count than Firebox).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: Pokégear → Welder to charge a ReshiZard (Volcanion's Flare Starter can chip + accelerate Fire meanwhile). Green's Exploration is live from the start, so begin chaining the Trainers you need.
  • Turn 2+: Flare Strike (230) while you build the Trainer toolbox; from here you heal and grind.

The core loop

Charge ReshiZard (Welder + Volcanion), Flare Strike (230) for KOs, and use Green's Exploration every turn (it's always on) to grab exactly the two Trainers you need — Great Potion/Mixed Herbs to heal the wall back up, Custom Catcher to gust a KO, or a disruptive Stadium/Tool. The opponent has to KO 270 HP repeatedly while you heal it — an attrition war you're built to win.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Green's every turn — it's never turned off (no Abilities in the deck), so treat it as your reliable per-turn tutor; plan which two Trainers win the turn.
  • Heal proactively — Great Potion turns their 2HKO into a 3HKO; the extra turn is the plan.
  • Search the answer, not just healing — Green's finds the disruption (Power Plant/ Lysandre Labs/Custom Catcher) that swings a matchup, not only Potions.
  • Volcanion keeps the Active safe — use its Flare Starter to accelerate Fire from the bench early so a KO doesn't cost your loaded attacker.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Water attackers — ReshiZard is Water-Weak; a Water deck 1HKOs the wall regardless of healing.
  • Single-prize decks that out-tempo the setup — Blacephalon/Malamar can race before the heal-lock stabilizes.
  • Item/Trainer lock (Chaotic Swell, anti-Item) — Green's + Custom Catcher rely on Trainers; shutting them off hurts more than it hurts Firebox.
  • Reset Stamp against you — disrupts the Trainer-chaining hand.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Green's Exploration: search 2 Trainer cards — playable only with no Ability Pokémon in play, which is always the case here (this deck runs zero Abilities).
  • Flare Strike: 230; Great Potion: heal 50 (Pokémon with 3 Energy).
  • Volcanion Flare Starter: an attack (damage + Fire acceleration) — not an Ability, so it never disables Green's.
  • ReshiZard: 270 HP — the tank the whole deck protects.

Sources: Pokemon.com (2019 Worlds power rankings — Green's vs. Firebox ReshiZard), PokéBeach ("Fireball Circus" comparison), Bulbapedia (Green's Exploration, Volcanion). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
malamar_2019:
How to Pilot — Malamar (2019)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2019 (TEU–UNM). The premier single-prize deck of the format — the "gatekeeper" that kept other non-GX decks in check.

One-line identity

A resilient Psychic engine: Malamar's Psychic Recharge accelerates Psychic Energy from the discard to your bench every turn, powering an infinitely-recurring Giratina (which comes back from the discard each turn) plus a toolbox of Psychic attackers. You give up one prize at a time, recur your attacker forever, and win the prize race.

The meta it lived in

2019 Standard was full of 3-prize Tag Teams, and Malamar punished them: a non-GX deck with an attacker (Giratina) that literally can't be permanently removed is incredibly stable in a format with almost no Pokémon recovery. It was the deck to beat among single-prize strategies and a strong, proactive choice into an unknown field. Its kryptonite: healing Supporters (Mallow & Lana) that negate a 2HKO turn, and fast decks that win the race before the engine stabilizes.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Inkay (50/131) ×4 → Malamar (51/131) ×4 — the engine. Psychic Recharge (Ability): once per turn per Malamar, attach a Psychic from the discard to a benched Psychic Pokémon. Two–three Malamar = a flood of Energy every turn. The whole deck exists to get Malamar online.
  • Giratina (97/214) ×2 — the main attacker. Shadow Impact: 130 (put 2 damage counters on one of your own) — and crucially it can return itself from the discard to your hand via its own Ability loop, so you re-play and re-attack it every turn. A single-prize 130 that never runs out. Distortion Door (Ability) spreads 2 counters to set up KOs.
  • Jirachi (99/181) ×4 — Stellar Wish: look at top 5, take a Trainer. The consistency engine that finds your setup/disruption pieces (it falls asleep active — pivot with Switch/ Escape Board).
  • Mysterious Treasure ×4 / Pokémon Communication ×4 — search Psychic Pokémon (Inkay, Giratina, techs); Communication trades a Pokémon in hand for any in deck.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Mimikyu (112/181) ×1 — Copycat / its Ability punishes Tag Teams; a tech attacker that walls or answers specific threats.
  • Espurr (44/131) ×1 — a support Basic (search/utility) that feeds the Psychic engine.
  • Mew (76/214) ×1 — Psypower: place a damage counter (combos with Distortion Door/Spell Tag to reach exact KO numbers on 70-HP targets like Jirachi/Marshadow); also Bench Barrier-style protection in some builds.
  • Latios-GX (78/236) ×1 — Tag Purge anti-Tag-Team attacker / a bigger hit when you need to go over a Giratina's ceiling.
  • Spell Tag ×4 — Tool: when the holder is KO'd (or via its effect) place damage — turns Giratina's 130 into "170" over a turn cycle and sets up spread KOs.
  • Cynthia ×3 / Lillie ×4 — draw. Lillie (draw to 6/8 early) is huge on setup turns; Cynthia refuels mid-game.
  • Acro Bike ×3 / Switch ×3 / Escape Board ×2 — dig/discard (seed Psychic Energy for Recharge), and pivot the sleeping Jirachi / a stuck attacker out.
  • Reset Stamp ×2 — shrink the opponent's hand to 4 (or fewer as prizes dwindle) — a comeback/disruption button when you're behind.
  • Power Plant ×2 / Viridian Forest ×2 — Power Plant shuts off opposing Abilities (Dedenne/Jirachi-GX draw, Zoroark's Trade) — a huge Stadium vs. the field; Viridian Forest fixes/discards Energy for Recharge.
  • 9 Psychic — the only Energy; Recharge recycles it endlessly.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: bench Inkay(s) + Jirachi; Stellar Wish for setup pieces, Lillie to load your hand, and get Psychic into the discard (Acro Bike/Viridian Forest) so Recharge is live. Evolve Malamar turn 2.
  • Turn 2–3: with 2 Malamar online, Recharge floods Energy → promote Giratina → Shadow Impact for 130, then re-loop it next turn.

The core loop

Recharge Energy onto the bench every turn, attack with Giratina (130, single prize), and recur it from the discard so it never stops. Distortion Door + Spell Tag + Mew place extra counters to reach KOs on bigger Pokémon over a turn cycle. Power Plant turns off their draw engines; Reset Stamp disrupts. You trade 1 prize for their 3 (Tag Teams) and grind the race.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • 2–3 Malamar is the setup goal — one isn't enough acceleration; protect them (they're the engine, not the attacker).
  • Giratina is un-killable — lean on the loop; you rarely "run out" of attacker, which wins attrition.
  • Stack spread damage (Distortion Door/Spell Tag/Mew) a turn ahead so Shadow Impact's 130 becomes lethal on 200+ HP targets.
  • Power Plant is a weapon — shutting off Dedenne/Jirachi-GX/Trade cripples the GX decks; keep it down.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Healing (Mallow & Lana, Great Potion) — negates a 2HKO turn and stalls your race; a real problem for a 130-damage attacker.
  • Fast aggro (Blacephalon, PikaRom) — can win the prize race or 1HKO before the engine stabilizes.
  • Reset Stamp / hand disruption against you — your setup is hand-reliant early.
  • Chip-immune / high-HP walls — anything that out-heals or out-sizes 130-per-turn.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Psychic Recharge: 1 Psychic from discard → benched Psychic Pokémon, per Malamar, per turn.
  • Shadow Impact (Giratina): 130 (+2 counters on your own), single prize, self-recurring.
  • Distortion Door / Spell Tag / Mew: place counters to reach exact KO numbers.
  • Setup: turn-2 Malamar, 2–3 Malamar for full acceleration.

Sources: SixPrizes ("Are You Squidding Me…", "Solidified Standard"), Pokemon.com 2019 Worlds coverage, Bulbapedia. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
perfection_mewtwo_2019:
How to Pilot — Perfection (Mewtwo & Mew-GX) (2019)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2019 (TEU–UNM, the Tag Team / Welder era). "Perfection" / "Fire M&M's." Mewtwo & Mew-GX won the 2019 World Championship in Henry Brand's hands and put six players into Worlds Top 8 — each with a different attacker suite.

Why this deck is special. Almost every other deck has one or two attackers. Perfection has a dozen, because Mewtwo & Mew-GX's Perfection Ability lets it use the attack of ANY Pokémon-GX on your Bench or in your discard pile. Your whole deck is a menu of GX attacks, and you pick the perfect one for the board in front of you — every turn, off a single 270-HP body, all as Psychic-type damage. Mastering this deck is mastering when to reach for each tool. This guide goes card-by-card through the toolbox and the niche each one fills.

One-line identity

A single 270-HP Tag Team that copies the entire format's best GX attacks. Welder accelerates 2 Fire a turn (and draws 3), so even a lightly-charged Mewtwo & Mew-GX can fire a 270-damage Sky Judgment, a healing Miraculous Duo, a bench-wiping Cross Division, or a shutdown Tag Purge — whatever the matchup demands.

The meta it lived in

2019 Standard was the Tag Team GX format: Reshiram & Charizard-GX (Fire), Pikachu & Zekrom-GX (Lightning), Malamar/Giratina (single-prize Psychic), Blacephalon (single-prize Fire OHKOs), and Zoroark variants. Perfection sat above all of them because it could answer each with a different attack from the same card. Its structural weaknesses were narrow: decks that hit 270 for a 1HKO (Reshiram & Charizard with Ninetales), Power Plant (Stadium that shuts off your GX Abilities — i.e., Dedenne/Jirachi draw), and being Psychic-Weak in the mirror (which Jirachi-GX fixes).

The core engine (in every Perfection list)

  • Mewtwo & Mew-GX (71/236) ×4 — the deck. Perfection (Ability): its attacks are the attacks of your Pokémon-GX on the Bench/in the discard. 270 HP means most decks can't OHKO it. Why four? It's your only "real" attacker, you burn through them, and prizing one hurts — running four keeps your start-with-one odds high (~48%) and lets you discard extras to fuel other lines. You typically use 2–3 per game, retreating a damaged one to a fresh one to dodge a KO.
  • Welder ×4 — the heart. Attach 2 Fire from hand to 1 Pokémon and draw 3. It's energy acceleration and your main draw engine in one Supporter — the reason a Fire base powers Psychic attacks. You want Welder turn 1 almost every game; the whole Trainer suite is built to find it.
  • Dedenne-GX (57/214) ×3 — Dedechange: discard your hand, draw 6. Refuels an empty hand without using your Supporter (so you can Dedechange then Welder the same turn — the explosive setup turn). Bonus: it discards your evolution-GX attackers so Perfection can copy them. Miraculous Duo-GX (its GX attack) is a fine emergency (damage + heal your whole board). Warning: 160 HP and 2 prizes — a bench liability vs. snipe.
  • Jirachi (99/181) — not in this exact list, but the archetypal partner — Stellar Wish: look at top 5, take a Trainer. (This list leans on Dedenne + heavy Item search instead; see Trainers below.)

The attacker toolbox — and the exact niche of each card

Perfection lists sort their GX attackers into four jobs: heavy hitters, energy accelerators, bench hitters, and protective/utility attackers. Here's every attacker in this build and precisely when you reach for it:

  • Reshiram & Charizard-GX (20/214) ×1 — the flexible heavy hitter.
  • Flare Strike (230): your bread-and-butter 1HKO on non-Tag-Team Pokémon. Downside (can't use it next turn) barely matters — you just copy a different attack next turn.
  • Outrage: cheap 2-Energy damage that scales with damage on Mewtwo & Mew-GX — punishes an opponent who chipped you but didn't KO.
  • Double Blaze-GX (also copyable!): a 6-Energy 1HKO that, boosted, punches through an opposing Tag Purge / damage-prevention — critical in the mirror and vs. other Tag Teams.
  • Solgaleo-GX (SM104) ×1 — the accelerator/refueler.
  • Turbo Strike: solid damage and attach a basic Energy from your deck to a benched Pokémon. This is your "I had to discard a ton of Energy" recovery turn — the default attack when you can't get a clean KO, quietly charging your next attacker so you never stall. Think "this is a Solgaleo game" whenever you dump Energy early.
  • Magcargo-GX (44/214) ×1 — the uncapped nuke.
  • Lava Flow: scales with Energy — with enough Fire (Welder + Giant Hearth + Fire Crystal) it 1HKOs literally anything, including a full-HP Tag Team. Even un-used, Burning Magma-GX (its GX attack) threatens damage that forces aggressive decks (Blacephalon, Mad Party) to play conservatively. Your ceiling attacker.
  • Latios-GX (78/236) ×1 — the protective attacker & ideal starter.
  • Tag Purge (120): does damage and prevents damage from your opponent's Tag Team Pokémon next turn — lets your Mewtwo & Mew-GX tank an opposing PikaRom/ReshiZard/mirror attacker. The premier anti-Tag-Team tech.
  • Clear Vision-GX: shuts off the opponent's GX attacks for the game — stops Tag Bolt-GX (PikaRom), Double Blaze-GX (ReshiZard), Cross Division, etc. from ever going live.
  • Bonus: free retreat, so it's a great card to start with (retreat into your setup).
  • Espeon & Deoxys-GX (72/236) ×1 — the bench hitter / prize-swinger.
  • Cross Division-GX: place 10 damage counters (20 if you have 3 extra Energy) anywhere across the opponent's board. Finish a damaged attacker and wipe a bench of low-HP Basics (Inkay, Ditto, Pidgey) before they evolve — a multi-prize turn out of nowhere. Great vs. evolution decks and Pidgeotto Control.
  • Naganadel-GX (160/236) ×1 — the snipe.
  • Venom Shot (170 to ANY Pokémon): reaches a benched threat without needing two Custom Catchers — snipes a Dedenne-GX for 2 prizes, or a damaged attacker they retreated to safety. Bodies Malamar (picks off benched pieces). Its inclusion "needs no justification."
  • Jirachi-GX (79/236) ×1 — the mirror/Malamar insurance.
  • Psychic Zone (Ability): removes Psychic Weakness from all Pokémon in play — turns off the mirror's and PikaRom-teched Mewtwo & Mew-GX's Psychic revenge-KO. Against Malamar, keep it benched at all times. In the mirror, drop it the moment you see a Psychic-type revenge KO coming (via their Energy Switch/Tag Switch transition). Tag Bolt-GX-style utility.
  • Cobalion-GX (106/181) ×1 — the clincher & anti-Raichu.
  • Iron Rule-GX: gives you an uninterrupted extra turn to attach one more Energy — the "one more turn to assemble the KO with no room for error" button in tense endgames (a better Altaria-GX). Metal Symbol (Ability, with Metal Energy) shines vs. Raichu & Alolan Raichu-GX's Tandem Shock paralysis.
  • Marshadow (81/214) ×1 — the Stadium answer.
  • Resetting Hole (Ability): discard the Stadium in play. Your searchable out to Power Plant, which is the single most dangerous counter to this deck (it shuts off Dedenne/ Jirachi-GX Abilities). Also clears opposing Giant Hearth/Thunder Mountain, etc.

The Trainer engine (why the odd counts)

  • Pokégear 3.0 ×4 — dig for a Supporter (almost always Welder). More outs to the turn-1 Welder = a more consistent deck.
  • Acro Bike ×4 — draw 2, keep 1: another way to find Welder and to dig/discard, seeding attackers and Energy where you want them.
  • Cherish Ball ×4 — search any Pokémon-GX (Mewtwo & Mew-GX, Dedenne-GX, any attacker); the primary way to find a toolbox piece.
  • Mysterious Treasure ×3 — discard a card → search a Psychic or Dragon Pokémon. Doubles as a way to put an evolution-GX attacker into the discard so Perfection can copy it.
  • Electromagnetic Radar ×1 — discard 2 → search 2 Pokémon-GX (great for Dedenne + a discard attacker at once).
  • Custom Catcher ×4 — gust. Needs two at once to gust a benched Pokémon, which is why the count is high; huge for reaching KOs the opponent tried to protect. (Naganadel-GX's Venom Shot covers this when you only have one.)
  • Bill's Analysis ×2 — draw depth (look at 6, take 2) — conserves resources and finds the specific piece a random draw would miss.
  • Switch ×2 — pivot out of a bad active or a status/gust trap; also gets a fresh Mewtwo & Mew-GX active.
  • Fire Crystal ×1 — return 3 Fire from discard to hand — combos with Welder and lets you be "careless" with Energy earlier (you'll get it back for the big Lava Flow / Sky Judgment turn).
  • Giant Hearth ×3 — Stadium: search 2 Fire to hand. "Welder's best friend" — both a discard outlet for Perfection and Energy fuel; keeps the Fire flowing for Lava Flow.
  • Viridian Forest ×1 — thin/fix Energy (discard a card, get a basic Energy) and a Stadium to overwrite an opponent's.
  • 8 Fire + 3 Psychic — Fire powers Welder and every Fire attack; Psychic enables the Psychic-cost attackers (Naganadel-GX's Venom Shot, Espeon & Deoxys-GX, and Mewtwo & Mew-GX's own Miraculous Duo-GX) — don't cut it to zero, those attacks matter.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 dream: get Welder (via Pokégear/Acro Bike). If your hand's bad, Dedenne-GX Dedechange for a fresh 6 then Welder — attach 2 Fire to Mewtwo & Mew-GX, draw 3, and you're online. Meanwhile Cherish Ball / Mysterious Treasure put the right GX attacker in play or the discard so Perfection can copy it.
  • Starter note: opening Latios-GX is fine (free retreat), and it discards nothing you need — retreat into your setup and it's a live Tag Purge later.

The core loop

Each turn: read the board → pick the attack. Need a clean 2-prize KO on a non-Tag-Team? Flare Strike. On a Tag Team? Load up for Sky Judgment/Double Blaze or Lava Flow. Getting raced by another Tag Team? Tag Purge (tank it) or Clear Vision-GX (shut their GX off). Facing a wide low-HP bench or Malamar? Cross Division / Venom Shot for multi-prize or snipes. Dumped Energy? Turbo Strike to refuel. Power Plant down? Marshadow Resetting Hole. Then Welder + attach to fund next turn, and rotate to a fresh Mewtwo & Mew-GX if the active is nearly dead.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Welder first, always. Your consistency, acceleration, and draw all run through it. Dedechange → Welder is the explosive turn.
  • Seed the discard. Perfection copies attackers in the discard too — Mysterious Treasure/Acro Bike/Giant Hearth aren't just draw, they're how you "install" Flare Strike, Turbo Strike, Lava Flow into your menu.
  • Match the tool to the matchup (the whole skill of the deck):
  • vs. ReshiZard / big Tag Teams: Tag Purge to tank, Clear Vision to shut off Double Blaze, Double Blaze yourself to punch through their Tag Purge.
  • vs. PikaRom: Clear Vision-GX (deny Tag Bolt-GX), Jirachi-GX vs. their Psychic tech, Cobalion vs. Raichu paralysis.
  • vs. Malamar/Giratina: Jirachi-GX benched (kill their Psychic revenge), Venom Shot / Cross Division to snipe their fragile pieces, Great-value 1HKOs to win the prize race.
  • vs. Blacephalon: race with clean KOs; Magcargo's Burning Magma-GX threat makes them play conservatively.
  • vs. Power Plant decks: Marshadow Resetting Hole is your out — find it.
  • Protect your bench. Dedenne-GX (2 prizes, 160 HP) is a snipe magnet — don't over-bench GX support you can't afford to lose.
  • Count to 270. That's the 1HKO number on a Tag Team; know whether this turn's attack + Energy gets you there or whether you Turbo Strike/Tag Purge and wait.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Decks that hit 270 (Reshiram & Charizard-GX + Ninetales/Victini) — Nine Temptations drags your damaged Mewtwo & Mew-GX up (negating the "hide on bench" plan) and 1HKOs you; the most dangerous matchup.
  • Power Plant — shuts off Dedenne/Jirachi-GX Abilities (your draw/insurance); you must find Marshadow.
  • Psychic Weakness in the mirror / vs. Psychic-tech PikaRom — answered by Jirachi-GX, but a mis-sequenced game loses to a revenge KO.
  • Reset Stamp late — shrinks your hand when you most need Welder; run enough Welder/Pokégear and lean on Dedechange to dig back.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Perfection: use the attack of any of your Pokémon-GX on Bench or in discard.
  • Welder: attach 2 Fire from hand + draw 3 (Supporter).
  • 1HKO benchmarks: 230 Flare Strike (non-Tag-Team), 270 Sky Judgment / big Lava Flow (anything).
  • Key techs: Tag Purge (tank Tag Teams), Clear Vision-GX (deny their GX attack), Cross Division-GX (spread 10–20 counters), Venom Shot (170 anywhere), Resetting Hole (kill Power Plant), Psychic Zone (remove Psychic Weakness).

Sources: Pokemon.com ("Mewtwo & Mew-GX Deck Strategy", "A World of Surprises" — Brand 1st 2019 Worlds), SixPrizes ("Fire M&M's"), PokéBeach ("Mew Mew Tactical", "Mimicking Mind Moves"), Flipside (Worlds 2019 Top-8 breakdown). Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
pikarom_judge_2019:
How to Pilot — Pikarom Judge (2019)

Format: Sun & Moon-on Standard 2019 (TEU–UNM). "PikaRom" — the aggressive Lightning Tag Team deck, here in a disruptive Judge build.

One-line identity

Explosive turn-1 aggression with hand disruption: Pikachu & Zekrom-GX's Full Blitz attacks and accelerates 3 Lightning on turn 1, Electropower spikes damage for 1HKOs, and Judge keeps resetting the opponent to 4 cards so they can't stabilize. Hit huge, early, and often while their hand stays small.

The meta it lived in

PikaRom was the premier Tag Team aggro deck — a turn-1 Full Blitz sets up a turn-2 Tag Bolt-GX or a huge Fusion Strike-style swing. The Judge engine (with Volkner to find your Items) leans into disruption: keep the opponent's hand at 4 while you power through with a wide Lightning toolbox (Zeraora-GX, Raichu & Alolan Raichu-GX). It races the Fire decks and punishes slow setups.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Pikachu & Zekrom-GX (33/181) ×2 — the core. Full Blitz: 150 and attach 3 Lightning from your deck to it — the turn-1 explosion that sets up everything. Tag Bolt-GX: 200 + 170 to a benched Pokémon (the +Energy version) — a double-KO GX attack. Needs to attack the turn Full Blitz sets it up.
  • Dedenne-GX (57/214) ×4 — Dedechange: discard hand, draw 6 — the draw engine that digs to your combo (and a Lightning body). Four copies for consistency.
  • Raichu & Alolan Raichu-GX (54/236) ×2 — Tandem Shock: 150 + Paralysis (with extra Lightning) — a lockdown attacker that stalls while you set up; a second Tag Team threat.
  • Zeraora-GX (86/214) ×2 — Thunderclap Zone (Ability): your Lightning Pokémon with a Lightning Energy retreat for free — mobility for the whole team; Plasma Fists a solid attack and Full Voltage-GX for a big hit.
  • Electropower ×4 — Item: +30 damage this turn (stackable) — the 1HKO enabler that turns Full Blitz/Tandem Shock into exact KO numbers on Tag Teams.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Hoopa (140/236) ×1 — Scoundrel Guard (can't be damaged by GX/EX in some lines) / a bench attacker; utility body.
  • Marshadow (81/214) ×1 — Resetting Hole: discard the Stadium — your out to Power Plant (which shuts off Zeraora/Dedenne Abilities) and opposing Stadiums.
  • Mew (76/214) ×1 — Bench Barrier-style protection: shields your bench from snipe (protect Dedenne-GX/Tapu Koko from Tag Bolt/Custom Catcher pings).
  • Tapu Koko Prism Star (51/181) ×1 — Aeroschnitzel / its Ability accelerates Lightning; a Prism Star accelerator/attacker.
  • Tapu Fini (53/236) ×1 — tech vs. Ultra Beasts / a utility attacker (cheaper vs. UB decks).
  • Judge ×4 / Volkner ×4 / Cynthia ×1 — the Supporter suite. Judge disrupts (both hands to 4) and fixes your own hand; Volkner searches a Fire/Lightning Item + an Energy (finds Electromagnetic Radar/Electropower).
  • Custom Catcher ×4 / Electromagnetic Radar ×4 — gust (2 at once) and Radar (search 2 Lightning GX for a discard of 2) — the search that finds your Tag Teams fast.
  • Energy Switch ×2 / Tag Switch ×1 / Switch ×1 — move Lightning to the right attacker and pivot (dodge gusts, un-paralyze).
  • Reset Stamp ×1 / Stadium Nav ×1 — hand disruption / Stadium search.
  • Lysandre Labs ×2 / Power Plant ×1 / Thunder Mountain Prism Star ×1 — Lysandre Labs turns off opposing Tools; Thunder Mountain reduces your Lightning attack costs (cheaper Full Blitz/Tandem Shock); Power Plant shuts off opposing Abilities.
  • 11 Lightning — fuel for Full Blitz's acceleration and attack costs.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (dream, going first or second): Full Blitz — 150 damage and 3 Lightning onto Pikachu & Zekrom-GX. You're now set up for a turn-2 Tag Bolt-GX or a big Electropower'd swing. Dedechange to dig if the hand's rough.
  • Judge on a turn the opponent is trying to set up — keep them at 4 cards.

The core loop

Full Blitz turn 1 → Tag Bolt-GX / Electropower'd attacks for KOs → Judge every time the opponent tries to rebuild, keeping their hand small. Zeraora free retreat lets you pivot attackers; Raichu Tandem Shock paralysis-locks while you reload; Custom Catcher/ Marshadow reach the pieces you need. Race to 6 prizes before they stabilize.

Priorities / rules of thumb
