Fill the bench with Fusion Strike Pokémon → Fusion Strike System (multiple Genesect = draw your deck) → Cross Fusion Strike → Techno Blast (210) every turn → Power Tablet to reach OHKO numbers → retreat Mew VMAX and return to reset the attack clause. Boss's Orders picks the target; Rose Tower answers their Path to the Peak. You out-draw and out-tempo the field.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Bench Fusion Strike Pokémon early — Fusion Strike System draws equal to your Fusion Strike count; a wide board = a huge hand.
  • Don't over-Genesect — 2–3 is usually right; too many and you draw fewer new cards (your hand's already full).
  • Reset Techno Blast with free retreat — Mew VMAX's 0 retreat means you attack every turn.
  • Answer Path to the Peak — Rose Tower/Stadium war is mandatory; without your Abilities the deck stalls.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Darkness (Single Strike Houndoom/Umbreon VMAX) — Mew VMAX is Dark-Weak; a dedicated Dark deck 1HKOs it.
  • Path to the Peak — shuts off Fusion Strike System (your whole engine); you must bounce it.
  • Fan of Waves / Special-Energy denial — the deck leans on Special Energy (Fusion Strike/ Double Turbo); stripping it slows you.
  • Being teched against — its popularity means opponents pack specific counters; a metagame problem more than a card problem.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Fusion Strike System: draw up to your Fusion Strike Pokémon count, once per Genesect per turn.
  • Cross Fusion Strike → Techno Blast: 210 for 2 Energy (330 with 4 Power Tablet).
  • Elesa's Sparkle: 2 Fusion Strike Energy from deck → 2 Pokémon.
  • Mew VMAX: 310 HP, free retreat (reset Techno Blast by retreating).

Sources: Pokemon.com (Fusion Strike Mew/Genesect strategy — Reklev), pokemoncard.io ("A Fusion Cocktail"), Flipside ("Countering Mew VMAX"), win.gg / Deltia's. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
ice_rider_palkia_2022:
How to Pilot — Ice Rider Palkia (2022)

Format: Sword & Shield-on Standard 2022 (BRS–on, the VSTAR/Inteleon era). This build mirrors Rikuto Ohashi's 2022 World Championship-winning Ice Rider Palkia deck.

One-line identity

A Water toolbox with two big hitters and the format's best engine: Origin Forme Palkia VSTAR accelerates Water (Star Portal) and hits up to ~290 with Subspace Swell + Choice Belt off a packed bench, while Ice Rider Calyrex VMAX OHKOs for 250 on just 2 Energy. The Inteleon (Shady Dealings) engine searches the exact Trainers you need every turn — making it consistent, flexible, and brutal.

The meta it lived in

2022 Standard revolved around Arceus VSTAR (the Starbirth toolbox), Mew VMAX (Fusion Strike), Origin Forme Palkia VSTAR/Inteleon, and Rapid Strike Urshifu. Ice Rider Palkia sat at the top: Palkia VSTAR's Star Portal Energy acceleration plus the Inteleon engine gave it both power and the best consistency in the format. Rikuto Ohashi took it all the way to the 2022 World Championship in London — Subspace Swell + Choice Belt off a full bench one-shots almost anything.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Origin Forme Palkia VSTAR (40/189) ×3 (from Origin Forme Palkia V ×3) — the core. Subspace Swell: 60 + 20 per Pokémon on both benches (up to ~200, +30 Choice Belt = ~230–290) for 2 Water — scales huge with a packed board. Star Portal (VSTAR Power, once per game): attach 3 Water from discard to your Water Pokémon — the acceleration that powers everything.
  • Ice Rider Calyrex VMAX (46/198) ×1 (from Ice Rider Calyrex V ×1) — the second hitter. Max Lance: 250 for 2 Water (discard 2 Water) — OHKOs most V/VSTAR on a tiny Energy cost. A one-of you set up when you want a big clean KO.
  • Sobble (41/198) ×4 → Drizzile (56/202) ×3 → Inteleon (58/202) ×2 — the engine. Shady Dealings (Drizzile searches 1 Trainer on evolve; Inteleon searches 2) — grabs the exact card you need (Boss's Orders, Path to the Peak, an Energy card, Irida). Sobble's Keep Calling fills the bench (which also grows Subspace Swell).
  • Irida ×3 / Melony ×2 — Water support Supporters: Irida searches a Water Pokémon + a Water Item (Capacious Bucket); Melony draws 3 + attaches a Water from discard to a benched Water V/VSTAR — turn-by-turn acceleration.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Manaphy (41/172) ×1 — Wave Veil (Ability): protects your bench from snipe (crucial vs. Rapid Strike Urshifu's G-Max Rapid Flow and Inteleon's Double Gunner).
  • Radiant Greninja (46/189) ×1 — Concealed Cards (discard an Energy → draw 2): a draw/ Energy-cycling engine; Moonlight Shuriken as an emergency two-target attack.
  • Boss's Orders ×2 / Leon ×1 / Marnie ×1 / Professor's Research ×1 / Roxanne ×1 — gust + draw + disruption. Boss's Orders pulls the KO target; Leon buffs Subspace Swell to reach numbers; Roxanne (after they take a prize) shrinks their hand to 4 + draws you 6 — a strong comeback button.
  • Evolution Incense ×3 / Level Ball ×3 / Quick Ball ×3 / Capacious Bucket ×2 / Battle VIP Pass ×1 / Ultra Ball ×1 / Rare Candy ×1 — search the Inteleon line and Water Energy; Battle VIP Pass (turn-1 only) benches 2 Basics for an explosive start; Rare Candy jumps Sobble→Inteleon.
  • Scoop Up Net ×2 — pick up an Inteleon/Sobble to re-trigger Shady Dealings (more searches) or reset Manaphy.
  • Escape Rope ×1 / Pal Pad ×1 / Choice Belt ×2 / Path to the Peak ×1 / Training Court ×1 — pivot/disrupt, recover Supporters, Choice Belt (+30 vs. V/VSTAR — the number-fixer), Path to the Peak (shut off opposing Abilities — huge vs. Mew/Arceus), Training Court (recover Water Energy).
  • 8 Water Energy — the only Energy; Star Portal/Melony/Bucket keep it flowing.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: ideally Battle VIP Pass → bench Sobble(s) + a Palkia V; Sobble Keep Calling to widen the bench. Attach Water.
  • Turn 2: Rare Candy / evolve an Inteleon (Shady Dealings → grab what you need), Melony or Star Portal to load Water, and start Subspace Swell.

The core loop

Widen your bench (Sobble Keep Calling) to pump Subspace Swell, accelerate Water (Star Portal once, then Melony/Bucket/Irida), and swing for 200+ (Choice Belt for the OHKO number). Use the Inteleon engine to fetch exactly the Trainer each turn — Boss's Orders for the KO target, Path to the Peak to shut off Mew/Arceus Abilities, Manaphy to blank their snipe. Bring in Ice Rider Calyrex VMAX (Max Lance 250) for a big clean KO when you want it.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Fill your bench — Subspace Swell scales with total benched Pokémon; a wide board is your damage.
  • Spend Star Portal at the right time — it's once per game; use it on the turn it enables a KO or sets up two attackers.
  • Inteleon fetches the answer — search the specific out (Path to the Peak vs. Ability decks, Boss's Orders for the KO, Choice Belt for the number). This flexibility is the deck's edge.
  • Manaphy down vs. snipe decks — Urshifu/Inteleon VMAX punish an unprotected bench.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Lightning (Palkia is Lightning-Weak) — Flying Pikachu VMAX / Lightning attackers hit Palkia for Weakness.
  • Path to the Peak against you — it doesn't stop Palkia's attacks, but it shuts off your own Inteleon/Radiant Greninja Abilities; you run your own to win the Stadium war.
  • Fast OHKO decks (Mew VMAX) — can race the setup; you rely on Manaphy + consistency to keep pace.
  • Bench snipe (Urshifu/Inteleon VMAX) — without Manaphy down, your Sobble/Drizzile get picked off.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Subspace Swell: 60 + 20 × Pokémon on both benches (+30 Choice Belt) for 2 Water.
  • Star Portal (VSTAR Power): attach 3 Water from discard (once per game).
  • Max Lance (Ice Rider Calyrex VMAX): 250 for 2 Water (discard 2 Water).
  • Shady Dealings: Drizzile 1 Trainer / Inteleon 2 Trainers on evolve.

Sources: Pokemon.com (Ice Rider Calyrex/Inteleon strategy; 2022 Worlds), Pokémon Center & Bulbapedia (Rikuto Ohashi 1st 2022 Worlds Ice Rider Palkia), ClutchPoints 2022 meta. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
malamar_rapid_strike_2022:
How to Pilot — Malamar Rapid Strike (2022)

Format: Sword & Shield-on Standard 2022 (CRE–on). A Psychic Malamar engine feeding Rapid Strike / Psychic attackers, backed by the Inteleon (Shady Dealings) toolbox.

One-line identity

A Psychic-acceleration toolbox: Malamar's Psychic Recharge streams Psychic Energy from the discard onto your bench every turn, powering a rotating cast of attackers, while the Inteleon engine (Shady Dealings + Quick Shooting) searches your Trainers and pings extra damage. Spiral Energy fuels the Rapid Strike side; you set up fast and hit from multiple angles.

The meta it lived in

2022 Standard was a VSTAR/VMAX world (Arceus, Mew, Palkia, Urshifu). This Malamar build is the non-VMAX, engine-driven option: Psychic Recharge gives it acceleration and resilience that the big-Basic decks lack, and the Inteleon package (searching + Quick Shooting spread) supplies the consistency and chip damage. It's a flexible, techable deck that adapts its attacker to the matchup rather than relying on one big hitter.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Inkay (69/198) ×4 → Malamar (70/198) ×4 — the engine. Psychic Recharge (Ability): once per turn per Malamar, attach a Psychic from the discard to a benched Psychic Pokémon — multiple Malamar = a flood of Energy every turn. The heart of the deck; get 2–3 online.
  • Sobble (41/198) ×4 → Drizzile (56/202) ×3 → Inteleon (58/202) ×1 + Inteleon (43/198) ×1 — the dual engine: Shady Dealings searches Trainers on evolve, and Inteleon (43/198) Quick Shooting pings 20 to any Pokémon each turn — the chip damage that lines up KOs.
  • Remoraid (36/163) ×2 → Octillery (37/163) ×2 — Rapid Strike Search (Ability): search any Rapid Strike card (a Rapid Strike attacker, Spiral Energy, Tower of Waters) — consistency for the Rapid Strike side.
  • Fog Crystal ×4 — search a Psychic Pokémon or a Psychic Energy — the tutor that finds Inkay/attackers and fuels Psychic Recharge; the deck's key consistency Item.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Cynthia's Ambition ×4 / Korrina's Focus ×4 / Brawly ×4 — a heavy, redundant draw suite: Cynthia's Ambition (draw to 6, or 8 after a KO), Korrina's Focus (draw to hand-size +? Rapid Strike Supporter), Brawly (search 2 Fighting/Rapid Strike Pokémon). Lots of draw keeps the engine flowing without a Bibarel/Crobat Ability (which Path to the Peak would shut off).
  • Evolution Incense ×4 / Level Ball ×4 — search the Inkay/Malamar and Inteleon/Octillery lines (Level Ball grabs the low-HP Basics/Stage-1s efficiently).
  • Scoop Up Net ×4 — re-trigger Shady Dealings / pick up a chipped Pokémon / reset a piece — the toolbox loop.
  • Rescue Carrier ×2 / Ordinary Rod ×1 — recover Pokémon/Energy (recur Malamar and Psychic Energy for the long game).
  • Escape Rope ×1 / Tower of Waters ×1 — pivot / free retreat for Rapid Strike Pokémon.
  • 4 Spiral Energy + 2 Psychic Energy — Spiral Energy provides Psychic and Fighting-type Energy (fuels both the Psychic Recharge targets and Rapid Strike/Fighting attackers); basic Psychic feeds Recharge directly.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: bench Inkay(s) + Sobble + Remoraid; Fog Crystal to find a piece/Energy; get Psychic into the discard so Recharge is live.
  • Turn 2: evolve Malamar (start Psychic Recharge) and an Inteleon (Shady Dealings), and begin streaming Energy onto your attacker while Quick Shooting chips.

The core loop

Get 2–3 Malamar online → Psychic Recharge floods Energy from the discard every turn → power your chosen attacker and swing, while Inteleon Quick Shooting pings 20 to set up KOs → Fog Crystal / Octillery keep you stocked, Scoop Up Net re-triggers your Abilities, and the heavy draw suite refuels. You adapt the attacker to the matchup and grind from multiple angles.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • 2–3 Malamar is the setup goal — one isn't enough acceleration; protect them (they're the engine).
  • Keep Psychic in the discard — Recharge pulls from there; use Fog Crystal/draw to seed it.
  • Quick Shooting pings set up KOs — place the 20 where it converts a 2HKO into a KO or kills an engine Basic.
  • Draw over Abilities — the deck leans on Supporter draw (Cynthia/Korrina/Brawly) so it's resilient to Path to the Peak; use them freely.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Darkness (Malamar/Psychic attackers are Dark-Weak in spots) — Dark decks punish the Psychic side.
  • Fast OHKO VMAX decks (Mew) — can out-tempo the engine setup before it stabilizes.
  • Bench snipe / disruption — your low-HP engine (Inkay, Sobble, Remoraid) is vulnerable to Urshifu/Inteleon spread before it evolves.
  • Consistency variance — a multi-line engine deck; a clunky start (no Fog Crystal/Malamar) falls behind.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Psychic Recharge: 1 Psychic from discard → benched Psychic Pokémon, per Malamar, per turn.
  • Quick Shooting (Inteleon): 20 to any Pokémon per turn; Shady Dealings searches Trainers on evolve.
  • Fog Crystal: search a Psychic Pokémon or a Psychic Energy.
  • Spiral Energy: provides Psychic and Fighting Energy; setup goal 2–3 Malamar.

Sources: JustInBasil / Limitless 2022 Standard Malamar & Inteleon lists, Pokemon.com (Inteleon engine strategy), SixPrizes 2022 analyses. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
urshifu_vmax_2022:
How to Pilot — Urshifu VMAX (2022)

Format: Sword & Shield-on Standard 2022 (BST–on). "Rapid Strike Urshifu" — a Fighting/Water bench-snipe deck built on the Inteleon engine; a top-tier archetype since Battle Styles.

One-line identity

The premier spread/snipe deck: Rapid Strike Urshifu VMAX's G-Max Rapid Flow does 120 to two of the opponent's Pokémon for just 3 Energy (efficient thanks to Rapid Strike Energy), picking off support Pokémon and setting up multi-prize turns. The Inteleon (Shady Dealings + Quick Shooting) engine searches your Trainers and pings extra damage, and Octillery finds any Rapid Strike card on demand.

The meta it lived in

Rapid Strike Urshifu has "stood the test of time" — Azul Garcia Griego won Players Cup III with it, and it stayed top-tier through 2022 with a fantastic matchup spread (it preys on the low-HP support Pokémon every deck runs). Its edge is snipe + prize math: G-Max Rapid Flow + Quick Shooting pings line up KOs across the board that single-target attackers can't. Its kryptonite is Manaphy (bench protection) and Psychic decks (Weakness).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Rapid Strike Urshifu V (87/163) ×3 → Rapid Strike Urshifu VMAX (88/163) ×2 — the core. G-Max Rapid Flow: 120 to two of the opponent's Pokémon for 2 Fighting + 1 Colorless (discard all Energy — but Rapid Strike Energy re-arms fast). Gale Thrust: 150 for one Energy when it moves into the Active spot — an efficient single hit. A 2-2 line is enough (you rarely need two at once).
  • Sobble (41/198) ×4 → Drizzile (56/202) ×3 → Inteleon (58/202) ×2 + Inteleon (43/198) ×1 — the dual engine: Shady Dealings (Drizzile 1 / Inteleon-CRE 2 Trainers) searches your toolbox, and Inteleon (43/198) Quick Shooting pings 20 to any Pokémon each turn — the extra damage that turns G-Max Rapid Flow's 120 into KOs.
  • Remoraid (36/163) ×2 → Octillery (37/163) ×2 — Rapid Strike Search (Ability): search any Rapid Strike card (Urshifu, Rapid Strike Energy, Tower of Waters, Korrina's Focus) — the consistency glue; grabbing your Energy and pieces in one Ability.
  • Irida ×4 — search a Water Pokémon + a Water Item (the Inteleon line + Bucket/Energy); the main setup Supporter.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Manaphy (41/172) ×1 — Wave Veil: protects your bench from snipe (mirror / Inteleon pings) — and note the opponent's Manaphy is your worst enemy, so pack ways around it.
  • Medicham V (83/203) ×1 — Yoga Loop: after a KO with Psycho Punch, take a turn cycle advantage — a soft-lock engine (KO a benched Pokémon / their Manaphy and chain turns).
  • Cheryl ×2 — heal all damage off one Pokémon — resets a chipped Urshifu VMAX so it can't be 2HKO'd (a grind-winning heal).
  • Boss's Orders ×1 / Marnie ×1 / Roxanne ×1 — gust + draw/disruption; Roxanne (after they take a prize) shrinks their hand to 4 + draws you 6.
  • Scoop Up Net ×4 — re-trigger Shady Dealings / pick up a chipped Pokémon / reset Manaphy — a key toolbox loop.
  • Evolution Incense ×3 / Level Ball ×3 / Quick Ball ×2 / Energy Search ×2 / Ultra Ball ×1 / Hisuian Heavy Ball ×1 — search the lines and Energy.
  • Escape Rope ×1 / Ordinary Rod ×1 / Pal Pad ×1 / Rescue Carrier ×1 / Tool Jammer ×1 — pivot, recover Pokémon/Energy/Supporters, and Tool Jammer (shut off an opposing Tool).
  • Tower of Waters ×2 — Stadium: free retreat for Rapid Strike Pokémon (pivot Urshifu into the Active for Gale Thrust / out to reset).
  • 4 Rapid Strike Energy + 3 Fighting Energy — Rapid Strike Energy provides 2 Energy at once (re-arms G-Max Rapid Flow after the discard); Fighting for costs/Martial-Arts-Dojo math.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: bench Sobble (Keep Calling widens it) + Urshifu V + Remoraid; attach Energy.
  • Turn 2: evolve Octillery (search Rapid Strike Energy/pieces) and an Inteleon (Shady Dealings), and start Gale Thrust / G-Max Rapid Flow — pinging with Quick Shooting.

The core loop

Set up the Inteleon engine (search + Quick Shooting pings), then G-Max Rapid Flow (120×2) to snipe support Pokémon and spread — combine the pings + snipe to take multi-prize turns (e.g. KO a Sobble and a Crobat V). Rapid Strike Energy re-arms the attack; Cheryl heals your VMAX to grind; Octillery keeps you stocked. You dismantle their board from every angle and win the prize race.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Aim the snipe to line up KOs — G-Max Rapid Flow + Quick Shooting is spread math; place damage where it converts to prizes (kill their engine before it evolves).
  • Re-arm with Rapid Strike Energy — the discard on G-Max Rapid Flow is fine because 1 Rapid Strike Energy = 2 Energy back.
  • Cheryl to reset a chipped Urshifu — denies their 2HKO and keeps your attacker alive.
  • Play around / remove Manaphy — an opposing Manaphy blanks your whole snipe plan; force it Active (Boss) or attack around it.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Psychic (Mew VMAX) — Urshifu is Psychic-Weak; Mew 1HKOs it and races — the toughest matchup.
  • Manaphy (opposing) — Wave Veil blanks G-Max Rapid Flow/Quick Shooting snipe; you must answer it.
  • 3-prize liability — VMAX gives up 3; decks that OHKO VMAX (Giratina/Mew) trade up on you.
  • Consistency wobble — infamous for a great on-paper spread but occasionally clunky starts; Octillery mitigates but doesn't erase it.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • G-Max Rapid Flow: 120 to two Pokémon for 2 Fighting + 1 Colorless (discard all Energy).
  • Gale Thrust: 150 for 1 Energy when Urshifu moves into the Active spot.
  • Quick Shooting (Inteleon): 20 to any Pokémon per turn; Octillery: search any Rapid Strike card.
  • Rapid Strike Energy: provides 2 Energy (re-arms the attack).

Sources: Pokemon.com (Rapid Strike Urshifu / Inteleon VMAX strategy — Azul GG PC3), PokéBeach ("Can Lightning Rapid Strike Twice"), PoTown Store, TCGplayer. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
colorless_lugia_2023:
How to Pilot — Colorless Lugia (2023)

Format: Scarlet & Violet-on Standard 2023 (post-rotation; Paldea Evolved-era Special Energy). "Lugia VSTAR / Archeops" — the Colorless toolbox that dominated the previous format and adapted to SV with new Special Energy.

One-line identity

A Special-Energy acceleration toolbox: Lugia VSTAR's Summoning Star puts 2 Archeops from your discard onto your bench, and each Archeops's Primal Turbo attaches 2 Special Energy from deck per turn — up to 4 a turn. That fuels a rotating cast of hard-hitting Colorless attackers (Lugia itself, Snorlax, Archeops, Drapion V) that trade prizes favorably against anything.

The meta it lived in

Lugia VSTAR was the most powerful deck of the pre-SV format — the BDIF that at one point put 21 of the Top 32 at a major on the deck — and it stayed strong post-rotation. The SV rotation cost it Powerful Colorless Energy and Quick Ball, but Paldea Evolved handed it a suite of Colorless Special Energy (Jet, V Guard, Gift, Therapeutic) and Snorlax as a new attacker. Its edge is flexibility + prize trade: Summoning Star + Primal Turbo can power any Colorless attacker for the matchup, and single-prize options keep the trade favorable.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Lugia V (138/195) ×4 → Lugia VSTAR (139/195) ×3 — the core. Summoning Star (VSTAR Power, once per game): put 2 Colorless non-Rule-Box Pokémon from discard onto the bench — you use it to drop 2 Archeops. Tempest Dive: 220 + discard a Stadium (answers Path to the Peak/Temple of Sinnoh). Lugia V's Read the Wind (discard 1 → draw 3) helps pitch Archeops early.
  • Archeops (147/195) ×4 — the engine. Primal Turbo (Ability): once per turn, search 2 Special Energy from deck and attach them to 1 Pokémon. Two Archeops = 4 Special Energy a turn on top of your manual attach. It can only enter play via Summoning Star (no Fossil line), so get it into the discard early.
  • Lumineon V (40/172) ×2 — Luminous Sign: search a Supporter on entry — grabs Research/Boss/Iono; also a way to find your setup piece and a single-prize attacker.
  • Snorlax (143/196) ×2 — a bulky Colorless attacker; its high-Energy attack is trivial to power with Primal Turbo, and it's a resilient body.

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Drapion V (118/196) ×1 — a Mew VMAX counter: its attack punishes Fusion Strike/ specific decks; a tech attacker Summoning Star can even recur.
  • Wyrdeer V (134/189) ×1 — its Ability (draw) / a Colorless attacker that consolidates Special Energy into one big hit.
  • Slaking V (58/78) ×1 — a big Colorless hitter (Primal Turbo powers its cost) for a clean KO.
  • Radiant Tsareena (16/195) ×1 — Ability heals your Pokémon (deny 2HKOs / grind).
  • Pumpkaboo (76/203) ×1 — Pumpkin Pit discards a Stadium (answer Path to the Peak/Temple of Sinnoh without needing to attack).
  • Professor's Research ×3 / Iono ×3 / Boss's Orders ×3 / Professor Burnet ×1 — draw + gust + disruption. Research (draw 7, discard hand — pitches Archeops); Iono (both players shuffle-and-draw to prize count — disruption); Burnet finds Pokémon + a Basic Energy.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Capturing Aroma ×4 / Nest Ball ×2 — search + discard (Ultra Ball pitches Archeops; Capturing Aroma finds a Pokémon + a Special Energy).
  • Choice Belt ×2 — +30 vs. V/VSTAR (Tempest Dive to 250, hits KO numbers).
  • Collapsed Stadium ×2 / Path to the Peak ×2 — your Stadiums: shrink benches / shut off opposing Abilities (and win the Stadium war so opposing Path doesn't lock your VSTAR Power before Summoning Star).
  • Special Energy suite: 4 Jet + 4 Double Turbo + 2 V Guard + 2 Gift + 2 Therapeutic — Jet Energy (Colorless + free-retreat pivot), Double Turbo (2 Colorless, cheap — but −20 damage), V Guard (−30 from V attacks — tanks Lugia), Gift (Colorless + draw 2 when attached), Therapeutic (Colorless + heal/status). All accelerated by Primal Turbo.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: get a Lugia V down (Ultra Ball/Capturing Aroma) and discard 2 Archeops (Research / Read the Wind / Ultra Ball). Bench setup pieces.
  • Turn 2: Lugia VSTAR → Summoning Star for 2 Archeops → next turn Primal Turbo ×2 = 4 Special Energy → start attacking (Tempest Dive 220, or power a Snorlax/Slaking).

The core loop

Discard 2 Archeops → Summoning Star them onto the bench → Primal Turbo ×2 loads 4 Special Energy a turn onto whichever attacker the matchup wants → swing (Lugia Tempest Dive, Snorlax, Slaking, Drapion V) → Boss's Orders for the KO, Iono to disrupt, Choice Belt to reach numbers. You trade prizes favorably with a flexible attacker suite the opponent can't fully prepare for.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Two Archeops in the discard before Summoning Star — a lone Archeops is fragile and half the engine; it's usually worth waiting a turn to have both.
  • Discard Archeops aggressively — Ultra Ball/Research/Read the Wind exist to get them into the discard turn 1–2; that's the setup priority.
  • Pick the attacker for the matchup — Tempest Dive (removes their Stadium), Snorlax/Slaking (big KO), Drapion V (vs. Mew) — the toolbox is the point.
  • Protect Summoning Star from Path + Marnie/Iono — the classic counter locks your VSTAR Power turn 2; sequence around it (Pumpkaboo/your own Stadium).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Lightning (Lugia is Lightning-Weak) — Miraidon/Lightning attackers hit Lugia for Weakness.
  • Path to the Peak + Iono/Marnie — denying Summoning Star turn 2 sets you back badly.
  • Losing the Archeops — if both Archeops get KO'd (or you only ever get one down), the engine collapses.
  • Temple of Sinnoh — turns your Special Energy into nothing (no acceleration effect); Tempest Dive/Pumpkaboo answer it.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Summoning Star (VSTAR Power): 2 Colorless non-Rule-Box Pokémon from discard → bench (once per game).
  • Primal Turbo (Archeops): 2 Special Energy from deck → 1 Pokémon per turn (×2 Archeops = 4).
  • Tempest Dive: 220 + discard a Stadium (250 w/ Choice Belt).
  • Read the Wind (Lugia V): discard 1 → draw 3 (pitch Archeops).

Sources: Pokemon.com (Lugia VSTAR strategy / Silver Tempest battle pass), PTCGO Store ("Summoning Star"), TheGamer / spellmana Lugia guides, 2023 EUIC power rankings. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
gardevoir_2023:
How to Pilot — Gardevoir (2023)

Format: Scarlet & Violet-on Standard 2023 (SV base-on). "Gardevoir ex" — the standout deck of the SV era's opening, widely rated the best deck in the format at EUIC 2023.

One-line identity

A Psychic engine with near-unlimited Energy: Gardevoir ex's Psychic Embrace attaches any number of basic Psychic Energy from your discard to your Psychic Pokémon each turn (2 damage counters per attach as the cost), fueling a rotating cast of attackers — Gardevoir ex's Miraculous Magic, Zacian V, Cresselia, and single-prize hitters — while Kirlia's Refinement draws you through the deck. Consistent, flexible, OHKO-capable.

The meta it lived in

Gardevoir ex was the clear standout when Scarlet & Violet landed — an autonomous draw engine (Kirlia), massive Energy acceleration (Psychic Embrace), and OHKO power in one Stage-2 line. It was the best deck at EUIC 2023 and stayed top-tier all year. Its main trouble came from Lost Zone Sableye decks (single-prize aggro that attacks first) and Path to the Peak (it's a Rule-Box deck). It beats most multi-prize decks (Mew, Miraidon, Goodra) once set up.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • **Ralts (60/189 ×3 + 67/195 ×1) → Kirlia (68/195 ×3 + 60/198 ×1) → Gardevoir ex (86/198) ×2
  • Gardevoir (61/198) ×2 — the core. Psychic Embrace (Gardevoir ex Ability): attach any number of basic Psychic Energy from the discard to your Psychic Pokémon (2 damage counters per Energy) — effectively unlimited acceleration. Kirlia's Refinement (Ability): discard a card → draw 2 — the autonomous draw engine (why you run a fat Kirlia count). The non-ex Gardevoir (61/198) adds Shining Arcana** (a search/setup Ability) — a key consistency piece.
  • Zacian V (16/25) ×1 — Roar of the Sword (search a Trainer) + Intrepid Slash: a hard-hitting attacker Psychic Embrace can fully load for a big clean KO (and it only gives up 2 prizes).
  • Cresselia (74/196) ×1 — Full Moon loads Energy / its attack heals + evens the prize trade — an efficient attacker that also moves damage (great when you're behind).
  • Radiant Greninja (46/189) ×1 — Concealed Cards draw/cycle + Moonlight Shuriken snipe; extra consistency and a spread option.

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Mew (11/25) ×1 — a bench-sitter / utility (protection or a small attacker depending on build).
  • Manaphy (41/172) ×1 — Wave Veil: protect your bench from snipe (Lost Zone Greninja/ Sableye, mirror).
  • Lumineon V (40/172) ×1 — Luminous Sign: search a Supporter on entry (Boss/Iono/ Research) — turn-1 consistency.
  • Iono ×3 / Professor's Research ×2 / Boss's Orders ×2 — draw + gust + disruption. Iono (shuffle-draw to prize count) disrupts and refills; Boss's Orders pulls the KO.
  • Battle VIP Pass ×4 — turn-1 only: bench 2 Basics (Ralts + a support) — the ideal opener.
  • Level Ball ×4 / Ultra Ball ×3 / Fog Crystal ×2 / Rare Candy ×3 — search: Level Ball (Ralts/Kirlia), Fog Crystal (a Psychic Pokémon or Psychic Energy — fuels Embrace), Rare Candy (jump Ralts → Gardevoir ex a turn early).
  • Super Rod ×2 / Pal Pad ×1 / Lost Vacuum ×1 — recover Pokémon/Energy (recycle Psychic for Embrace), recover Supporters, and remove a Tool/Stadium.
  • Forest Seal Stone ×1 — once-per-game "search any card" on a Pokémon — grabs the exact piece.
  • Artazon ×1 / Collapsed Stadium ×1 — Stadiums: Artazon (search a non-Rule-Box Basic — more Ralts/support) and Collapsed Stadium (shrink benches; also removes your own damaged Pokémon to deny prizes), plus a counter to opposing Path to the Peak.
  • 10 Psychic + 2 Reversal Energy — a high basic Psychic count is the fuel for Psychic Embrace; Reversal Energy powers a single-prize attacker for a big comeback hit when you're behind on prizes.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Battle VIP Pass → Ralts + a support; start evolving. Get Psychic into the discard (Research/Fog Crystal) so Embrace has fuel.
  • Turn 2: Rare Candy → Gardevoir ex, start Kirlia Refinement draw, and use Psychic Embrace to load an attacker (Zacian/Cresselia/Gardevoir) for the KO.

The core loop

Draw through the deck with Kirlia Refinement → Psychic Embrace to pour discarded Psychic Energy onto your chosen attacker → swing for KOs (Gardevoir ex, a loaded Zacian V, or a Reversal-Energy single-prizer when behind) → Cresselia to even the prize trade / move damage, Boss's Orders for the target, Iono to disrupt. Recycle Psychic with Super Rod so Embrace never runs dry.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Seed the discard with Psychic — Embrace pulls from there; Research/Fog Crystal/Ultra Ball fill it. No discarded Psychic = no acceleration.
  • Mind the 2-counters cost — Psychic Embrace damages the Pokémon you load; don't over-attach onto a Pokémon you need to survive (or use it on an attacker you're about to swing with).
  • Kirlia is the engine — run/protect it — Refinement is your draw; a wide Kirlia bench keeps you flowing.
  • Answer Path to the Peak — you're a Rule-Box deck; win the Stadium war (Artazon/Collapsed) or you lose your Abilities.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Darkness / Metal (Gardevoir ex is Metal-Weak) — and Lost Zone Sableye's single-prize aggro attacks first and races you — the toughest matchup.
  • Path to the Peak — shuts off Psychic Embrace + Refinement (your whole engine); you must bounce it.
  • Fast single-prize aggro — anything that takes the early prize lead before you set up (Collapsed Stadium/Cresselia/Roxanne help you claw back).
  • Snipe on Kirlia/Ralts — killing the engine before it evolves sets you back (Manaphy only helps vs. bench spread, not gusts).

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Psychic Embrace: attach any number of basic Psychic from discard to Psychic Pokémon (2 damage counters each).
  • Kirlia Refinement: discard 1 → draw 2 (Ability, per Kirlia, per turn).
  • Rare Candy: jump Ralts → Gardevoir ex; Fog Crystal: a Psychic Pokémon or Psychic Energy.
  • Reversal Energy: powers a single-prize attacker for a comeback KO.

Sources: Pokemon.com ("Gardevoir ex Deck Strategy"), pokemontcgzone / Ultimate Guard 2023 meta, 2023 EUIC power rankings, JustInBasil. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
lost_box_kyogre_2023:
How to Pilot — Lost Box Kyogre (2023)

Format: Scarlet & Violet-on Standard 2023 (early SV, Lost Zone era). This build mirrors Shao Tong Yen's 2023 Junior World Championship-winning "Lost Box Kyogre" deck.

One-line identity

A single-prize Lost Zone toolbox with a game-ending combo: the Comfey + Colress's Experiment engine banks cards in the Lost Zone to unlock tiered payoffs (4 = Cramorant, 7 = Mirage Gate Energy acceleration, 10 = Sableye's Lost Mine spread), and once your deck is nearly empty you fire Kyogre's Aqua Storm to take up to four Prizes in one turn. Fast, flexible, and hard to race.

The meta it lived in

2023 Standard (up to Obsidian Flames) was wide-open — Gardevoir ex, Lugia VSTAR, Mew VMAX, Giratina VSTAR, Miraidon ex, and Lost Zone Box were all viable. Lost Box thrived because its single-prize attackers trade favorably against the multi-prize ex/V decks, and the Kyogre finish punishes boards full of two-prize Pokémon. Shao Tong Yen won the Junior World Championship in Yokohama with this exact 60.

The Lost Zone thresholds (the whole framework)

Cards go to the Lost Zone (unrecoverable) to unlock powers at set counts: - 4 in the Lost Zone → Cramorant's Spit Innocently (110, free) — an early attacker. - 7 → Mirage Gate (attach 2 Energy of any type from deck to 1 Pokémon) — your acceleration. - 10 → Sableye's Lost Mine (place 12 damage counters spread) — a multi-KO spread attack. Your whole game is racing to these numbers with Comfey/Colress's Experiment.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Comfey (79/196) ×4 — Flower Selecting (Ability): look at top 2, put 1 in hand and 1 in the Lost Zone — the engine that draws and banks Lost Zone cards each turn (free retreat, so you loop it). The heart of the deck.
  • Colress's Experiment ×4 — look at top 5, take 3, put 2 in the Lost Zone — the fastest Lost Zone accelerator; play it before Comfey for max information.
  • Sableye (70/196) ×2 — Lost Mine (needs 10 in Lost Zone): place 12 damage counters however you like across the opponent's board — takes multiple KOs on small targets (Comfey, Kirlia) at once.
  • Kyogre (3/25) ×1 — the finisher. Aqua Storm: needs a big Energy investment (Mirage Gate + Energy Recycler + Super Rod loop), does damage to multiple Benched Pokémon — set up perfectly it takes 4 Prizes in one turn to close the game.

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Cramorant (50/196) ×1 — Spit Innocently (110, free at 4 Lost Zone) — the turn-1/ early attacker to grab a prize or snipe.
  • Radiant Greninja (46/189) ×1 — Concealed Cards (discard Energy → draw 2) draw/cycle, and Moonlight Shuriken (discard 2 Water → 90 to two benched) as a snipe attacker.
  • Dragonite V (SWSH154) ×1 — Dragon Gale: 280 (unconditional big damage) with 30 to your own bench — the "just KO it" attacker (needs Mirage Gate Energy).
  • Raikou V (48/172) ×1 — Fleet-Footed (draw a card when it's your Active on turn 1) / Lightning Rondo — a Lightning attacker + Forest Seal Stone holder.
  • Manaphy (41/172) ×1 — Wave Veil: protect your bench from snipe (mirror / Radiant Greninja) — get it down early vs. spread decks.
  • Klara ×1 — recover 2 Pokémon + 2 Energy (or the split) from discard — a recovery Supporter for the long game.
  • Battle VIP Pass ×4 — turn-1 only: bench 2 Basics (Comfey + an attacker) — the ideal explosive opener.
  • Switch Cart ×4 / Escape Rope ×4 — free retreat / pivot to keep looping Comfey and dodge gusts (Escape Rope also disrupts).
  • Mirage Gate ×4 — the 7-Lost-Zone Energy accelerator (2 Energy of any type from deck) — powers Dragonite/Kyogre/Greninja.
  • Nest Ball ×3 / Hisuian Heavy Ball ×1 / Echoing Horn ×1 — search Basics (Heavy Ball grabs your many singletons; Echoing Horn recovers a KO'd Basic).
  • Energy Recycler ×2 / Super Rod ×2 / Pal Pad ×1 — recycle Energy/Pokémon (fuel the Kyogre combo) and recover Supporters (chain Colress's Experiment).
  • Forest Seal Stone ×2 — Tool: a once-per-game "search any card" VSTAR-Power-style effect on a Pokémon — grabs the exact combo piece.
  • PokéStop ×2 — Stadium: flip 3 cards, take Items, discard the rest — digs for Items (and can seed Energy for recycling).
  • 5 Water + 3 Psychic + 3 Lightning — spread typing for Mirage Gate flexibility (Kyogre/ Greninja Water, Raikou Lightning, etc.).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Battle VIP Pass → Comfey + an attacker; Colress's Experiment then Flower Selecting to bank Lost Zone cards. Aim to reach 4 (Cramorant online) and set up.
  • Turn 2: hit 7 (Mirage Gate) to accelerate onto Dragonite/Greninja and start taking prizes; keep looping Comfey toward 10 (Sableye).

The core loop

Race the Lost Zone with Colress's Experiment + Comfey → attack with the right single-prize piece (Cramorant early, Radiant Greninja/Dragonite mid, Sableye's Lost Mine for multi-KO) → protect your bench with Manaphy → and when your deck is nearly empty, assemble the Kyogre Aqua Storm 4-prize finish (Mirage Gate + Energy Recycler + Super Rod). You trade single prizes favorably and close with the combo.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Colress's Experiment before Comfey — see 5 cards first so Flower Selecting banks the right card.
  • Know your thresholds — 4/7/10 gate your whole game; count the Lost Zone every turn and plan which payoff you're racing to.
  • Manaphy down vs. spread — an unprotected bench of Comfey dies to Lost Mine / Moonlight Shuriken in the mirror.
  • Set up Kyogre only when you can take 4 — the combo needs the Energy loop; don't fire it half-baked.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Hisuian Goodra VSTAR / tanky single-attacker decks — hard to KO and won't give you easy prizes; a grind (Echoing Horn helps).
  • Bench snipe against your Comfey — without Manaphy, the mirror / Greninja picks apart your engine.
  • Getting stuck below a threshold — a clunky start that can't reach 4/7 leaves you passing turns.
  • Aggressive ex decks that race the setup — if they OHKO faster than you set up, the single-prize plan can fall behind.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Lost Zone thresholds: 4 = Cramorant, 7 = Mirage Gate, 10 = Sableye Lost Mine.
  • Flower Selecting (Comfey): top 2 → 1 hand, 1 Lost Zone (Ability, free retreat).
  • Colress's Experiment: top 5 → take 3, 2 to Lost Zone.
  • Kyogre Aqua Storm: the 4-prize finisher (needs the Energy loop).

Sources: Bulbapedia & Pokémon Center (Shao Tong Yen 1st 2023 Worlds Juniors — Lost Box Kyogre), tcgpark ("A Guide to Lost Zone Kyogre"), TCGplayer, PoTown Store. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
mew_fusion_strike_2023:
How to Pilot — Mew (Fusion Strike) (2023)

Format: Scarlet & Violet-on Standard 2023 (post-rotation). The Fusion Strike Mew VMAX deck carried into the SV format — still fast, consistent, and combo-driven.

One-line identity

The engine deck that survived rotation: Genesect V's Fusion Strike System draws you a fresh hand once per Genesect every turn, Mew VMAX's Cross Fusion Strike copies Genesect's Techno Blast (210) for 2 Energy, and Power Tablet (×4 in a turn) pushes it to OHKO numbers. Post-rotation it leans harder on Forest Seal Stone, Judge/Iono disruption, and Lost-Zone tools (Lost Vacuum / Lost City) for the prize-trade race.

The meta it lived in

2023 Standard was a varied field — Gardevoir ex, Lugia VSTAR, Lost Zone Box, Giratina VSTAR, Miraidon ex. Mew VMAX remained a top pick because its draw engine is still elite and it's a turn-2 attacker that races anything. The Fusion Strike Energy variant "fell out of favor late last year" then came back as the format opened up; its counters are the same as ever — Path to the Peak (shuts off Fusion Strike System) and Darkness attackers (Weakness).

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Genesect V (185/264) ×4 — the heart. Fusion Strike System (Ability): once per Genesect per turn, draw until your hand equals your Fusion Strike Pokémon in play — a huge, repeatable draw with a wide bench. Techno Blast: 210 (can't attack next turn, but Mew copies it). Bench 2–3.
  • Mew V (113/264) ×4 → Mew VMAX (114/264) ×3 — the attacker. Cross Fusion Strike: copy a benched Fusion Strike Pokémon's attack (usually Techno Blast 210) for 2 Colorless. Free retreat — retreat Mew VMAX and return it to reset the "can't attack" clause. Max Miracle (130, ignores effects) bypasses damage-prevention.
  • Meloetta (124/264) ×1 — Melodious Echo: 70 × Fusion Strike Energy on all your Pokémon — a secondary attacker that helps the prize trade vs. single-prize decks.
  • Elesa's Sparkle ×2 — attach 2 Fusion Strike Energy from deck to 2 Fusion Strike Pokémon — turn-1 acceleration.

Support / tech (why the odd counts)

  • Oricorio (42/264) ×1 — its Ability protects your bench from spread/snipe (a specific answer, e.g. vs. Lost Zone Sableye's Lost Mine spread) — a key SV-era tech.
  • Boss's Orders ×2 / Iono ×1 / Judge ×1 — gust + disruption. Iono/Judge shrink both hands (disruption that the engine recovers from better than the opponent does); Boss's Orders pulls the KO target.
  • Power Tablet ×4 — +30 to a Fusion Strike attack; stacking all four (enabled by the draw) turns 210 into 330 — OHKO on a VSTAR/VMAX.
  • Battle VIP Pass ×4 — turn-1 only: bench 2 Basics (Genesect + Mew V) — the ideal opener.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Cram-o-matic ×4 / Nest Ball ×2 — search + dig (Cram-o-matic: discard an Item, flip → search any card; also pitches a dead Battle VIP Pass).
  • Lost Vacuum ×2 / Switch Cart ×2 / Escape Rope ×1 / Pal Pad ×1 — remove a Tool/Stadium (their Path to the Peak / a Manaphy-style Tool), pivot/reset Mew VMAX, recover Supporters.
  • Forest Seal Stone ×3 — once-per-game "search any card" on a Pokémon — the SV-era consistency boost (grab Boss/Power Tablet/the missing piece).
  • Choice Belt ×2 — +30 vs. V/VSTAR (another OHKO number-fixer).
  • Box of Disaster ×1 — a Tool with disruptive/Lost-Zone utility (tech).
  • Stadium suite: 2 Lost City + 1 Path to the Peak + 1 Crystal Cave — Lost City (KO'd Pokémon go to the Lost Zone, not discard — denies opponents recovery and helps your prize trade); Crystal Cave (heal); Path to the Peak (deny opposing Abilities — and win the Stadium war so their Path doesn't stick).
  • 4 Fusion Strike Energy + 3 Double Turbo Energy — Fusion Strike Energy fuels Meloetta/the system; Double Turbo powers Cross Fusion Strike cheaply.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: Battle VIP Pass → Genesect V + Mew V; attach Energy to Mew V. Fusion Strike System to dig; Elesa's Sparkle if going second.
  • Turn 2: evolve Mew VMAX, Cross Fusion Strike → Techno Blast (210) — +Power Tablet for the OHKO.

The core loop

Fill the bench with Fusion Strike Pokémon → Fusion Strike System (multiple Genesect = draw your deck) → Cross Fusion Strike → Techno Blast (210) every turn → Power Tablet to reach OHKO numbers → retreat + return Mew VMAX to reset the clause. Boss's Orders picks the target, Iono/Judge disrupt, Lost City grinds the prize trade, Forest Seal Stone fetches the missing piece. You out-draw and out-tempo the field.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Bench Fusion Strike Pokémon early — the draw scales with your count; a wide board = a huge hand.
  • Don't over-Genesect — 2–3 is right; too many and you draw fewer new cards.
  • Reset Techno Blast with free retreat — Mew VMAX's 0 retreat means you attack every turn.
  • Answer Path to the Peak — Lost Vacuum it / win the Stadium war; without your Abilities the deck stalls.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Darkness attackers — Mew VMAX is Dark-Weak; a dedicated Dark deck 1HKOs it.
  • Path to the Peak — shuts off Fusion Strike System (your engine); you must remove it.
  • Single-prize aggro (Lost Zone Sableye) — trades favorably against your 3-prize VMAX and attacks first; Meloetta/Oricorio/Lost City help the trade.
  • Being teched against — its popularity means opponents pack Drapion V / Dark / Path specifically for you.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Fusion Strike System: draw up to your Fusion Strike Pokémon count, once per Genesect per turn.
  • Cross Fusion Strike → Techno Blast: 210 for 2 Energy (330 with 4 Power Tablet).
  • Elesa's Sparkle: 2 Fusion Strike Energy from deck → 2 Pokémon; Forest Seal Stone: search any card (once per game).
  • Mew VMAX: 310 HP, free retreat (reset Techno Blast by retreating).

Sources: Pokemon.com (Fusion Strike Mew/Genesect strategy; 2023 EUIC power rankings — Reklev), pokemoncard.io ("A Fusion Cocktail"), Flipside. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
ancient_toolbox_2024:
How to Pilot — Ancient Toolbox (2024)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2024. "Ancient Box" / Roaring Moon — this build mirrors Sakuya Ota's 2024 Junior World Championship-winning Roaring Moon deck; a fast Darkness Paradox toolbox.

One-line identity

A turbo Darkness toolbox: Professor Sada's Vitality attaches 2 basic Energy from hand (and heals), Dark Patch accelerates Darkness from the discard, and Explorer's Guidance draws — so you power up a rotating cast of Ancient Paradox attackers fast: Roaring Moon (scaling discard damage), Flutter Mane (cheap bench snipe), and Great Tusk (a big Fighting hitter). Flexible, aggressive, and consistent.

The meta it lived in

Ancient Box was a top-tier aggro-toolbox in 2024's wide field — its strength is speed + flexibility: multiple cheap Ancient attackers, each covering different targets, all powered by the same Sada's Vitality/Dark Patch engine. Flutter Mane in particular is a menace, 1HKOing V/ex Basics for a single Energy. Sakuya Ota won the Junior World Championship with a Roaring Moon build. It races most decks and picks apart benches before they set up.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Roaring Moon (109/162) ×4 — the main attacker (non-ex, single prize). Frenzied Gouging/ its scaling attack does more as your discard fills with Energy (Dark Patch/Sada feed it) — a hard-hitting single-prize body that trades up on multi-prize decks. The deck "roars back" as the discard grows.
  • Flutter Mane (78/162) ×4 — the star tech. Restart-style Ability + cheap attacks: its attack 1HKOs a Basic V/ex for very little Energy, and it snipes/pressures early. Four copies because it's the most efficient attacker in the deck.
  • Great Tusk (97/162) ×2 — a Fighting attacker. Land Collapse discards the top of the opponent's deck (mill/disruption; discards 4 if you played an Ancient Supporter) — and its Fighting typing covers Lightning/Colorless targets Roaring Moon can't.
  • Professor Sada's Vitality ×4 — the engine Supporter: attach 2 basic Energy from hand to your Ancient Pokémon + heal 40 — turbo acceleration that also keeps your attackers healthy.
  • Dark Patch ×2 — attach a basic Darkness from the discard to a benched Darkness Pokémon — extra acceleration (and it feeds Roaring Moon's discard-scaling).

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Fezandipiti ex (38/64) ×1 — Flip the Script: draw 3 after a KO last turn — comeback draw (a rare 2-prize liability you accept for the draw).
  • Radiant Greninja (46/189) ×1 — Concealed Cards draw/cycle + Moonlight Shuriken snipe (extra consistency + a spread option).
  • Ancient Booster Energy Capsule ×4 — Tool for Ancient Pokémon: +HP and an attack boost (and it's un-disrupted by Future counters) — makes your attackers bulkier and hit harder.
  • Defiance Band (169/198) ×1 — +damage when you're behind on prizes — an exact-KO/comeback tool.
  • Secret Box (163/167) ×1 (ACE SPEC) — search 4 cards (a Pokémon, an Item, a Tool, a Supporter) — the explosive setup/consistency card that assembles a perfect turn.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Explorer's Guidance ×4 — draw: shuffle hand, draw a number based on... (an Ancient draw Supporter) — the deck's main draw engine alongside Sada.
  • Boss's Orders ×1 — gust the KO target.
  • Nest Ball ×4 / Earthen Vessel ×4 / Pokégear 3.0 ×4 — search Basics (Nest), Energy (Earthen Vessel discards 1 → 2 Basic Energy, feeding the discard), and dig for Supporters (Pokégear).
  • Night Stretcher ×2 / Super Rod ×1 / Pal Pad ×2 / Superior Energy Retrieval ×1 — recover Pokémon/Energy/Supporters (recur attackers + refuel the discard-scaling).
  • Counter Catcher ×2 — a free gust when you're behind on prizes (great in an aggro deck that sometimes falls a prize behind, then swings back).
  • Ultra Ball ×1 / Hisuian Heavy Ball ×1 — search (Heavy Ball grabs your singleton techs).
  • PokéStop ×3 — Stadium: flip 3, take Items, discard the rest — digs for Items and feeds the discard (Energy for Dark Patch/Roaring Moon).
  • 6 Darkness Energy — powers the Darkness attackers + Dark Patch + Roaring Moon's discard scaling. (Sada attaches any basic Energy, so the count is lean.)

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1–2: bench Flutter Mane + Roaring Moon; Explorer's Guidance/Squawk-style draw to set up; Professor Sada's Vitality to attach 2 Energy and start attacking. Flutter Mane often takes an early KO on a V/ex Basic for almost nothing.
  • Seed the discard (Earthen Vessel/PokéStop) so Roaring Moon scales and Dark Patch is live.

The core loop

Turbo-power an Ancient attacker with Sada's Vitality + Dark Patch → pick the tool for the target: Flutter Mane to snipe/1HKO Basics, Roaring Moon to hit hard (scaling with your Energy discard), Great Tusk for Fighting coverage + deck disruption. Counter Catcher/ Boss pull the KO; Ancient Booster Capsule bulks and boosts; Fezandipiti/Explorer's Guidance refill. You race with single-prize attackers and trade up on their ex/V.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Flutter Mane is your efficiency — 1HKO a V/ex Basic for a single Energy; lead with it to take cheap early prizes.
  • Feed the discard — Roaring Moon scales with discarded Energy, and Dark Patch pulls from there; Earthen Vessel/PokéStop/Superior Energy Retrieval keep it stocked.
  • Sada's Vitality is your tempo — 2 Energy + heal every turn keeps attackers online and alive; it's the engine.
  • Single-prize trade — your attackers give up 1; you can trade them for their ex/V all day — don't over-bench 2-prize liabilities (Fezandipiti aside).

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fighting / specific Weakness — Darkness attackers have Weakness exposure (and Great Tusk is Fighting, itself hit by Grass/Water in spots).
  • Iron Thorns ex — Initialization shuts off Fezandipiti/Radiant Greninja Abilities (though your core attackers don't need Abilities — it's less crippling than for others).
  • Healing / high-HP walls — decks that out-heal or out-size your single-prize hits can grind you.
  • Running out of steam — an aggro deck; if you stall a turn or run low on Energy in the discard, a control deck can stabilize.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Professor Sada's Vitality: attach 2 basic Energy from hand to Ancient Pokémon + heal 40.
  • Dark Patch: 1 basic Darkness from discard → benched Darkness Pokémon.
  • Flutter Mane: cheap attacker that 1HKOs V/ex Basics for minimal Energy.
  • Roaring Moon: single-prize, damage scales with discarded Energy; Secret Box (ACE SPEC): search 4 cards.

Sources: Pokemon.com & PocketMonsters (Sakuya Ota 1st 2024 Worlds Juniors — Roaring Moon), JustInBasil (Ancient Box / SV5–SV6 decks), PokéBeach 2024 meta analyses. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
iron_thorns_2024:
How to Pilot — Iron Thorns (2024)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2024. "Crushing Thorn" — this build mirrors Fernando Cifuentes's 2024 Masters World Championship-winning Quad Iron Thorns ex deck.

One-line identity

The ultimate disruption/stall deck: Iron Thorns ex's Initialization turns off all Abilities of your opponent's Rule-Box Pokémon (ex, V, Radiant) while it's Active — a built-in Path to the Peak that never leaves. Behind that lock you strip their Energy with Crushing Hammer, drag up and KO their setup Pokémon with Pokémon Catcher/Prime Catcher, and grind out the game with a lone 230-HP wall that just won't die.

The meta it lived in

Worlds 2024's expected field — Regidrago, Lugia/Archeops, Charizard ex, Raging Bolt, Gardevoir — was entirely built on Rule-Box Abilities (Teal Dance, Infernal Reign, Fezandipiti, Radiant Greninja). Iron Thorns' Initialization shuts every one of them off, and Cifuentes rode this four-Pokémon, coin-flip-heavy disruption deck undefeated through Day 1 to the title. It's a slow, high-variance control deck that "did what Path to the Peak used to do" — except it's stapled to your Active attacker.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Iron Thorns ex (77/167) ×4 — the entire deck. Initialization (Ability, while Active): your opponent's Rule-Box Pokémon can't use Abilities — a permanent Ability-lock. Volt Cyclone: 140 + move an Energy from Iron Thorns to a benched Iron Thorns (sets up your next attacker while you swing). 230 HP makes it a real chore to KO. Four copies = you always open with one and can chain them (Legacy-style) as they trade.
  • Future Booster Energy Capsule (164/182) ×3 — Tool: +20 damage to the Active and free retreat for your Future Pokémon — negates Iron Thorns' brutal 4-Energy retreat (the key pivot enabler) and pushes Volt Cyclone to 160 (two attacks = 320).
  • Technical Machine: Turbo Energize (179/182) ×1 — a TM that accelerates Energy from deck — helps you reach Volt Cyclone's cost faster (there's no natural Energy acceleration otherwise).
  • 7 Lightning + 4 Double Turbo Energy — Lightning powers Volt Cyclone; Double Turbo (2 Energy, −20 damage) speeds the setup (Volt Cyclone still 120 with it).

The disruption package (why the counts)

  • Crushing Hammer ×4 — flip a coin; heads → discard an Energy from an opponent's Pokémon. The signature card: against Ability-acceleration decks (Gardevoir, Ogerpon) it can cost them a whole turn. High variance, high impact.
  • Pokémon Catcher ×4 — flip a coin; heads → gust a benched Pokémon Active. Chase down and KO their setup/support Pokémon while they're locked under Initialization.
  • Prime Catcher (157/162) ×1 (ACE SPEC) — a guaranteed gust + switch (no coin flip) — the premier version of the Catcher effect for the KO that matters.
  • Canceling Cologne ×2 — shut off an opposing Active Ability for a turn — critically answers Mimikyu (whose Safeguard would make it immune to your ex attacker) so Volt Cyclone can KO it.
  • Techno Radar ×2 — search 2 Future Pokémon (find your Iron Thorns fast — you only run 4).

Support / tech (why the odd 1-ofs)

  • Arven ×4 — search a Tool (Future Booster Capsule) + an Item (Crushing Hammer/Catcher) — the consistency engine that assembles your disruption.
  • Professor's Research ×3 / Judge ×3 / Boss's Orders ×3 / Iono ×1 — draw + disruption + gust. Judge (both hands to 4) is your main hand-disruption (kicks decks that need lots of cards, like Gardevoir/Lost Zone); Boss's Orders is the guaranteed gust to complement the coin-flip Catchers.
  • Colress's Tenacity ×2 — search a Stadium + an Energy (finds Lost City + Lightning).
  • Penny ×1 / Giovanni's Charisma ×1 — Penny picks up a damaged Iron Thorns (heal by re-benching / deny a prize); Giovanni's Charisma adds damage for an exact KO.
  • Pokégear 3.0 ×4 — dig for the Supporter you need (Judge/Arven/Boss).
  • Earthen Vessel ×1 / Energy Loto ×1 / Lost Vacuum ×1 — Energy search/draw, and Lost Vacuum removes a pesky Tool/Stadium (or your own Booster Capsule when you need to re-Tool).
  • Lost City ×3 — Stadium: KO'd Pokémon go to the Lost Zone (not discard) — shuts off Regidrago (no dragons in the discard to copy) and denies all recovery. A key anti-meta Stadium.

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1 (go first): open Iron Thorns ex Active (you always do — it's your only Pokémon) → Initialization immediately locks their Abilities. Start attaching Lightning; Arven for a Booster Capsule + a Crushing Hammer.
  • Turn 2–3: Crushing Hammer their Energy, Catcher/Boss their setup Pokémon, and Volt Cyclone for 140–160 while charging the next Iron Thorns.

The core loop

Keep an Iron Thorns ex Active so Initialization stays on → disrupt every turn (Crushing Hammer their Energy, Judge/Iono their hand, Catcher/Boss + KO their locked-out setup Pokémon) → Volt Cyclone to chip and pass Energy to the next Iron Thorns → Lost City to deny recovery/Regidrago. They can't use their engine; you grind them out from behind the wall.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Keep Iron Thorns Active — Initialization only works from the Active spot; Future Booster Capsule (free retreat) is how you pivot between Iron Thorns without stranding Energy.
  • Go first — you want the turn-1 lock and the Energy runway to Volt Cyclone by turn 3.
  • Disrupt the setup, not just the Active — Catcher/Boss their key Pokémon while it's locked and KO it; that's how you convert the lock into prizes.
  • Canceling Cologne for Mimikyu — the classic Iron Thorns counter (Safeguard) is answered by Cologne → then Volt Cyclone KOs it.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Mimikyu (Safeguard) — immune to your ex attacker's damage; you must Canceling Cologne it (or Boss around it) or you can't take the KO.
  • Klawf / non-Ability attackers — decks that don't rely on Abilities and set up past Initialization out-attack your weak 140 damage.
  • Gardevoir ex — can flood Energy back through Crushing Hammer and out-grind; a frustrating matchup even with the lock.
  • Coin-flip variance — Crushing Hammer/Pokémon Catcher are flips; a cold streak loses games the lock should have won.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Initialization: opponent's Rule-Box Pokémon can't use Abilities while Iron Thorns is Active.
  • Volt Cyclone: 140 + move 1 Energy to a benched Iron Thorns (160 with Booster Capsule).
  • Iron Thorns ex: 230 HP; Future Booster Capsule: +20 + free retreat.
  • Lost City: KO'd Pokémon → Lost Zone (shuts off Regidrago / denies recovery).

Sources: Bulbapedia & Pokémon Center (Fernando Cifuentes 1st 2024 Worlds Masters — "Crushing Thorn"), Limitless (Cifuentes list), PokéBeach ("Initialization"), Joseph Writer Anderson, Fanfinity. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
miraidon_2024:
How to Pilot — Miraidon (2024)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2024. "Miraidon ex" — this build mirrors Jesse Parker's 2024 Masters World Championship Semifinalist list; a fast, consistent Lightning toolbox.

One-line identity

The turbo consistency deck: Miraidon ex's Tandem Unit searches 2 Basic Lightning Pokémon straight to your bench (usable by every Miraidon, at any time), and Electric Generator accelerates Lightning Energy from your deck — so you flood the board and attack by turn 1–2 with a toolbox of Basic attackers (Miraidon's 220 Photon Blaster, Iron Hands' extra-prize Amp You Very Much, Bloodmoon Ursaluna's 240). Fast, flexible, and hard to disrupt.

The meta it lived in

2024's wide field rewarded consistency, and Miraidon has one of the best engines in the game: Tandem Unit turns a single Nest Ball into a full board, so the deck almost never bricks. It's a proactive, hard-hitting deck that races anything and swings the prize trade with Iron Hands ex. Jesse Parker took it to the Masters Semifinals at Worlds 2024. Its main predators are Iron Thorns (shuts off Tandem Unit) and Fighting-Weakness hits.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Miraidon ex (81/198) ×2 — the core. Tandem Unit (Ability): search 2 Basic Lightning Pokémon → bench — usable by every Miraidon, any time, no restriction; this is the engine that builds your whole board (one Nest Ball → a full bench). Photon Blaster: 220 (can't attack next turn — but a second Miraidon can). Only 2 copies because Tandem Unit finds the others.
  • Electric Generator ×4 — look at top 5, attach up to 2 Basic Lightning from there to your benched Lightning Pokémon — the acceleration that powers a turn-2 (or turn-1 going second) attack. Thin your deck first (Tandem Unit/Ultra Ball) to hit them.
  • Iron Hands ex (70/182) ×2 — the prize-swinger. Amp You Very Much: 120 and, if it KOs, take an extra Prize card — turns a 2-prize KO into 3, or maps out a 3-3 prize plan. Expensive, but Electric Generator/Double Turbo powers it (100 with Double Turbo, still enough for small Basics).
  • Squawkabilly ex (169/193) ×2 — Squawk and Seize: discard hand, draw 6 (a turn-1 optional-mulligan / Professor's Research) — the consistency starter that digs to your engine.

The attacker toolbox (why the 1-ofs)

  • Raikou V (48/172) ×1 — Lightning Rondo: 20 + 20 per benched Pokémon (both sides) — a cheap turn-1-going-second attacker that often 1HKOs a Basic; Fleet-Footed draws.
  • Bloodmoon Ursaluna ex (141/167) ×1 — Blood Moon: 240 (Colorless — powered by Double Turbo); Seasoned Skill drops the cost by 1 per prize the opponent has taken — a late-game finisher (with Defiance Band it hits 270 to KO a Charizard ex).
  • Zapdos (29/78) ×1 — a Lightning attacker to hit exact numbers (230-HP targets / Mimikyu).
  • Iron Bundle (56/182) ×1 — Hypnoblast/its Ability forces the opponent to switch — drag their win-condition out of the Active spot to stall/disrupt.
  • Mew ex (151/165) ×1 — Restart (fill hand) — draw when your hand is small; free-retreat pivot.
  • Fezandipiti ex (38/64) ×1 — Flip the Script: draw 3 after a KO last turn — comeback draw.
  • Lumineon V (40/172) ×1 — Luminous Sign: search a Supporter on entry (Boss/Research).
  • Radiant Greninja (46/189) ×1 — Concealed Cards draw/cycle + a snipe attacker.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Arven ×4 — search a Tool + an Item (Forest Seal Stone/Bravery Charm + Electric Generator) — the consistency glue.
  • Boss's Orders ×3 / Professor's Research ×1 — gust the KO target + draw.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Nest Ball ×4 — search (thin the deck for Electric Generator; find Miraidon).
  • Prime Catcher ×1 (ACE SPEC) / Switch Cart ×2 — guaranteed gust + switch; pivot (reset Photon Blaster / retreat a sleeping/loaded Pokémon).
  • Forest Seal Stone ×2 — once-per-game "search any card" on a Pokémon — grab the missing piece.
  • Bravery Charm ×1 / Rescue Board ×1 / Town Store ×1 — +HP (Iron Hands survives a hit), free retreat, and a Stadium/Tool search.
  • 16 Lightning + 2 Double Turbo Energy — a huge Lightning count so Electric Generator always hits; Double Turbo powers Iron Hands/Ursaluna's Colorless costs.

Ideal early game

  • Going second (preferred): Miraidon ex → Tandem Unit for 2 Lightning Pokémon → Electric Generator onto the bench → attack immediately (Raikou Lightning Rondo, or set up Iron Hands). Squawkabilly first if your hand is rough.
  • Going first: Tandem Unit to flood the bench and charge with Electric Generator for a turn-2 Photon Blaster.

The core loop

Tandem Unit to build the board (chain it — find a Pokémon and another Miraidon, then Tandem Unit again) → Electric Generator to accelerate → pick the attacker for the job: Photon Blaster (220) for big KOs, Amp You Very Much for the extra-prize swing, Bloodmoon as a finisher. Boss's Orders/Prime Catcher pick the target; a second Miraidon resets the Photon Blaster drawback. You out-tempo and out-consistency the field.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Chain Tandem Unit — one Miraidon finds another + a Pokémon; do it again for a full board off almost nothing. This is the deck's consistency.
  • Thin before Electric Generator — Tandem Unit/Ultra Ball remove Pokémon/cards so Generator hits its 2 Energy off the top 5.
  • Map the prizes with Iron Hands — Amp You Very Much's extra prize sets up 3-3 or 3-2-1 plans; sequence KOs so it lands.
  • Reset Photon Blaster — attack with a second Miraidon (or another attacker) so you never skip a turn.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Iron Thorns ex — Initialization shuts off Tandem Unit (and Mew); use Tandem Unit before they get Iron Thorns Active, and lean on Boss + non-Ability attackers.
  • Fighting-Weakness hits — Miraidon/Iron Hands are Lightning (some take extra from Fighting/ specific tech).
  • Mimikyu (Safeguard) — immune to your ex attackers; Boss around it or use Zapdos/a non-ex.
  • Prized Miraidon — you run only 2; a prized/late Miraidon slows the engine (Ultra/Nest mitigate).

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Tandem Unit: search 2 Basic Lightning Pokémon → bench (every Miraidon, any time).
  • Photon Blaster: 220 (can't attack next turn — a 2nd Miraidon can).
  • Electric Generator: top 5 → attach 2 Basic Lightning to benched Lightning Pokémon.
  • Amp You Very Much (Iron Hands): 120 + take an extra Prize on a KO; Bloodmoon: 240 (270 w/ Defiance Band).

Sources: Pokemon.com ("Miraidon ex Deck Strategy"), Pokémon Center (Jesse Parker 2024 Worlds Masters Semifinalist — Miraidon ex), spellmana / Joseph Writer Anderson Miraidon guides, JustInBasil. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
regidrago_vstar_2024:
How to Pilot — Regidrago VSTAR (2024)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2024 (TWM-on, the Teal Mask Ogerpon era). This build mirrors Evan Pavelski's 2024 Senior World Championship-winning Regidrago VSTAR — the single most popular deck at Worlds 2024.

One-line identity

"All of Pokémon's best dragons in one deck." Regidrago VSTAR's Apex Dragon copies the attack of any Dragon Pokémon in your discard pile — so you discard a toolbox of dragons and pick the perfect attack each turn: Dragapult ex's Phantom Dive (200 + spread), Giratina VSTAR's Lost Impact (280 nuke), Hisuian Goodra VSTAR's shield, or Kyurem's Trifrost (multi-prize). Teal Mask Ogerpon ex + Energy Switch powers it up by turn 2.

The meta it lived in

2024 Standard was one of the widest metagames ever — 19+ viable decks (Charizard ex, Lugia, Raging Bolt, Iron Thorns, Gardevoir, Miraidon…). Regidrago was the anti-meta toolbox: it had favorable matchups into Charizard ex and Gardevoir ex, could pivot its attack to whatever the board demanded, and held four of the Top 8 at the Masters World Championship while winning the Senior Division. It was the most popular deck at Worlds 2024 and, later in the year, came to be regarded by many as the best deck in the format (BDIF) — it dominated LAIC with six of the Top 12. Its flexibility is unmatched — the deck rarely has a truly dead matchup.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Regidrago V (135/195) ×4 → Regidrago VSTAR (136/195) ×3 — the core. Apex Dragon (3 Energy): use the attack of any Dragon in your discard — the whole toolbox. Legacy Star (VSTAR Power, once per game): discard top 7, then take 2 cards from discard to hand — digs deep and recovers key pieces (Energy Switch, Prime Catcher). Save Legacy Star as your out to Iono/Unfair Stamp disruption — don't reflexively fire it turn 2.
  • Teal Mask Ogerpon ex (25/167) ×3 — the engine. Teal Dance (Ability): attach a Grass Energy from hand to Ogerpon + draw a card — draw and acceleration in one, every turn. Energy Switch then moves that Grass onto Regidrago to power Apex Dragon by turn 2. Its Myriad Leaf Shower also 1HKOs a Charizard ex (Grass) — a real attacker when needed.
  • Dragapult ex (130/167) ×2 — the primary discard target. Phantom Dive: 200 to the Active + 6 damage counters spread on the bench — 2-shots anything and snipes/softens their board (great vs. Charizard/Gardevoir setup Pokémon and Dusknoir).
  • Energy Switch ×4 — move the Teal-Dance Grass from Ogerpon onto Regidrago; the accelerant that enables the turn-2 attack.

The dragon toolbox (why each 1-of)

  • Giratina VSTAR (131/196) ×1 — Lost Impact via Apex Dragon: 280 (put 2 Energy in the Lost Zone) — the big nuke for high-HP targets a Phantom Dive can't reach.
  • Hisuian Goodra VSTAR (136/196) ×1 — Rolling Iron: a defensive attack that reduces incoming damage — survivability vs. decks that would OHKO Regidrago.
  • Kyurem (47/64) ×1 — Trifrost: 110 to each of 3 of the opponent's Pokémon (discard Kyurem's Energy — cheaper on Regidrago at 3) — a multi-prize/spread finisher, great vs. Stage-2 decks (snipe their Basics before they evolve).
  • Mew ex (151/165) ×1 — Restart draw (fill hand to a size) — consistency when your hand is small.
  • Squawkabilly ex (169/193) ×1 — Squawk and Seize (a turn-1 Professor's Research) — discard your hand, draw, and seed Dragons into the discard for Apex Dragon.
  • Fezandipiti ex (38/64) ×1 — Flip the Script: draw 3 after a Pokémon was KO'd last turn — comeback draw.
  • Hawlucha (127/172) ×1 — Flying Entry: chip the bench (helps Phantom Dive KO 70-HP Basics).

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Professor's Research ×4 / Iono ×3 / Boss's Orders ×2 — draw + discard (seed Dragons) + disruption + gust. Iono disrupts and refills; Boss's Orders pulls the KO target.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Nest Ball ×4 / Earthen Vessel ×4 — search + discard: Earthen Vessel (discard 1 → 2 Basic Energy) and Ultra Ball both put Dragons in the discard for Apex Dragon.
  • Superior Energy Retrieval ×1 / Super Rod ×1 — recover Energy/Pokémon (recur attackers + refuel).
  • Prime Catcher ×1 (ACE SPEC) / Switch ×1 — Prime Catcher gusts and switches (the premier disruption/KO tool); Switch pivots.
  • Canceling Cologne ×1 — shut off an opposing Ability for a turn (answers Mimikyu's Safeguard, etc.).
  • Temple of Sinnoh ×1 — Stadium: turns off Special Energy (a tech vs. Lugia's Special- Energy engine / V Guard Energy math).
  • 7 Grass + 3 Fire — Grass fuels Teal Dance/Apex Dragon; Fire enables specific dragon attacks (Radiant Charizard-style / off-costs).

Ideal early game

  • Turn 1: get Regidrago V down + a Teal Mask Ogerpon ex; Teal Dance a Grass; Squawkabilly's Squawk and Seize to dig and seed a Dragapult ex into the discard.
  • Turn 2: Energy Switch the Grass onto Regidrago, attach + evolve to Regidrago VSTAR, and Apex Dragon → Phantom Dive (200 + spread) — off to the races.

The core loop

Discard the right dragon → Apex Dragon copies its attack: Phantom Dive to 2-shot + spread, Lost Impact to nuke, Trifrost for multi-prize, Goodra to tank. Teal Dance + Energy Switch re-power Regidrago each turn; Boss's Orders/Prime Catcher pick the KO; Legacy Star (saved for late) recovers pieces and beats Iono. You take prizes on multiple fronts and adapt to any board.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Seed the discard first — Apex Dragon copies dragons in the discard; Squawkabilly/Ultra Ball/Earthen Vessel exist to put Dragapult (and the right tech) there. No discarded dragon = no attack.
  • Save Legacy Star — it's your once-per-game out to Iono/Unfair Stamp; don't waste it turn 2 unless you must.
  • Pick the attack for the board — Phantom Dive (spread/2HKO), Lost Impact (nuke), Trifrost (vs. evolution decks / multi-prize), Ogerpon Myriad Leaf Shower (vs. Charizard). This choice is the skill.
  • Turn-2 Apex Dragon is the tempo goal — Teal Dance + Energy Switch is the line.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Iono / Unfair Stamp disruption — hits your combo hand; you rely on Legacy Star + Ogerpon draw to recover (hence saving the VSTAR Power).
  • Raging Bolt ex — a high-HP attacker where Phantom Dive can't take multi-prize turns; a grind (Giratina/Kyurem help).
  • Prized key pieces — if Ogerpon or your dragons are prized, setup slows badly.
  • Fast aggro that races the turn-2 setup — you need the turn-2 attack; a slow open falls behind.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Apex Dragon: use any Dragon's attack from your discard (3 Energy).
  • Phantom Dive: 200 + 6 counters spread; Lost Impact: 280; Trifrost: 110 × 3 Pokémon.
  • Teal Dance: attach 1 Grass from hand to Ogerpon + draw 1 (Ability); Energy Switch moves it.
  • Legacy Star (VSTAR): discard top 7, take 2 from discard (once per game — save for Iono).

Sources: Bulbapedia & Pokémon Center (Evan Pavelski 1st 2024 Worlds Seniors — Regidrago VSTAR), TCGplayer & PokéBeach Regidrago guides, TheGamer, SNKRDUNK. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
dragapult_2025:
How to Pilot — Dragapult (2025)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2025 (JTG/BLK-on). The Dragapult ex / Dusknoir "Cursed Blast" build — the 2025 World Championship finalist archetype (2nd place, Justin Newdorf/Jose Cruz Galindo-Resendiz), and one of the most balanced, skill-rewarding decks in the format.

New to this deck? Read this first. Dragapult is a spread-damage prize-mapping deck. Its attack (Phantom Dive) does 200 to the Active and lets you drop 6 damage counters anywhere on their bench — so every turn you're doing two jobs at once: taking a KO up front and pre-damaging benched Pokémon for future KOs. Dusknoir then converts that spread into extra knockouts by dropping 13 damage counters on a single Pokémon on demand. The skill is sequencing: placing counters this turn so that next turn (or the same turn, with Dusknoir) you take multiple prizes at once. This guide walks through the counter math so you can plan those swing turns.

One-line identity

A Ghost/Dragon spread deck: Dragapult ex's Phantom Dive does 200 to the Active + 6 damage counters spread on the opponent's bench, softening their board while you take KOs; Dusknoir's Cursed Blast then places 13 damage counters on one Pokémon (a manual KO on almost anything) to convert the spread into multi-prize turns. Drakloak's Recon Directive draws you through the deck. Fast, flexible, and disruptive.

The meta it lived in

2025 Standard's top tier was Gardevoir ex, Dragapult ex, Gholdengo ex, Raging Bolt ex. Dragapult/Dusknoir was prized for being the most balanced deck in the format — "almost no completely unfavorable matchups," bringing most games close to 50-50. It excels at punishing wide boards of low-HP support Pokémon (Gardevoir's Ralts, Lost Zone Comfey) by wiping their engine with spread. Its trouble is single high-HP attackers (Gholdengo ex) that Phantom Dive's 200 can't 1HKO — that's exactly what Dusknoir is there to fix.

The engine — exactly how the damage works (read carefully)

  1. Dragapult ex — Phantom Dive (attack): "200 damage. Put 6 damage counters on your opponent's Benched Pokémon in any way you like." So each attack = 200 to the Active (a KO on most 2-prize ex Pokémon) plus 6 counters (60 damage) spread however you choose across their bench. You use those 6 counters to (a) snipe a low-HP Basic (e.g. a 70-HP Dreepy/Ralts before it evolves), or (b) pre-load damage on a benched threat so you can finish it next turn.
  2. Dusknoir — Cursed Blast (Ability): "Once during your turn (before your attack), you may put 13 damage counters on 1 of your opponent's Pokémon. Then, this Pokémon [Dusknoir] is Knocked Out." 13 counters = 130 damage placed instantly on any one Pokémon — enough to finish a big benched target or combine with Phantom Dive's spread for a huge multi-KO turn. Dusknoir KOs itself when you use it (you give up a prize), so time it when the payoff is worth it.
  3. Dusclops — Cursed Blast (Ability): the Stage-1 version places 5 damage counters (50) and also KOs itself — a smaller, earlier version you can use while building toward Dusknoir.
  4. Munkidori (95/167) ×1 — Adrena-Brain: move up to 3 counters (if it has Darkness) — here it's used to nudge damage onto exactly the right Pokémon to line up a KO, or to heal a counter off your own board.

The core skill: count HP. Phantom Dive's 6 counters + Dusknoir's 13 + attack damage let you pre-map knockouts. Example: Phantom Dive 200 KOs their Active and drops 6 counters onto a benched 130-HP ex (now at 60 damage); next turn Dusknoir's 13 counters (130) finishes it — two ex KOs across two turns, or one giant turn if you have both online.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Dreepy (128/167) ×4 → Drakloak (129/167) ×4 → Dragapult ex (130/167) ×3 — the core. Drakloak's Recon Directive (Ability): look at top 2, put 1 in hand and 1 on the bottom — smooth draw/dig every turn (a wide Drakloak bench = lots of digging). Dragapult ex's Phantom Dive (above) is your engine attack. Note: this build cuts Rare Candy to a single copy because Budew (below) can shut Candy off — you mostly evolve Dreepy → Drakloak → Dragapult the natural way, which the Drakloak draw supports.
  • Duskull (18/64) ×2 → Dusclops (19/64) ×2 → Dusknoir (20/64) ×1 — the finisher line. Cursed Blast (Dusknoir 13 / Dusclops 5, above). You build this over a few turns; against slower decks (Gardevoir/Dragapult mirror) you set it up patiently.
  • Budew (4/131) ×2 — Itchy Pollen (Ability): while Budew is your Active, neither player can play Item cards. A turn-1 Item lock that cripples setup decks (no Ultra Ball/ Rare Candy/Nest Ball for them) — a huge tempo tool. It's a Basic you lead with.

The tech attackers / support (why the 1-ofs)

  • Genesect (40/64) ×1 — a support/attacker tech (draw or a specific attack) — flexibility.
  • Hawlucha (118/198) ×1 — Flying Entry: chip the bench for free — combos with Phantom Dive's spread to reach KO counts on benched Pokémon.
  • Munkidori (95/167) ×1 — Adrena-Brain (above): fine-tune counter placement for exact KOs / heal a counter off your board (needs a Darkness Energy — hence Luminous/Neo Upper flexibility).
  • Fezandipiti ex (38/64) ×1 — Flip the Script: draw 3 after a KO last turn — comeback draw.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Iono ×4 / Professor's Research ×3 / Boss's Orders ×2 / Brock's Scouting ×1 / Hilda ×1 — draw + disruption + gust. Iono disrupts + refuels (great when ahead); Boss's Orders drags the target you want to Phantom-Dive/Cursed-Blast; Hilda is a newer draw Supporter that smooths setup.
  • Buddy-Buddy Poffin ×4 / Ultra Ball ×4 / Nest Ball (via Poffin role) / Rare Candy ×1 — Buddy-Buddy Poffin benches two ≤70-HP Basics (Dreepy/Duskull) — the key setup Item; Ultra Ball searches + discards.
  • Counter Catcher ×3 / Night Stretcher ×3 — free gust when behind; recover Pokémon/Energy (recur your lines).
  • Jamming Tower ×1 / Air Balloon ×2 — Jamming Tower (Stadium) turns off both players' Tools (disruption; note you then can't use your own Tools); Air Balloon gives free retreat to pivot Dragapult/Budew.
  • Psychic Energy ×2 + Fire Energy ×1 + Luminous Energy ×3 + Neo Upper Energy ×1 (ACE SPEC) — Dragapult's Phantom Dive costs Psychic + Fire; Luminous Energy provides any 1 type (fixes costs); Neo Upper Energy (ACE SPEC) provides 2 Energy of any type to an evolved Pokémon — a fast way to arm Dragapult.

Ideal early game (turn by turn)

  • Turn 1: ideally lead Budew Active (Itchy Pollen = Item lock) to strangle a setup deck, while you Buddy-Buddy Poffin out Dreepy/Duskull. Evolve toward Drakloak.
  • Turn 2: get a Dragapult ex online (natural evolution, or Rare Candy if Budew isn't needed) and Phantom Dive — 200 to the Active + place the 6 counters where they set up future KOs (snipe a low-HP Basic, or pre-damage a benched ex).

The core loop (every turn once set up)

  1. Phantom Dive: KO the Active (200) and place 6 counters to snipe/soften their bench.
  2. Recon Directive (each Drakloak) to dig for your next piece.
  3. When the payoff is there, Cursed Blast (Dusknoir 13 / Dusclops 5) to finish a benched threat or set up a multi-prize turn — remember Dusknoir KOs itself (give up 1 prize, take 2+).
  4. Munkidori to nudge counters for the exact KO; Boss's Orders/Counter Catcher to drag the target Active; Iono to disrupt when ahead.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Sequence the counters — the whole skill is placing Phantom Dive's 6 (and Dusknoir's 13) so you take multiple prizes over the next turn or two. Always ask: "where do these counters set up a KO?"
  • Budew turn 1 vs. setup decks — Item-locking Gardevoir/Gholdengo on turn 1 can win the game before it starts; lead Budew when you see a setup deck.
  • Watch the opponent's prize count with Cursed Blast — Dusknoir KOs itself, giving up a prize; if the opponent is close to winning, that extra prize can hand them the game. Don't Cursed Blast into your own loss.
  • Phantom Dive the right Stage 2 — in the mirror, the player who attacks the opponent's Stage 2 (not just the Active) first is often ahead; snipe their Drakloaks/setup.
  • Air Balloon to pivot — free-retreat Dragapult/Budew so you're never stuck Active.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Straight Dragapult (mirror) — the non-Dusknoir version is a documented unfavorable matchup (they're faster; you committed deck space to the Dusknoir line).
  • Single high-HP attackers (Gholdengo ex) — Phantom Dive's 200 can't 1HKO them; you must lean on Dusknoir/Munkidori math to win the prize race (slightly unfavorable).
  • Dragon Weakness / Lillie's Clefairy — Gardevoir's Lillie's Clefairy makes its Psychic attacks 1HKO your Dragapult (double damage to Dragons); a real threat.
  • N's Zoroark ex — slightly unfavorable (around 40-60), but more winnable than Gardevoir's.

Quick reference — the numbers
