  • Phantom Dive: 200 to the Active + 6 damage counters (60) spread on their bench.
  • Cursed Blast: Dusknoir 13 counters (130) / Dusclops 5 counters (50) on 1 Pokémon — then that Pokémon KOs itself.
  • Recon Directive (Drakloak): look at top 2, 1 to hand, 1 to bottom (draw/dig).
  • Itchy Pollen (Budew): both players can't play Items while Budew is Active.

Sources: Pokemon.com (2025 Worlds power rankings), spellmana (2025 Worlds Top 8 — Dragapult/ Dusknoir 2nd), TCGplayer Dragapult guide, TCG Protectors, JustInBasil. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
flutter_devo_gardevoir_2025:
How to Pilot — Flutter Devo Gardevoir (2025)

Format: Scarlet & Violet Standard 2025 (JTG/BLK-on, the "ex" era). This build mirrors the Munkidori/Scream Tail Gardevoir ex archetype — Riley McKay won the 2025 World Championship with a Gardevoir ex deck, beating Dragapult/Dusknoir in the final.

New to this deck? Read this first. Gardevoir ex is a damage-counter engine, not a straightforward beatdown. Its Ability (Psychic Embrace) accelerates Energy but hurts your own Pokémon on purpose — and the whole deck is built to turn that self-damage into a weapon. You'll be doing three things every turn: (1) attaching Energy from your discard with Psychic Embrace, (2) moving the resulting damage onto the opponent with Munkidori, and (3) attacking with single-prize Pokémon (Scream Tail/Drifloon) whose damage scales with damage counters. You trade your 1-prize attackers for their 2-prize ex Pokémon — a prize race that's very hard to lose once you understand the loop. Take it slow; this guide spells out every interaction.

One-line identity

A single-prize damage-counter deck: Gardevoir ex's Psychic Embrace attaches any number of basic Psychic Energy from your discard to your Psychic Pokémon (placing 2 damage counters on each Pokémon you fuel), Munkidori's Adrena-Brain moves that self-damage onto the opponent, and Scream Tail / Drifloon hit for more the more damage counters are in play. You out-trade the format's 2-prize ex decks with cheap single-prize attackers.

The meta it lived in

2025 Standard was a Stage-2 ex format — Gardevoir ex, Dragapult ex/Dusknoir, Gholdengo ex, Raging Bolt ex. Gardevoir ex was the deck to beat all season and cemented it by winning Worlds 2025. Its plan (trade single-prize attackers for 2-prize ex Pokémon, healing your own board with Munkidori) is almost impossible to out-race once set up. Its hardest matchups are Darkness attackers (N's Zoroark ex) that hit Gardevoir for Weakness, and fast decks that KO you before the engine comes online.

The engine — exactly how the loop works (read carefully)

  1. Gardevoir ex — Psychic Embrace (Ability): "As often as you like during your turn, you may attach a basic Psychic Energy from your discard pile to 1 of your Psychic Pokémon. If you do, put 2 damage counters on that Pokémon." So you can pour many Energy out of the discard in one turn — but every Energy you attach this way puts 2 damage counters (20 HP) on the Pokémon you attached it to. That self-damage is the "cost."
  2. Munkidori — Adrena-Brain (Ability): "Once during your turn, if this Munkidori has any Darkness Energy attached, you may move up to 3 damage counters from 1 of your Pokémon to 1 of your opponent's Pokémon." This is how you turn the self-damage into offense — move the counters Psychic Embrace put on your board onto their Pokémon. Each Munkidori with a Darkness Energy can do this once per turn, so 2–3 Munkidori = 6–9 counters moved per turn. Munkidori needs a Darkness Energy attached to work — that's why the deck runs Darkness Energy alongside Psychic.
  3. Scream Tail / attackers: these deal more damage the more damage counters exist (see below), so the counters you generate feed your attacks and your movement.

The beautiful part: Psychic Embrace's "downside" (self-damage) is the deck's fuel. Munkidori moves it onto the opponent, and if a counter would be wasted, Scream Tail's damage scales off it anyway.

Engine cards (why they're here)

  • Ralts (84/198) ×3 → Kirlia (85/198) ×2 → Gardevoir ex (86/198) ×2 — the core. Psychic Embrace (above) is the Energy engine; Miracle Force (its attack) does solid damage and can be boosted by Munkidori counters. You run Rare Candy to jump Ralts → Gardevoir ex, skipping Kirlia, for a turn-2 setup. 190 HP.
  • Munkidori (95/167) ×4 — Adrena-Brain (above). Four copies because moving damage is the whole plan — you want 2+ online with Darkness attached. Also heals your own board (moving counters off your Pokémon keeps them alive — this is how you "undo" a Phantom Dive). Its Mind Bend attack can confuse, too.
  • Scream Tail (86/182) ×1 — the main attacker. Slap Whip: does more damage per damage counter on the opponent's Pokémon (with Bravery Charm + Munkidori setup it can exceed 330 — enough to KO anything). A single-prize attacker: they take only 1 prize for KO'ing it, but it hits like a 2-prize threat.
  • Psychic Energy ×7 + Darkness Energy ×4 — Psychic fuels Psychic Embrace/attacks; the 4 Darkness are exclusively to turn on Munkidori's Adrena-Brain (Munkidori needs Darkness attached). Rule to remember: you can't Psychic Embrace onto Munkidori (it's a Darkness Pokémon, not Psychic) — attach its Darkness manually or via other means.

The tech attackers (why the 1-ofs)

  • Flutter Mane (78/162) ×1 — a fast Basic that can attack turn 1 and slow the opponent down early (buys time to set up your Stage-2 line). Also a cheap Psychic attacker you can Psychic Embrace onto.
  • Lillie's Clefairy ex (56/159) ×1 — Fairy Zone (Ability): your Psychic Pokémon's attacks do double damage to Dragon Pokémon. This is the Dragapult answer — it lets Scream Tail/Miracle Force/Mew-copied attacks 1HKO the opposing Dragapult ex (Dragons usually have no Weakness, so this is huge).
  • Mew ex (151/165) ×1 — Restart (Ability): draw until you have 3 cards — your draw refuel now that Kirlia's draw Ability is gone. Genome Hacking (attack): copy an attack of the opponent's Active — most importantly copy Phantom Dive and, with Lillie's Clefairy in play, 1HKO an opposing Dragapult ex and spread counters. (Note: you can't Psychic Embrace onto Mew ex either — it's Psychic, so actually you can. Mew ex is Psychic-type.)
  • Fezandipiti ex (38/64) ×1 — Flip the Script (Ability): once, after a Pokémon was KO'd last turn, draw 3 — comeback draw. A single-prize-focused deck triggers this often.

Support / tech (why the counts)

  • Iono ×4 / Professor's Research ×4 / Arven ×2 — draw + disruption + search. Iono (shuffle both hands, draw to prize count) disrupts and refuels — great when you're ahead (they draw fewer). Arven searches a Tool (Bravery Charm) + an Item.
  • Ultra Ball ×4 / Nest Ball ×2 / Earthen Vessel ×3 / Rare Candy ×2 — search + Energy discard. Earthen Vessel (discard 1 → 2 Basic Energy) both finds Energy and seeds the discard so Psychic Embrace has fuel; Rare Candy jumps to Gardevoir ex.
  • Counter Catcher ×2 / Secret Box ×1 (ACE SPEC) — Counter Catcher (free gust when behind on prizes) drags up a support Pokémon to KO; Secret Box searches 4 cards (Pokémon
  • Item + Tool + Supporter) for an explosive turn.
  • Night Stretcher ×2 / Super Rod ×1 — recover Pokémon/Energy (recur attackers + refill Psychic for Embrace).
  • Technical Machine: Evolution ×2 — a Tool-TM that lets a Basic evolve the turn it's played / an extra evolution — speeds your Stage-2 setup (helps get Gardevoir/attackers online).
  • Technical Machine: Devolution ×1 — a TM attack that devolves an opponent's Pokémon — combos with your spread damage to KO an evolved Pokémon (devolve a damaged Stage 2 → the Basic can't survive), and disrupts Rare-Candy decks.
  • Bravery Charm ×1 — Tool: +50 HP. On Scream Tail/Drifloon it lets them survive a hit and hold more Energy/counters (bigger attacks); also protects Fezandipiti/Munkidori from snipe.
  • Artazon ×2 — Stadium: search a non-Rule-Box Basic (more Ralts/Munkidori/Scream Tail) — consistency, and a counter to opposing Stadiums.

Ideal early game (turn by turn)

  • Turn 1: bench Ralts (+ Munkidori, Flutter Mane). Play Artazon to grab more Basics. Use Earthen Vessel/Ultra Ball to get Psychic Energy into the discard (Psychic Embrace pulls from there). If you have Flutter Mane charged, attack to slow them.
  • Turn 2: Rare Candy → Gardevoir ex (skip Kirlia). Attach a Darkness Energy to a Munkidori (turns on Adrena-Brain). Psychic Embrace several Psychic onto an attacker (Scream Tail), accepting the 2-counters-each self-damage. Move that damage onto their board with Munkidori. Attack.

The core loop (every turn once set up)

  1. Psychic Embrace to load your attacker with Energy from the discard (each attach = 2 counters on that Pokémon).
  2. Adrena-Brain with each Darkness-carrying Munkidori: move up to 3 counters per Munkidori off your board and onto theirs — this both heals your attacker and pre-damages their Pokémon.
  3. Attack with Scream Tail (scales with the counters you just placed) — often KO'ing a 2-prize ex while giving up only 1 prize back.
  4. Use Lillie's Clefairy + Mew ex's Genome Hacking (copy Phantom Dive) to answer Dragapult; Counter Catcher/Boss to pick the target; Iono to disrupt when ahead.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Turn-2 Gardevoir ex (via Rare Candy) is the goal — Psychic Embrace needs to start ASAP, and you need Psychic in the discard to fuel it (seed it with Earthen Vessel/Ultra Ball).
  • Always have a Darkness Energy on a Munkidori — Adrena-Brain is dead without it; this is the #1 beginner mistake. Match your Darkness count to your Munkidori.
  • Use Adrena-Brain to heal as much as to damage — moving counters off your attacker keeps it alive through their attack; you can undo an entire Phantom Dive across a few Munkidori.
  • Stay single-prize — the deck wins the prize trade by giving up 1 for their 2. Don't over- expose Gardevoir ex/Mew ex/Fezandipiti (2-prize liabilities) unless necessary.
  • Lillie's Clefairy before you fight Dragapult — its Fairy Zone (double damage to Dragons) is how you 1HKO their Dragapult ex; get it down early in that matchup.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • N's Zoroark ex / Darkness attackers — Gardevoir ex is Metal-Weak and Darkness decks (N's Zoroark, N's Reshiram) KO it and don't leave counters for you to exploit — the toughest matchup.
  • Fast KO decks before setup — if they take the first two prizes before Gardevoir ex is online, the single-prize trade can fall behind (Flutter Mane/Budew-style early disruption helps).
  • Charizard ex — initiates the prize trade using your Dark Weakness; a careful matchup.
  • Being unable to attach Darkness (Itchy Pollen / Item lock) — if you can't get Darkness on Munkidori, the engine stalls; plan around it.

Quick reference — the numbers

  • Psychic Embrace: attach any number of basic Psychic from discard to Psychic Pokémon; 2 damage counters per attach on that Pokémon.
  • Adrena-Brain (Munkidori, needs Darkness attached): move up to 3 damage counters from your Pokémon to the opponent's, once per Munkidori per turn.
  • Scream Tail: damage scales with damage counters (330+ possible with Bravery Charm + Munkidori setup) — single prize.
  • Rare Candy: Ralts → Gardevoir ex; Lillie's Clefairy: your Psychic attacks do double to Dragons; Mew ex Genome Hacking: copy the opponent's attack (e.g. Phantom Dive).

Sources: Pokemon.com ("Rebuilding a Gardevoir ex Deck"), PokéBeach ("Gardevoir ex — My Top Choice", "Gardevoir ex in Journey Together"), spellmana (2025 Worlds Top 8 — Riley McKay 1st), TCGplayer, JustInBasil. Verified vs. the collection workbook (60 cards).
joltdengo_2025:
Joltdengo (2025)

Gholdengo ex / Joltik Box hybrid — "An Unlikely Alliance." 2025 Standard (JTG/BLK-on, the "ex" era). Won the 2025 World Championship Seniors division.

New to this deck? Read this first. Joltdengo is two decks stapled together. One half is a Joltik Box — a swarm of cheap single-attack Basics (Iron Hands ex, Miraidon ex, Pikachu ex) that you power up instantly with Joltik's energy-search attack and use to take fast, opportunistic prizes early. The other half is Gholdengo, a late-game hammer whose attack, Make It Rain, hits for 50 damage per Basic Energy you throw away from your hand — so the whole deck is built to hoard Basic Energy in hand, then dump it for a 200–300 nuke. Each turn you're doing one of two things: (1) setting up and swinging with a Box attacker while stockpiling Energy, or (2) once you have Gholdengo ex out and a fat hand of Energy, unloading Make It Rain to close games. The magic trick that ties it together: Energy you spend on Iron Hands early comes back to your hand (via Superior Energy Retrieval) and becomes Make It Rain damage later. Same Energy, two jobs.

One-line identity

A hybrid toolbox that has one of the format's best early games (Joltik Box) welded to one of its best late games (Gholdengo), letting it apply pressure from turn 2 and still have a lethal top-end.

The meta it lived in

2025 Standard was the mature "ex" format. The top tier was Gardevoir ex (the eventual Worlds Masters winner, Riley McKay), Dragapult ex / Dusknoir, Gholdengo ex, and Raging Bolt ex. Straight Gholdengo was the most-played Day 1 deck at Worlds (~22%, climbing to ~30% Day 2) but none of its pilots cracked Top 4 in Masters — the pure list was consistent but linear. The Joltik Box hybrid, by contrast, took the Seniors division title (Gabriel Semedo, per PokéBeach): grafting the Box's explosive early game onto Gholdengo's late game fixed the pure deck's biggest weakness — falling behind early.

The engine — exactly how Make It Rain works (read carefully)

Everything in this deck orbits Gholdengo ex and the goal of keeping your hand stuffed with Basic Energy.

Gholdengo ex — Coin Bonus (Ability): Once during your turn you draw a card. If Gholdengo ex is in the Active Spot, draw 2 instead. With 3 copies of Gholdengo ex, at least one Coin Bonus fires most turns, keeping your hand full so you always have Energy and options.

Gholdengo ex — Make It Rain (attack): Discard any number of Basic Energy cards from your hand. This attack does 50 damage for each card you discarded. - 4 Energy discarded = 200 damage (KOs most ex Basics). - 5 Energy = 250; 6 = 300 (KOs the fattest attackers). - Crucially, the Energy is discarded from your hand, not from Gholdengo — Gholdengo itself never needs Energy attached to attack. That's why the deck is a Basic-Energy stockpiling machine.

The worked example: You have 5 Basic Energy in hand and Gholdengo ex active. You attack: discard all 5 → 250 damage, one-shotting almost anything. Next turn, Superior Energy Retrieval pulls up to 4 of those Energy back from the discard, and Coin Bonus + Gimmighoul refill the rest — so you can do it again.

The stockpiling + refuel loop

  • Gimmighoul (SSP 97) — Minor Errand-Running (attack): Search your deck for 2 Basic Energy and put them in your hand. This both thins your deck and loads the hand for Make It Rain. (This is the specific SSP "Minor Errand-Running" print — 4 copies.)
  • Superior Energy Retrieval (Item): Discard 2 other cards from your hand, then return up to 4 Basic Energy from your discard pile to your hand. This is the refuel button — after you dump Energy for Make It Rain (or spend it on Iron Hands), this brings it right back. 4 copies because the whole engine depends on recycling.
  • Earthen Vessel: discard 1 card, search 2 Basic Energy → hand. More hand-loading. (2 copies.)

Consistency — the Genesect line replaces Ultra Ball

Genesect ex — Metallic Signal (Ability): When you play Genesect ex from hand to your Bench, search your deck for up to 2 Metal Evolution Pokémon and put them in your hand. Gholdengo ex is a Metal Stage-1, so Metallic Signal pulls Gholdengo ex straight out of the deck. This is why the list runs no Ultra Ball — Genesect is your Gholdengo-finder, and it does it without discarding cards. (2 copies.)

The Box attackers (the toolbox)

  • Joltik (50/142) — Jolting Charge (attack): Search your deck for up to 2 Basic Grass Energy and up to 2 Basic Lightning Energy and attach them to your Pokémon in any way you like. This is the acceleration that powers up the toolbox attackers in a single turn. (2 copies; note the deck runs 4 Grass + 4 Lightning + 3 Metal Energy precisely so Joltik always has targets.)
  • Iron Hands ex — Amp You Very Much (attack): 120 damage, and if this attack Knocks Out a Pokémon, take 1 more Prize card (so a KO gives you 2 prizes, or 3 on a 2-prize ex). The prize-swinger — Iron Hands is how you steal tempo early.
  • Miraidon ex — Tandem Unit (Ability): Once during your turn (when you play it from hand), search for 2 Basic Lightning Pokémon and put them on your Bench — e.g. fetch Joltik plus another attacker to build your board instantly.
  • Pikachu ex (57/191): enables the Area Zero Underdepths–style bench expansion (more attackers/support on board).
  • Latias ex — Skyliner (Ability): your Basic Pokémon retreat for free — the pivot that lets you move a spent attacker out and a fresh one in without paying Energy.
  • Fezandipiti ex — Flip the Script (Ability): once, after one of your Pokémon was Knocked Out last turn, draw 3 cards. Bounce-back draw after you take a hit.

Support & tech (why each count)

  • 4 Arven — your best Item-tutor turn after turn (grabs Nest Ball + a Tool, or the Item you need).
  • 3 Boss's Orders — gust the opponent's benched liability into the Active for a prize. High count because targeting the right Pokémon wins prize races.
  • 2 Brock's Scouting — draw/search Supporter to smooth setup.
  • 1 Team Rocket's Petrel — disruptive search Supporter (situational hand attack / tutor).
  • 1 Professor Turo's Scenario — return one of your damaged or liability Pokémon (a low Genesect ex or Fezandipiti ex) to hand without giving up a prize. Great for un-benching a fragile ex before it's KO'd.
  • 2 Levincia (Stadium) — Lightning support Stadium; also contests the opponent's Stadium.
  • 4 Nest Ball — free Basic search; the deck is almost entirely Basics, so this is prime setup.
  • 3 Buddy-Buddy Poffin — bench two low-HP Basics (Joltik, Gimmighoul) at once. Explosive turn-1 setup.
  • 2 Pokégear 3.0 — dig for the Supporter you need.
  • 1 Prime Catcher (ACE SPEC) — the free-gust Item; your one-of ACE SPEC, saved for a lethal gust.
  • 2 Air Balloon — free retreat so you can keep Gholdengo ex active for the 2-card Coin Bonus and pivot freely.
  • 1 Bravery Charm — +50 HP Tool to push a key attacker out of a KO range.

Ideal early game

Turn 1: Get a Basic (ideally Joltik and/or Gimmighoul) down via Nest Ball / Buddy-Buddy Poffin. If you can, play Gimmighoul → Minor Errand-Running to bank 2 Basic Energy, or Joltik → Jolting Charge to load Energy onto an attacker. Bench Genesect ex if you have it (Metallic Signal → grab Gholdengo ex). Aim to have a threat online by turn 2.

Turn 2: This is the fork. - Aggro line: Joltik → attach Grass/Lightning to Iron Hands ex → Amp You Very Much for 120 and a prize swing. Keep stockpiling Basic Energy in hand. - Late-game setup line: get Gholdengo ex active, fire Coin Bonus (draw 2), keep hoarding Energy, and start swinging Make It Rain once you have 4+ Energy in hand.

The core loop

  1. Coin Bonus (draw, 2 if Gholdengo active) + Gimmighoul / Earthen Vessel to keep Basic Energy flowing into hand.
  2. Take early prizes with Iron Hands ex (Joltik-fueled) while the hand fills.
  3. When you have 4–6 Basic Energy in hand and Gholdengo ex active, Make It Rain for 200–300 to close.
  4. Superior Energy Retrieval brings the discarded Energy back → repeat.
  5. Boss's Orders / Prime Catcher to gust the exact KO that finishes the prize race.

The signature combo (the thing that makes the hybrid work)

Joltik searches Energy → attach to Iron Hands ex → Amp You Very Much takes an early KO (and a bonus prize). When Iron Hands is later Knocked Out, Superior Energy Retrieval pulls those same Energy cards back to your hand → they now become Make It Rain damage on Gholdengo. The Energy does double duty: first it powers Iron Hands, then it is Gholdengo's damage. That recycling is why the deck has a strong early and late game instead of running out of gas.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Keep Basic Energy in your hand, not on your board. Gholdengo's damage lives in your hand. Resist over-attaching.
  • Keep Gholdengo ex active when you can (with Air Balloon to pivot) for the 2-card Coin Bonus draw.
  • Genesect ex, not Ultra Ball, finds Gholdengo. Plan your Genesect drops around when you need the Stage-1.
  • Use Iron Hands' bonus prize to break parity — a well-timed Amp You Very Much can put you a prize ahead in a race you'd otherwise lose.
  • Turo's Scenario your own fragile ex (a low Genesect/Fezandipiti) before the opponent can gust-and-KO it for easy prizes.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Fire attackers 1HKO Gholdengo ex cheaply — Gholdengo is Metal and Fire-Weak, so a Fire deck trades up on your main late-game piece.
  • Linearity when behind on prizes — the deck wants to be even or ahead; if it drops the first two prizes it can struggle to stabilize.
  • Toedscruel (Trickened Garden / Energy-lock effects) can block Superior Energy Retrieval and choke the refuel loop.
  • Gardevoir ex late — hard to reliably KO a well-set Gardevoir line, and Gard's Munkidori-fueled damage can pick off your low-HP Joltik/Gimmighoul before they contribute.

Quick reference numbers

  • Make It Rain: 50 × (Basic Energy discarded from hand). 4 = 200, 5 = 250, 6 = 300.
  • Coin Bonus: draw 1, or 2 if Gholdengo ex is Active.
  • Iron Hands ex — Amp You Very Much: 120, +1 Prize on KO.
  • Bellowing targets: aim for 4+ Energy in hand before committing to a Make It Rain turn.
  • Energy base: 4 Grass / 4 Lightning / 3 Metal (Joltik wants the Grass + Lightning; Gholdengo eats any Basic).
  • Genesect ex Metallic Signal: grabs up to 2 Metal Evolution Pokémon (Gholdengo ex counts).

Sources

Deck identity and result: PokéBeach ("An Unlikely Alliance," 2025 Worlds Seniors, Gabriel Semedo). Card mechanics per the JTG/BLK-on Standard-legal printings noted above (Gholdengo ex SSP, Gimmighoul SSP 97, Genesect ex, Joltik 50/142, Iron Hands ex, Miraidon ex, Superior Energy Retrieval). Meta context from 2025 World Championship (Anaheim) Day 1/Day 2 usage reporting.

Research-based, not playtested by Claude. Matchup and priority calls reflect documented competitive consensus rather than first-hand testing.
raging_bolt_2025:
Raging Bolt (2025)

Raging Bolt ex / Teal Mask Ogerpon ex — Yuya Okita's Tera-Ancient toolbox. 2025 Standard (JTG/BLK-on, the "ex" era).

New to this deck? Read this first. Raging Bolt is a one-shot nuke deck. Its whole plan is to pile Grass Energy onto Raging Bolt ex, then fire Bellowing Thunder, which discards all of that Energy at once to hit for 70 damage per Energy — three Energy is 210, four is 280, five is 350. Because the attack empties the tank every time, the deck is built to re-load Energy every single turn so you can nuke again next turn. The refueling machine is Teal Mask Ogerpon ex, whose Ability attaches a Grass Energy from hand and draws you a card for free each turn; you then shuttle that Grass onto Raging Bolt with Energy Switch and Professor Sada's Vitality (which also heals). So each turn you do the same thing: draw and attach with Ogerpon, move Energy onto Raging Bolt, and swing for a huge number. Noctowl is the glue that fetches the Trainers you need to keep the loop running.

One-line identity

A Tera-Ancient turbo deck that reloads Grass Energy every turn to fire 70-per-Energy one-shots with Raging Bolt ex, backstopped by Noctowl's Trainer-searching consistency.

The meta it lived in

2025 Standard's top tier was Gardevoir ex (Worlds Masters champion), Dragapult ex / Dusknoir, Gholdengo ex, and Raging Bolt ex. Raging Bolt was the format's premier hit-hard-and-fast option: it doesn't grind, it removes threats with 280–350 one-shots before they set up. Yuya Okita's Raging Bolt / Teal Mask Ogerpon build (per the Coded Yellow Worlds deck-product writeup) is the reference list — it leans on the Tera engine (Area Zero Underdepths bench expansion, Ogerpon acceleration) and Noctowl for consistency.

The engine — exactly how Bellowing Thunder works (read carefully)

Raging Bolt ex — Bellowing Thunder (attack): Discard all Energy from this Pokémon. This attack does 70 damage for each Energy card you discarded. - 3 Energy = 210, 4 = 280, 5 = 350. It scales with how much you've loaded. - The catch: it discards everything from Raging Bolt, so after every Bellowing Thunder your attacker is empty. The entire deck exists to refill it before your next turn.

Raging Bolt ex — Thunderburst Storm (attack): does damage for each Energy attached but does NOT discard the Energy. This is the lower-commitment option: when you want to chip a target while keeping your board loaded, you use Thunderburst Storm; when you want the full nuke, you use Bellowing Thunder and dump it all. The two attacks work together — chip and hold, or empty the tank.

The worked example: You've loaded 4 Grass onto Raging Bolt ex over two turns (Ogerpon + Sada + Energy Switch). You fire Bellowing Thunder: discard all 4 → 280 damage, one-shotting a Gardevoir ex or Dragapult ex. Raging Bolt is now empty — but Professor Sada's Vitality next turn attaches 2 back, Ogerpon + Energy Switch add more, and Night Stretcher recovers any discarded Energy, so you're reloaded to nuke again.

The reload machine

  • Teal Mask Ogerpon ex — Teal Dance (Ability): Once during your turn, attach a Grass Energy from your hand to Ogerpon, then draw a card. Acceleration and card draw in a single free Ability — this is the heartbeat of the deck. You use it every turn.
  • Energy Switch (Item): move a Basic Energy from one of your Pokémon to another — specifically, shuttle the Grass Ogerpon just attached onto Raging Bolt ex.
  • Professor Sada's Vitality (Supporter): attach 2 Basic Energy from hand to your Ancient Pokémon and heal 40 damage from them. Raging Bolt ex and Koraidon are Ancient, so this is your primary acceleration and it keeps the attacker out of KO range. (4 copies — it's the most important Supporter.)
  • Crispin (Supporter): search your deck for 2 Basic Energy of different types, attach 1 to a Pokémon and put the other in hand. Fixes/accelerates when Sada isn't in hand.
  • Night Stretcher (3 copies): recover a Pokémon or a Basic Energy from discard → hand. Refuels the Energy you keep discarding with Bellowing Thunder.

Consistency — the Noctowl engine

Noctowl — Jewel Seeker (Ability): Once during your turn, when you play this Pokémon from your hand to evolve 1 of your Pokémon, if you have any Tera Pokémon in play, you may search your deck for up to 2 Trainer cards, reveal them, and put them into your hand. - The trigger has two conditions: (1) you're evolving Hoothoot → Noctowl this turn, and (2) you have a Tera Pokémon in play. Ogerpon ex and Raging Bolt ex are Tera, so the condition is almost always met once you're set up. - Reward: search 2 Trainers — usually Professor Sada's Vitality, Energy Switch, or ball/recovery — the exact pieces that keep the reload loop flowing. Hoothoot (114/142) is the Basic you start; you run 3 Hoothoot / 3 Noctowl to fire this repeatedly. - Gotcha: Jewel Seeker only fires on the evolution turn. Playing Noctowl from hand onto an already-in-play Hoothoot is what triggers it — you can't re-use an already-evolved Noctowl for it.

Setup & tech (why each count)

  • Fan Rotom — Fan Call (attack, typically Turn 1): benches multiple Basic Colorless/support Pokémon at once, building your board fast. Fan Rotom + Squawkabilly ex is the ideal turn-1 setup. (2 copies.)
  • Squawkabilly ex — Squawk and Seize (Ability): turn-1 discard-your-hand-and-draw-5 to dig for your setup pieces; also a Colorless attacker in a pinch.
  • Koraidon — Ancient support/attacker; benefits from Sada's Vitality acceleration.
  • Raging Bolt (111/142, non-ex) — a single-prize attacker option; useful when you want to attack without giving up 2 prizes on a KO.
  • Ditto (132/165) — flexible evolution filler: via its rule it can stand in as an evolving Basic to smooth lines.
  • Latias ex — Skyliner (Ability): your Basic Pokémon retreat for free — the pivot.
  • Fezandipiti ex — Flip the Script (Ability): after one of your Pokémon was KO'd last turn, draw 3 — bounce-back draw.
  • Area Zero Underdepths (Stadium): lets Tera Pokémon expand your Bench to 8, giving room for extra Ogerpon/attackers/support. A core Tera-deck engine. (2 copies.)
  • Jamming Tower (Stadium): turns off both players' Pokémon Tools — disruption vs. Tool-reliant decks; also contests Underdepths.
  • 4 Nest Ball / 4 Ultra Ball / 3 Earthen Vessel — search density: Nest Ball for Basics, Ultra Ball for anything, Earthen Vessel for Basic Energy.
  • 2 Boss's Orders — gust the KO that finishes the prize race.
  • 1 Iono — hand disruption + refresh when you're ahead on prizes.
  • 1 Professor Turo's Scenario — pick up a damaged/liability Pokémon of yours without giving up a prize.
  • 1 Energy Switch — the Ogerpon-to-Raging-Bolt shuttle.
  • 1 Prime Catcher (ACE SPEC) — free gust; saved for the lethal turn.

Ideal early game

Turn 1: You want the board wide and the hand deep. Ideal: Squawkabilly ex → Squawk and Seize (dump a clunky hand, draw fresh) and/or Fan Rotom → Fan Call to bench multiple support Basics (Hoothoot, Ogerpon, a second attacker). Get Teal Mask Ogerpon ex down so Teal Dance can start every turn. Drop Area Zero Underdepths if you need the bench space.

Turn 2: Begin the loop. Teal Dance (attach Grass to Ogerpon + draw). Evolve Hoothoot → Noctowl with a Tera Pokémon in play → Jewel Seeker for 2 Trainers (grab Sada / Energy Switch). Sada's Vitality loads 2 Energy onto Raging Bolt ex; Energy Switch moves Ogerpon's Grass over. Now you likely have 3 on Raging Bolt → Bellowing Thunder for 210, and you're set to escalate.

The core loop

  1. Teal Dance (Ogerpon: attach Grass + draw) — every turn.
  2. Noctowl (Jewel Seeker) on evolution turns → fetch the 2 Trainers that keep it flowing.
  3. Sada's Vitality / Energy Switch / Crispin move and add Energy onto Raging Bolt ex (Sada also heals 40).
  4. Bellowing Thunder — discard it all for 70 × N — one-shot the threat.
  5. Night Stretcher / Sada reload the discarded Energy for next turn → repeat.

The core combo (express clearly)

Fan Rotom / Squawkabilly turn-1 board → Teal Dance each turn (Grass on Ogerpon + draw) → Energy Switch / Sada's Vitality move+add that Energy onto Raging Bolt ex → Bellowing Thunder discards it all for a 210–350 one-shot on almost anything → Night Stretcher / Sada reload for the next nuke. Noctowl continuously fetches the Trainers (Sada, Energy Switch, recovery) that keep the machine fed. The deck's power is that it can do this every turn from turn 2, out-tempoing setup decks.

Priorities / rules of thumb

  • Fire Teal Dance every turn — free Energy + a card is the engine; never skip it.
  • Only evolve into Noctowl when a Tera Pokémon is in play — otherwise Jewel Seeker whiffs. Keep an Ogerpon or Raging Bolt ex out before you evolve.
  • Choose the right attack: Bellowing Thunder to one-shot and reset; Thunderburst Storm to chip while keeping Energy when a full dump isn't lethal and you'd rather stay loaded.
  • Use the non-ex Raging Bolt (111/142) to deny prizes — against decks racing you, a single-prize attacker changes the prize math.
  • Sada's heal 40 matters — it can drag Raging Bolt back out of an opponent's KO range, buying you the turn you need to nuke.
  • Bench math with Area Zero Underdepths — expanding to 8 lets you keep spare Ogerpon/attackers so a gust-KO doesn't stall your loop.

Weaknesses / bad matchups

  • Stall / control — the deck must keep discarding and reloading Energy; if it's denied Energy or forced to whiff a reload, the loop stalls. Control decks that blunt your one-shot cadence are its worst matchup.
  • Gardevoir ex — noted as a tough matchup; Gard grinds and its Munkidori-fueled spread can pick off your Hoothoot before it becomes Noctowl, choking your consistency engine.
  • Spread damage (Dragapult ex / Munkidori) targets your low-HP setup Basics (Hoothoot, Ogerpon) before they contribute.
  • Energy denial hurts disproportionately because every Bellowing Thunder empties the tank — anything that slows the refill slows your whole plan.
  • Weakness exposure — mind type Weakness on your attackers when choosing what to send Active.

Quick reference numbers

  • Bellowing Thunder: 70 × (Energy discarded from Raging Bolt ex). 3 = 210, 4 = 280, 5 = 350.
  • Thunderburst Storm: damage per Energy attached, no discard (keep your board loaded).
  • Teal Dance: attach 1 Grass from hand to Ogerpon + draw 1, every turn.
  • Sada's Vitality: +2 Energy to an Ancient Pokémon and heal 40 (Raging Bolt ex & Koraidon are Ancient).
  • Noctowl — Jewel Seeker: on evolve, with a Tera in play, search 2 Trainers.
  • Area Zero Underdepths: Tera Pokémon bench expands to 8.
  • Energy base: 6 Grass / 3 Fighting / 3 Lightning.

Sources

Deck identity and list: Coded Yellow / Worlds deck-product writeup of Yuya Okita's Raging Bolt ex / Teal Mask Ogerpon ex build. Card mechanics per the JTG/BLK-on Standard-legal printings noted above (Raging Bolt ex 123/162, Teal Mask Ogerpon ex, Noctowl 115/142, Fan Rotom, Professor Sada's Vitality, Crispin, Area Zero Underdepths). Meta context from 2025 World Championship (Anaheim) reporting.

Research-based, not playtested by Claude. Matchup and priority calls reflect documented competitive consensus rather than first-hand testing.
